system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological scoping assessment  
Substance: 6-PPD-quinone (6PPQ, N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-4,5-dione)  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5  
Major source of human exposure: oxidation product of the tire additive 6-PPD; detected in roadway runoff, urban surface waters, house dust and airborne tire-wear particles.

------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Relevance of existing data
------------------------------------------------------------------
• Experimental data – aquatic species: extremely high acute toxicity to salmonids (Nanogram L-1 LC50) (Tian et al., 2021; Kolodziej et al., 2022).  
• Experimental data – mammalian / human cells: cytotoxicity, oxidative stress and apoptosis reported in HepG2, A549, BEAS-2B, HUVEC and RAW264.7 cells at low µM concentrations (Zhao et al., 2023; Sitaras et al., 2024; Deng et al., 2023).  
• No guideline in-vivo mammalian studies located (ECHA, EPA, PubMed searches, May 2024).  
• QSAR/Read-across: electrophilic 1,4-benzoquinones and para-phenylenediamine-quinones are known redox cyclers, glutathione depletors, skin sensitisers and nephro-/hepatotoxins.  
• Physical-chemical parameters (EPA CompTox DSSTox, 2024):  
  – MW 299.4 g/mol, logKow 3.3, water solubility 0.3–1 mg L-1, Vp 2.4 × 10-7 Pa.  

------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Toxicokinetic considerations (systemic availability)
------------------------------------------------------------------
• Oral: logKow 3–4 and moderate solubility indicate appreciable gastrointestinal uptake; in-silico (PKCSM, 2024) predicts 45–65 % human intestinal absorption.  
• Inhalation: compound is semi-volatile and adheres to PM2.5/PM10 tire wear particles; QSAR predicts >90 % deposition + rapid alveolar diffusion.  
• Dermal: predicted Kp ≈ 2 × 10-4 cm h-1 → low-to-moderate dermal uptake but possible due to lipophilicity.  
• Distribution: likely binds to plasma proteins (quinone-thiol adducts); no bioaccumulation expected (moderate logKow, metabolic reactivity).  
• Metabolism / Excretion: rapid GSH conjugation, reduction to hydroquinone, phase-II conjugation; urinary/biliary elimination of conjugates expected; GSH depletion is rate-limiting.  

Systemic-availability rating  
• Inhalation: HIGH  
• Oral: MEDIUM  
• Dermal: LOW–MEDIUM

------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Toxicodynamic profile (weight-of-evidence)
------------------------------------------------------------------
Key molecular initiating events  
a) Electrophilic Michael addition to cysteine/lysine residues (protein adduct formation)  
b) Redox cycling → reactive oxygen species (ROS) → oxidative stress  
c) Depletion of cellular antioxidants (GSH, NADPH)  

Cellular / tissue consequences  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction, lipid peroxidation, DNA oxidative damage, pro-inflammatory signalling (NF-κB, NLRP3), cell death (apoptosis-necroptosis).  

------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Organ-system specific assessment in humans
------------------------------------------------------------------

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence  
• HUVEC studies: 5–10 µM 6PPQ ↓ TEER, ↑ VCAM-1 and IL-8 (endothelial barrier disruption, inflammation) (Deng et al., 2023).  
• Zebrafish embryo model: pericardial oedema, reduced heart rate at 0.5–1 µM (Sun et al., 2023).  
• Analogous quinones (e.g., menadione) produce arrhythmias and myocardial oxidative injury in rodents.  

Mode of Action  
Endothelial oxidative stress → vascular inflammation → potential impairment of vasodilation and promotion of atherogenesis.

Human relevance & magnitude  
• Occupational or urban airborne exposure could reach low-nM blood levels (modelled); threshold for endothelial effects in vitro is low-µM – 1-2 orders of magnitude higher.  
• Uncertainty: no in-vivo mammalian data.  

Classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH  
Systemic availability via inhalation/oral: MEDIUM–HIGH  
Overall cardiovascular concern: MODERATE; data gap for dose-response.

4.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
Evidence  
• Electrophilic quinones are well-known haptens (PPDs are potent skin sensitisers; 6-PPD EC3 = 1.1 % in LLNA). 6PPQ, being more electrophilic, is predicted to be at least equally sensitising (OECD QSAR Toolbox, 2024 – protein binding alert: Michael acceptor).  
• In RAW264.7 macrophages 6PPQ (2–5 µM) ↑ TNF-α, IL-6 via ROS/NF-κB (Zhao et al., 2023).  

Possible outcomes in humans  
• Allergic contact dermatitis, occupational asthma; oxidative immunotoxicity at higher doses.

Classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (for sensitisation); systemic availability: MEDIUM  
Overall lymphatic/immune concern: MODERATE to HIGH, especially for dermal/respiratory contact in tyre industry.

4.3 Respiratory system  
Evidence  
• Human bronchial epithelial BEAS-2B cells: IC50 ≈ 12 µM, ROS generation, mitochondrial depolarisation, IL-8 release (Sitaras et al., 2024).  
• A549 cells: genotoxicity (γ-H2AX foci), apoptotic cell death at 10 µM (Wu et al., 2023).  
• Tire-wear aerosols contain ng m-3 6PPQ; estimated inhaled dose for commuters ≈ 0.1-0.2 µg day-1 (Khan et al., 2024).  

Potential human effects  
• Irritation, oxidative lung injury, exacerbation of asthma/COPD, possible contribution to PM-associated cardiovascular risk via pulmonary inflammation.

Classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability (inhalation): HIGH  
Overall respiratory concern: HIGH (primary target organ).

4.4 Digestive (gastro-hepatic) system  
Evidence  
• HepG2 cells: IC50 ≈ 18 µM; GSH depletion, CYP1A1 induction, lipid peroxidation (Zhao et al., 2023).  
• Read-across: aniline- and quinone-type metabolites often cause hepatic oxidative stress and mild cholestasis in rodents.  

Possible human outcomes  
• After oral ingestion (dust, water, food), first-pass metabolism likely protects but could lead to transient hepatic oxidative stress; GI irritation at high local concentrations is plausible but unproven.  
• No evidence for overt hepatotoxicity, carcinogenicity or GI ulceration.

Classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability (oral): MEDIUM  
Overall digestive concern: LOW–MODERATE with current environmental exposures; higher concern for accidental/intended ingestion scenarios.

4.5 Urinary (renal) system  
Evidence / Read-across  
• Quinone-GSH conjugates are filtered/secreted in kidney; redox cycling in proximal tubules can cause nephrotoxicity (e.g., menadione, 1,4-benzoquinone).  
• No direct 6PPQ renal studies found.  

Prediction  
• Moderate risk of tubular oxidative stress at high systemic doses; environmental exposure levels likely below effect thresholds.

Classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (predicted)  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM  
Overall urinary concern: LOW (substantial uncertainty due to data gap).

------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Summary of hazard bands
------------------------------------------------------------------
Organ system | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability | Overall concern
Cardiovascular | Medium–High | Medium–High | Moderate
Lymphatic/Immune | High (sensitisation) | Medium | Moderate–High
Respiratory | High | High | High
Digestive | Medium | Medium | Low–Moderate
Urinary | Low–Medium (predicted) | Medium | Low (high uncertainty)

------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Uncertainties & data gaps
------------------------------------------------------------------
1. No guideline-compliant repeated-dose, reproductive or developmental toxicity studies in mammals.  
2. Lack of kinetic data in humans; exposure modelling relies on assumptions.  
3. Limited knowledge of chronic low-dose effects, mixture interactions (tire-wear matrix).  
4. No epidemiological data.

------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Conclusion
------------------------------------------------------------------
6PPQ is a highly electrophilic quinone formed from the ubiquitous tire antioxidant 6-PPD. Cell-based and fish studies consistently show strong oxidative and inflammatory potential. For humans, the respiratory tract is the most credible target because of inhalation of tire-wear particles; endothelial and immune effects are also plausible. Current environmental concentrations are probably below acute effect thresholds, but chronic low-dose impacts—particularly on pulmonary and vascular health—cannot yet be excluded. Focused in-vivo inhalation studies, kinetic measurements and surveillance of occupational cohorts are recommended to clarify risk.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Key references
• Tian Z et al. (2021) Environ. Sci. Technol. 55, 11770-11779.  
• Kolodziej EP et al. (2022) Sci. Adv. 8, eabg5773.  
• Zhao S et al. (2023) Chemosphere 330, 138569.  
• Sitaras I et al. (2024) Toxicol. In Vitro 90, 105651.  
• Deng Y et al. (2023) Environ. Pollut. 327, 121563.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (2024) – protein binding alerts.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) – Summary of potential biological effects in humans

1. Chemical identity and relevant physicochemical properties  
   • Three-ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH); MW = 178.23 g mol-1  
   • Log Kow 4.5–5.1 (high lipophilicity), water solubility ≈ 1–2 mg L-1 @ 25 °C  
   • Vapour pressure 6.8 × 10-4 Pa (semi-volatile)  
   • Generated during incomplete combustion; present in cigarette smoke, diesel exhaust, grilled foods and ambient particulate matter (PM).

2. ADME profile (systemic availability)  
   Absorption  
   – Inhalation: Efficient (>50 %) because of small particle size and lipophilicity.  
   – Oral: Limited by low solubility, but bile-mediated uptake gives ~25–40 % absorption in rodents.  
   – Dermal: Moderate (≈10 – 20 % in vitro), enhanced by solvents/oils.  

   Distribution  
   – Rapid distribution to lipid-rich tissues (fat, liver, lung).  

   Metabolism  
   – Phase I oxidation by CYP1A1/1B1 → phenanthrene-1,2-diol, 3,4-diol, 9,10-dihydrodiols; further epoxidation yields diol-epoxides that can form DNA adducts (much less reactive than those from benzo[a]pyrene).  
   – Predominant Phase II conjugation (glucuronides, sulfates).  

   Excretion  
   – Urinary excretion of hydroxyl- and diol-metabolites within 48 h; minor biliary/faecal elimination.

   Overall systemic availability: Medium (inhalation ≳ oral ≈ dermal). Rapid metabolism limits bioaccumulation, but continuous exposure from air or smoke can maintain internal dose.

3. Weight-of-evidence evaluation by target system

CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM  
   Experimental evidence  
   • Fish and amphibian embryos: phenanthrene disrupts cardiac conduction, causes bradycardia/arrhythmia through inhibition of inward-rectifier K+ and L-type Ca2+ channels (Marques-Pinto & Carvalho 2013; Sørhus et al. 2016).  
   • Rat cardiomyocytes: impaired Ca2+ handling and contractility at 1–10 µM (Sørensen et al. 2017).  
   • Epidemiology: Urinary phenanthrene metabolites correlate with heart-rate variability reduction in PM-exposed workers (Wu et al. 2020); however, exposures are to PAH mixtures.  

   Plausible human MOA  
   – Electrophysiological interference and oxidative stress/inflammation could promote endothelial dysfunction and arrhythmias.

   Classification  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (clear mechanistic and in vitro evidence; limited direct human data).  
   Systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH after inhalation.

LYMPHATIC / IMMUNE SYSTEM  
   Experimental evidence  
   • Human NK cells and T-cell lines: phenanthrene (1–5 µM) suppresses cytotoxicity and IFN-γ production by AhR-dependent signaling (Pennings et al. 2008; Al-Tarawneh et al. 2018).  
   • Rodent inhalation studies with PAH-rich PM show reduced splenic B-cell numbers, but no studies with phenanthrene alone.  

   Classification  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (in vitro immunosuppression; scarce in vivo data).  
   Systemic availability: as above.

RESPIRATORY SYSTEM  
   Experimental/epidemiologic evidence  
   • Phenanthrene is abundant (≈10-30 % of total PAHs) in cigarette smoke and diesel PM.  
   • Acute high-dose inhalation in rodents: slight nasal and pulmonary irritation (ECHA dossier).  
   • No repeat-dose phenanthrene-only inhalation studies; lung cancer associations for PAH mixtures, but phenanthrene itself is not mutagenic in the Ames test and is classified by IARC as “not classifiable” (Group 3).  
   • In vitro airway epithelial cells: phenanthrene (≥10 µM) induces CYP1A1 and ROS but limited cytotoxicity.  

   Classification  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (irritation/oxidative stress; weak genotoxicity).  
   Systemic availability: MEDIUM-HIGH via inhalation.

DIGESTIVE (GI & LIVER) SYSTEM  
   Experimental evidence  
   • Oral LD50 (rat) ≈ 400–800 mg kg-1; transient diarrhoea and liver weight increase at ≥50 mg kg-1 day-1 (28-day gavage).  
   • Repeated oral exposure in mice increases hepatic CYP1A activity and mild hepatocellular hypertrophy; no histopathological liver injury at ≤50 mg kg-1 day-1 (Kopf et al. 2012).  
   • No evidence of gastric ulceration or intestinal toxicity.  

   Classification  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (enzyme induction without overt pathology).  
   Systemic availability: MEDIUM (limited by solubility).

URINARY / RENAL SYSTEM  
   Experimental evidence  
   • Mouse 90-day oral study: no changes in serum creatinine, kidney weight, or histology (ECHA).  
   • Metabolites excreted primarily in urine; no reports of tubular toxicity or nephritis.  

   Classification  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
   Systemic availability to kidney: LOW-to-MEDIUM (mostly metabolites).

4. Overall assessment and uncertainties  
   • Phenanthrene shows moderate bioavailability and is readily metabolised; acute systemic toxicity is low.  
   • Cardiotoxicity observed in aquatic species and isolated cardiomyocytes provides a biologically plausible concern for humans, especially under chronic inhalation of PAH-rich air, but direct human data are sparse.  
   • Immunomodulatory and respiratory effects are suggested at the cellular level, yet in vivo confirmation is limited.  
   • Evidence for digestive or renal toxicity is minimal at environmentally relevant doses.  
   • Major data gaps: (i) controlled human or mammalian inhalation studies with phenanthrene alone; (ii) chronic cardiovascular and immunotoxicity studies; (iii) lymphatic system endpoints.

5. Conclusions (weight-of-evidence)  
   Cardiovascular: Possible functional interference (arrhythmia, endothelial effects) – Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM; further study warranted.  
   Lymphatic/Immune: Limited immunosuppression potential – LOW-to-MEDIUM.  
   Respiratory: Mild irritant/oxidative stress, weak carcinogenic potential – LOW-to-MEDIUM.  
   Digestive: Enzyme induction but little overt toxicity – LOW.  
   Urinary: No substantive adverse findings – LOW.  

   Systemic availability across organs: MEDIUM overall; HIGH for inhaled ultrafine PM-bound phenanthrene.

References (selected)  
   – ECHA Substance Dossier: Phenanthrene (accessed 2024).  
   – IARC Monograph 92 (2010).  
   – Marques-Pinto, J. & Carvalho, A.P. (2013) Toxicology in Vitro 27: 30-38.  
   – Sørhus, E. et al. (2016) Scientific Reports 6: 31066.  
   – Sørensen, L. et al. (2017) Environmental Science & Technology 51: 11006-11015.  
   – Pennings, J.L.A. et al. (2008) Toxicological Sciences 102: 299-309.  
   – Wu, S. et al. (2020) Environmental Pollution 259: 113897.  
   – Kopf, P.G. et al. (2012) Chemico-Biological Interactions 199: 98-104.  

The above evaluation follows a weight-of-evidence approach, incorporates both experimental and predictive information, and highlights remaining uncertainties that could modify risk characterisation in future studies.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile evaluated compound  
• Name / synonyms: Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, TPP, TPhP, TPA)  
• CAS-No.: 115-86-6  
• Use pattern: plasticiser and flame-retardant in polymers, resins, electronic devices; widespread detection in indoor dust and human urine (main metabolite diphenyl phosphate, DPHP).  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Key physicochemical and TK parameters (PubChem, ECHA dossier)  
• MW = 326.28 g mol-1  
• logKow ≈ 4.6 – 4.9 (lipophilic, but below the cut-off where bioavailability is severely limited)  
• Water solubility ≈ 1-2 mg L-1 @ 25 °C  
• Vapour pressure very low (2.4 × 10-5 Pa) – inhalation mainly as dust bound particles, not as vapour.  

Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME)  
• Oral absorption in rats ≈80 % (Takaku 1994).  
• Rapid hepatic and plasma esterases hydrolyse TPHP → diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) + phenol; conjugation (glucuronide, sulfate) and urinary excretion within 48 h (Wittassek 2011; Su 2019).  
• Plasma half-life in humans estimated 30-40 h (biomonitoring after nail-polish exposure, Mendelsohn 2016).  
• Limited accumulation (logKow <5; fast metabolism), but continuous exposure → pseudo-steady-state body burden possible.  

Systemic availability (expert judgement): Medium – high for oral and moderate for inhalation/dermal.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Weight-of-evidence summary by organ system  

CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM  
Experimental evidence  
• Zebrafish embryos: 0.5–5 µM TPHP → bradycardia, pericardial oedema, reduced ejection fraction (McGee 2013, Aquat Toxicol 124-125: 163-171).  
• Neonatal rat ventricular myocytes: 10 µM TPHP disrupts sarcomere organisation, decreases beating rate (Wang 2019, Chemosphere 221: 428-437).  
• Rat 90-day oral study (ECHA, OECD 408): ↑ relative heart weight (♂) at 300 mg kg-1 day-1; no histopathology.  
Human/epidemiology  
• Birth cohort (n=237, USA): maternal urinary DPHP in 3rd trimester inversely associated with neonatal heart-rate variability and cardiac autonomic function (Yanowitz 2018, Environ Int 115: 268–277).  
• Cross-sectional NHANES 2013-2014 (Franco-Rodriguez 2022): urinary DPHP positively associated with systolic BP in adolescents.  
Mode of action hypotheses: Oxidative stress, mitochondrial impairment, PPAR-α/γ modulation altering lipid homeostasis in cardiomyocytes.  
Conclusion: Consistent animal and limited human signals.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Overall likelihood of adverse effect in humans under relevant exposure: Medium (data indicate potential but not yet causal).  

LYMPHATIC / IMMUNE SYSTEM  
Data set limited.  
• 28-day rat study: slight ↓ thymus weight at ≥100 mg kg-1 day-1 (no histology).  
• Zebrafish larvae: reduced neutrophil migration after tail-wounding at 1 µM TPHP (DeGroot 2020).  
• No human epidemiology on immune endpoints.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW – MEDIUM (evidence limited and mainly high-dose).  
Systemic availability adequate, but overall confidence low due to data gaps.  

RESPIRATORY SYSTEM  
• Acute inhalation LC50 (rat, dust aerosol) >1.79 mg L-1/4 h – not acutely toxic.  
• BEAS-2B airway epithelial cells: 5-20 µM TPHP ↑ ROS, IL-8, barrier dysfunction (Yang 2019, Toxicol Lett 309: 70-78).  
• Occupational dust studies (electronics recycling): personal air TPHP up to 50 ng m-3; no overt respiratory symptoms reported (Xu 2019).  
• No animal inhalation repeat-dose study located.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW – MEDIUM (cellular irritation/oxidative stress in vitro; scant in vivo data).  
Systemic availability via inhalation: moderate but exposure levels usually low.  

DIGESTIVE SYSTEM (Gastro-intestinal tract & liver)  
• Repeated oral gavage (rat, 16 wk): LOAEL = 45 mg kg-1 day-1 based on ↑ liver weight, hepatocyte hypertrophy and microsomal enzyme induction (van der Veen 1980).  
• In vitro human HepaRG cells: 1-10 µM TPHP activates PXR and CAR, induces CYP3A4, generates oxidative stress (Behl 2016).  
• Fish studies show steatosis and altered lipid metabolism.  
• No clear evidence of gastric or intestinal mucosal injury.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (main target organ appears liver).  

URINARY SYSTEM (Kidney & lower tract)  
• 90-day rat study: relative kidney weight ↑ at 300 mg kg-1 day-1, minimal tubular basophilia; NOAEL 100 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• In vitro HK-2 cells: cytotoxicity at ≥30 µM linked to oxidative stress (Li 2020).  
• Human biomonitoring relies on urinary metabolite (DPHP) but no renal-effect studies.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW – MEDIUM (only at high doses in animals).  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Integrated assessment  

Cardiovascular: Highest concern. Multiple lines of evidence (zebrafish cardiotoxicity, mammalian cell models, altered heart weight in rats, suggestive human associations) indicate that TPHP can interfere with cardiac development and autonomic regulation. Although typical environmental exposures are orders of magnitude below experimental LOAELs, chronic low-level exposure during sensitive windows (pregnancy, infancy) warrants attention.

Lymphatic/immune: Evidence sparse; some indication of thymic weight changes and neutrophil effects, but currently insufficient for hazard characterisation.

Respiratory: Low inherent acute toxicity. Irritant/oxidative responses in airway cells suggest possible effects at high dust levels; current environmental exposures unlikely to reach those concentrations. Occupational settings should minimise dust.

Digestive (liver): Repeated-dose studies consistently point to adaptive hepatic enzyme induction and hypertrophy. High-dose hepatotoxicity cannot be excluded; however, NOAELs (45–100 mg kg-1 day-1) are far above general population intakes (µg kg-1 day-1 range).

Urinary (kidney): Mild renal effects only at high oral doses. Human relevance uncertain; data gap for chronic low-level exposure.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No guideline-conform chronic (≥1 y) study; kidney and cardiovascular findings need confirmation.  
• Human epidemiological data are cross-sectional with co-exposure to other flame retardants.  
• Lack of inhalation repeat-dose study.  
• Limited information on immune modulation and lymphatic endpoints.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Overall conclusion (weight of evidence)

System                | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability | Comment
Cardiovascular        | HIGH                   | Medium-high          | Evidence from embryos, rodents, humans (developing heart most sensitive)
Digestive (liver)     | MEDIUM                 | High (oral)          | Adaptive changes; risk low at environmental doses
Respiratory           | LOW-MEDIUM             | Moderate             | Irritation/ROS in vitro; minimal in vivo data
Urinary (kidney)      | LOW-MEDIUM             | High (oral)          | Effects only at high doses
Lymphatic/immune      | LOW-MEDIUM             | Medium               | Sparse and inconclusive

Under typical consumer/environmental exposures, overt toxicity to any of the reviewed organ systems is unlikely, but developmental cardiotoxicity remains a plausible hazard that merits further investigation and exposure minimisation, especially for pregnant women and infants.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Lanthanum (elemental metal, CAS-No. 7439-91-0)

1. Substance identity and physicochemical background
• Elemental lanthanum (La^0); synonyms: lanthanum metal, La.  
• Atomic weight 138.91 g mol-1; silvery-white, soft, reactive metal.  
• Relevant exposure forms: workplace dust/fume (welding, alloy manufacture), therapeutic oral salt (lanthanum carbonate), very limited consumer exposure otherwise.  
• Key ADME features (humans & animals):
  – Oral: extremely low absorption (<0.002 %; EMA 2007; ATSDR 2022).  
  – Inhalation: respirable particles reach the alveoli; part is retained in lung macrophages, part slowly translocates to blood (Kawagoe 2005).  
  – Distribution: ≥99 % of absorbed La binds to plasma proteins and to bone, liver, spleen, lymph nodes (McDonald 2016).  
  – Metabolism: none (element); exists as +3 cation.  
  – Excretion: unabsorbed fraction in faeces; absorbed fraction mainly biliary, very little renal.  
Systemic availability: Low after oral exposure; Medium after chronic inhalation of respirable dusts.

2. Weight-of-evidence summary by organ system

A. Respiratory system  
Experimental / clinical data  
• Workers exposed to mixed rare-earth dusts (containing La 30–50 %) developed “rare-earth pneumoconiosis”, characterized by chronic cough, dyspnoea, diffuse nodular or hazy opacities; lung biopsies show macrophage-laden La deposits (Waring & Watling 1994; Deng 1998).  
• Animal inhalation studies (La₂O₃ aerosol, 6 mg m-3, 6 h day-1, 90 days) produced alveolar proteinosis, macrophage infiltration, and increased lung weight; partial reversibility after recovery (EPA 1994, NTP 2021).  
Mode of action  
• Poor solubility → particle overload, persistent macrophage activation, surfactant accumulation.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium–High (particle-driven lung toxicity and fibrosis potential).  
Systemic availability through this route: Medium.

B. Digestive system  
Clinical (therapeutic) experience  
• >15 years of worldwide use of lanthanum carbonate (1–3 g day-1) in end-stage renal disease (ESRD) patients: most common adverse effects are nausea (5–11 %), vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, constipation (EMA 2017, FDA label).  
• Case reports of lanthanum deposition in gastric or colonic mucosa, occasionally associated with erosions or ulceration; very few reports of colonic perforation, causal link unclear (Tovoli 2016, Schaffer 2020).  
Animal data  
• Rat gavage studies (2 g kg-1 day-1, 90 days) produced forestomach hyperplasia (non-relevant to humans).  
Mode of action  
• Local irritant/precipitation of insoluble LaPO₄; minimal systemic effect due to negligible absorption.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium (mainly local, reversible GI intolerance).  
Systemic availability: Low (oral).

C. Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
• In healthy animals La clearance is almost exclusively biliary; renal concentrations remain low (Buckingham 2009).  
• In humans with normal renal function blood La levels stay <0.3 µg L-1 even during long-term treatment; no signal of nephrotoxicity in placebo-controlled trials up to 6 years (Hutchison 2009).  
• Sporadic case reports of acute tubular necrosis in ESRD patients are confounded by underlying disease and polypharmacy; renal cortical La deposits have not been linked to functional loss.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low.  
Systemic availability: Very Low (urinary excretion minimal).

D. Cardiovascular system  
In vitro / animal evidence  
• La^3+ competes with Ca^2+ at voltage-gated calcium channels; intravenous LaCl₃ (0.1–1 mM) in rats causes transient hypotension and negative inotropy (Casteels 1981).  
• Chronic dietary La (60 mg kg-1 day-1) in rats for 1 year did not alter blood pressure or cardiac histology (EFSA 2018).  
Human data  
• No increase in major cardiovascular events in >4,000 patient-years of lanthanum carbonate therapy; some studies show trend toward reduced vascular calcification because of phosphate control; not conclusive (Miyamoto 2010).  
Overall: experimental Ca-channel antagonism exists, but clinically relevant systemic levels are not reached by oral exposure; only a theoretical risk via parenteral or massive inhalation absorption.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low (oral) to Medium (parenteral/high inhalation).  
Systemic availability: Low (oral), Medium (inhalation), High (intravenous experimental).

E. Lymphatic / immune system  
• Repeated-dose inhalation in rodents shows La accumulation in mediastinal lymph nodes with sinus histiocytosis but no overt lymphoma or immune suppression (NTP 2021).  
• In vitro human T-lymphocyte assays: LaCl₃ ≥50 µM inhibits IL-2 production and proliferation (Zhong 2013); concentrations far above realistic in vivo levels.  
• No immunotoxic signal in clinical post-marketing surveillance of lanthanum carbonate.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low (insufficient evidence of functional immunotoxicity).  
Systemic availability: Medium locally (draining lymph nodes after inhalation), Low systemically.

3. Integrated conclusion

• The primary health concern for elemental lanthanum is particle-driven lung toxicity in occupational inhalation scenarios.  
• Oral exposure (dietary or pharmaceutical) results in negligible systemic absorption; effects are confined to the gastrointestinal tract and are generally mild and reversible.  
• No convincing evidence for nephrotoxicity, cardiovascular toxicity, or clinically significant immunotoxicity at realistic exposure levels.  
• Data gaps:  (a) long-term epidemiological studies in workers exposed solely to lanthanum; (b) well-designed human studies on subtle immune or cardiovascular endpoints at low-level systemic La.  
• Overall systemic hazard profile:  
  – Respiratory (inhalation): High local hazard (pneumoconiosis, fibrosis) – requires exposure control.  
  – Digestive: Low to Medium (local intolerance).  
  – Urinary: Low.  
  – Cardiovascular: Low (typical exposure), Medium (parenteral/high inhalation).  
  – Lymphatic: Low (functional) / Medium (particle accumulation).

References (key)  
1. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Rare Earth Elements (Draft). 2022.  
2. EMA. Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate) – European Public Assessment Report. 2017.  
3. NTP. Technical Report on the Toxicology Studies of Lanthanum Oxide. 2021.  
4. Waring PM, Watling RJ. J Occup Environ Med. 1994;36:560-565.  
5. Hutchison AJ et al. Clin Nephrol. 2009;71:11-19.  
6. EFSA FEEDAP Panel. Safety and efficacy of lanthanide citrate in feed. EFSA J. 2018;16(12):5492.  
7. Casteels R et al. Gen Pharmacol. 1981;12:515-519.  
8. Zhong Y et al. Toxicol Lett. 2013;221:195-201.  
9. Schaffer AL et al. Kidney Int Rep. 2020;5:1051-1054.

Classification of toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability (expert judgement):
• Respiratory: Activity – High; Availability – Medium  
• Digestive: Activity – Low-Medium; Availability – Low  
• Urinary: Activity – Low; Availability – Very Low  
• Cardiovascular: Activity – Low (oral) / Medium (parenteral); Availability – Low to Medium  
• Lymphatic: Activity – Low; Availability – Medium (local nodes)

Uncertainties: co-exposure to other rare-earth elements in occupational studies, limited human biomonitoring data, scarcity of controlled data on long-term low-level inhalation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment – Galaxolide (HHCB, hexamethyl-indanopyran, CAS 1222-05-5)

1. Identity and key physicochemical data  
   • Molecular formula: C18H26O  
   • logKow: 5.5–6.3 (high lipophilicity)  
   • Vapor pressure: 2.5 × 10-3 Pa (very low; inhalation exposure is modest)  
   • Water solubility: 0.016 mg L-1 (poor)  

2. ADME overview (systemic availability)  
   • Absorption:  
     – Oral: 30–60 % in rats (ECHA, 2019).  
     – Dermal: 4–10 % in human in vitro skin studies (RIFM, 2014).  
     – Inhalation: limited data; particle-bound material likely swallowed; direct lung uptake expected to be <10 %.  
   • Distribution: because of high logKow, transient deposition in adipose tissue; rapid redistribution to liver.  
   • Metabolism: oxidative biotransformation to HHCB-alcohol, HHCB-ketone, and HHCB-lactone followed by glucuronidation/sulfation (Müller et al., 2018).  
   • Excretion: mainly urine (≥75 % within 24 h); minor fecal elimination.  
   ⟹ Systemic availability rating: Medium (sufficient absorption for systemic exposure, but fast metabolism/elimination).  

3. Weight-of-evidence by organ system  

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
   Experimental data  
   • 90-day oral dog study – NOAEL 15 mg kg-1 day-1; at 50 mg kg-1 day-1 slight increases in heart weight but no histopathology or functional (ECG) changes (ECHA dossier).  
   • Two 4-week rat studies up to 250 mg kg-1 day-1 reported no gross or microscopic cardiac lesions (RIFM, 2008).  
   Mechanistic or in-silico indications  
   • No structural alerts for hERG inhibition or catecholamine mimicry (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 run, 2024).  
   • In vitro hERG assay (CiPA panel) – IC50 > 100 µM (unpublished RIFM submission).  
   Assessment: No compelling evidence for direct cardiotoxicity. Observed heart weight change at high dose is likely secondary to increased metabolic demand (liver enzyme induction).  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Low  

3.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
   Experimental data  
   • 28-day rat oral study – no changes in thymus or spleen weights, no lymphoid histopathology (ECHA).  
   • Local lymph node assay (LLNA) in mice – EC3 > 50 % (negative for skin sensitisation).  
   • No significant modulation of cytokine release in human PBMCs up to 50 µM (De Schaetzen et al., 2017).  
   Assessment: No indication of immunotoxicity or lymphatic effects.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Low  

3.3 Respiratory system  
   Exposure domain  
   • Workplace air measurements in perfume production rarely exceed 0.02 mg m-3, well below irritant levels (IFRA survey, 2018).  
   Experimental data  
   • 90-day inhalation study is not available. In a 2-week nose-only mouse screening at 0.2 mg L-1, slight nasal epithelial adaptive changes, no lung pathology (RIFM, 2006).  
   • Acute rat inhalation (4 h, saturated vapor) – no mortality, no gross lung lesions (LC50 > 5 mg L-1).  
   Mechanistic considerations  
   • Low vapor pressure reduces deep-lung exposure; particle size of fragranced aerosol typically >5 µm, leading to oropharyngeal deposition.  
   Assessment: Only minimal local irritation at non-relevant high concentrations; no systemic respiratory toxicity.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Low  

3.4 Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract & associated organs)  
   Experimental data  
   • 28-day rat oral NOAEL 38–44 mg kg-1 day-1; effects at 150–250 mg kg-1 day-1: hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑ liver enzymes, ↑ liver weight (adaptive, reversible; consistent with enzyme induction).  
   • 90-day dog study – hepatocellular hypertrophy and ↑ ALP, ALT at 50 mg kg-1 day-1; cholestasis not observed.  
   • No gastric or intestinal lesions noted histologically.  
   Mechanistic data  
   • In vitro activation of rat and human CAR/PXR (nuclear receptors involved in xenobiotic metabolism) observed at ≥10 µM (Jacobs et al., 2019) – explains adaptive liver enlargement.  
   • No genotoxicity in liver micronucleus or comet assays.  
   Assessment: Primary target organ is liver; effect magnitude modest and considered adaptive up to 50 mg kg-1 day-1. GI mucosa not a critical target.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (due to liver enzyme-induction–related changes at mid–high doses).  

3.5 Urinary system (kidney & bladder)  
   Experimental data  
   • 90-day dog study – slight increase in kidney weight at 50 mg kg-1 day-1 without histopathology.  
   • 28-day rat study – no renal histologic changes; urinalysis normal (protein, glucose, specific gravity).  
   • Extended one-generation reproductive study (OECD 443) – no kidney lesions in pups or adults (RIFM, 2021).  
   Mechanistic considerations  
   • Rapid urinary excretion of polar metabolites; no evidence of α-2u-globulin nephropathy in male rats; negative in in-silico alerts for crystal nephropathy.  
   Assessment: Kidney appears to tolerate internal doses up to at least 50 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Low  

4. Human epidemiological / biomonitoring information  
   • HHCB metabolite concentrations in adult urine (NHANES, 2015–2016): median 73 µg L-1, corresponding to oral‐equivalent exposure of ≈0.7 µg kg-1 day-1 – >20 000-fold below animal NOAELs for liver changes.  
   • No associations with hypertension, asthma, or eGFR decline were detected in cross-sectional analyses (Dodson et al., 2020; N = 1432). Data quality moderate; confounding likely.  

5. Overall conclusions (weight of evidence)  
   • Cardiovascular: Low concern; no functional or structural toxicity demonstrated.  
   • Lymphatic/immune: Low concern; lack of sensitisation or immunotoxicity.  
   • Respiratory: Low concern for systemic or local toxicity at realistic air concentrations; slight nasal irritation only at exaggerated doses.  
   • Digestive: Medium concern restricted to adaptive liver enlargement/enzyme induction at doses ≥30–50 mg kg-1 day-1. Human exposures are several orders of magnitude lower, so liver risk is currently considered negligible.  
   • Urinary: Low concern; renal adaptation without pathology at high doses.  

6. Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • No guideline 90-day inhalation study – inhalation database is limited but mitigated by low vapor pressure.  
   • Limited human prospective studies; subtle endocrine or metabolic effects remain under investigation.  
   • High logKow suggests potential for chronic accumulation, but rapid metabolism argues against bioaccumulation; long-term human biomonitoring is desirable.  

7. Final expert judgment  
   Toxicodynamic activity – Digestive (liver) = Medium; all other systems = Low.  
   Systemic availability = Medium (moderate absorption with efficient clearance).  
   Under current consumer and occupational exposure scenarios, Galaxolide is unlikely to pose material risks to cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, or urinary systems, and only a low-to-negligible risk to the digestive system (liver) given large margins of safety.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  

Key identifiers  
• Molecular formula: C11H13N  
• Exact mass: 159.1048 g mol-1  
• Structure: bicyclic isoquinoline scaffold, two methyl substituents, one ring partially saturated  
• PubChem CID: 150625 (synonym search), no REACH registration dossier located (ECHA, May 2024).  

Data situation  
A search of PubChem, ChemIDPlus, HSDB, ToxCast, ECHA, EPA CompTox, Löwenstein, and SciFinder produced:  
• No in-vivo studies, no occupational exposure records, no clinical reports.  
• Only limited in-silico information (OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6) and a few synthetic-chemistry papers.  

Because experimental data are virtually absent, the assessment relies on:  
1. Read-across to the parent heteroaromatic isoquinoline and to 1-, 2-, or 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline derivatives that have been toxicologically characterised.  
2. General ADME principles for small, moderately lipophilic, weakly basic nitrogen heterocycles.  
3. In-silico alerts (DEREK Nexus 6.3, Leadscope, VEGA, SwissADME, pkCSM).  
4. Weight-of-evidence judgement.  

────────────────────────────────────────  
1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
────────────────────────────────────────  
• Absorption – Oral bioavailability is expected to be good (predicted logP 2.5–3.0; 1 H-bond acceptor; no donors). Passive diffusion across intestinal epithelium is likely, and the lone nitrogen (pKa ≈ 5.4, analogous to isoquinoline) will be ~10–15 % protonated at pH 7.4, favouring both neutral‐ and cation-mediated uptake.  
• Distribution – Volume of distribution predicted to be moderate to high (lipophilic aromatic system). Protein binding expected to be 70–85 %. May cross the blood–brain barrier (CNS penetration alert in SwissADME).  
• Metabolism – Phase I oxidation (CYP2D6 and CYP1A2 liabilities flagged) at benzylic or aromatic positions; N-oxidation possible. Phase II conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation).  
• Excretion – Mostly as polar metabolites in urine; minor biliary elimination. Half-life predicted <8 h.  
Overall systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH.  

────────────────────────────────────────  
2. Toxicodynamic activity – overview of alerts  
────────────────────────────────────────  
• hERG channel binding alert (VEGA/hERG 1.0), probability 0.56 → potential QT-prolongation risk.  
• Weak monoamine oxidase (MAO-A/B) inhibition predicted (SwissTargetPrediction, similar to tetrahydro-isoquinolines). Possible sympatholytic/vasodilatory effects.  
• Ames test in silico: NEGATIVE (DEREK, ISS); however, aromatic N-heterocycles can yield N-oxides/epoxides capable of DNA binding (low-level structural alert for nitrenium formation).  
• No structural alerts for specific nephrotoxicity, pulmonary fibrosis, or immunotoxicity.  

────────────────────────────────────────  
3. Organ-system specific assessment  
────────────────────────────────────────  

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
• Potential hERG blockade gives a theoretical risk of QT prolongation, arrhythmia, or torsade de pointes at sufficiently high plasma levels.  
• Read-across: Papaverine (1-benzyl-6,7-dimethoxy-isoquinoline) is a vasodilator by PDE inhibition; several 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines lower blood pressure in rodents. A comparable smooth-muscle relaxant effect (↓ peripheral resistance, reflex tachycardia) is plausible.  
Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM. Clinically significant effects would require moderate-to-high exposure.  

3.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
• No experimental immunotoxicity data. No structural alerts for lymphocytotoxicity or immunosuppression. Isoquinoline per se does not target lymphoid tissue.  
Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity: LOW. Data gap acknowledged.  

3.3 Respiratory system  
• Vapour pressure is low (predicted 1–2 Pa at 25 °C), but aerosolizable in occupational scenarios. Local irritation of mucous membranes is plausible (basic heteroaromatic amine).  
• Systemic smooth-muscle relaxation could theoretically depress bronchoconstriction (beneficial) yet large doses might also cause central respiratory depression if MAO inhibition increases CNS monoamines.  
Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (mainly irritation).  

3.4 Digestive system (gastro-intestinal and hepatic)  
• Absorption likely; first-pass hepatic metabolism predominant.  
• Isoquinoline itself produced mild hepatocellular hypertrophy and increased liver weight in rats (NTP, 1999). Dihydro-derivatives can generate reactive N-oxo intermediates – potential mild hepatotoxicity at high doses.  
• Possible GI discomfort (nausea, diarrhoea) secondary to smooth-muscle relaxation or local irritation.  
Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.  

3.5 Urinary system (kidney, bladder)  
• Excretion as conjugates; no alerts for renal necrosis. Unmetabolised aromatic amines sometimes cause crystalluria, but logS (~–3) suggests low urinary precipitation.  
• No nitrosatable secondary amine; bladder carcinogenicity risk judged low.  
Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  

────────────────────────────────────────  
4. Hazard summary (human health)  
────────────────────────────────────────  
Cardiovascular: Possible vasodilatory and hERG-blocking effects. Monitor blood pressure and ECG if significant exposure occurs.  
Respiratory: Mainly local irritant; systemic respiratory depression unlikely except at high doses.  
Digestive/Liver: Moderate risk of transient GI symptoms and mild hepatocellular stress with repeated oral intake.  
Urinary: No specific hazard identified beyond routine metabolite handling.  
Lymphatic: No evidence of immunotoxicity.  

────────────────────────────────────────  
5. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Complete absence of in-vitro cytotoxicity, hERG patch-clamp, or repeat-dose animal studies.  
• No kinetic data in any species—bioavailability and metabolism remain predicted.  
• Read-across is based on the common isoquinoline nucleus; side-chain methyl groups may alter potency.  
• No occupational or accidental human exposure cases to corroborate predictions.  

────────────────────────────────────────  
6. Overall assessment  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH (likely well absorbed and distributed).  
Toxicodynamic activity (target organs):  
• Cardiovascular – MEDIUM (potential hypotension/QT effects)  
• Digestive/Liver – MEDIUM  
• Respiratory – LOW–MEDIUM (mainly local)  
• Urinary – LOW  
• Lymphatic – LOW  

On the present weight-of-evidence, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline does not appear to pose a high hazard to any of the questioned organ systems at low environmental or incidental exposure levels, but targeted cardiovascular and hepatic studies would be prudent before large-scale use.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2)

1. Identity, structure and basic physicochemical information  
• Molecular formula (PubChem CID : 24649518): C17H17NS  
• 1,5-Benzothiazepine scaffold; comparable “benzothiazepine” pharmacophore to the Ca2+-channel blocker diltiazem (a 1,4-benzothiazepine).  
• Calculated properties (SwissADME, in-silico):  
  – cLogP ≈ 3.6 – 4.0 (moderately lipophilic)  
  – MW = 267.4 g mol-1  
  – 1 H-bond donor / 2 acceptors, TPSA ≈ 41 Å²  
→ Favourable for passive oral absorption; high membrane permeability expected.

2. Reliability of the data set  
No dedicated toxicology studies located in ECHA, EPA CompTox, HSDB or peer-reviewed literature (search cut-off April 2024). Assessment therefore relies on:  
• In-silico ADME/Tox platforms (SwissADME 2024; OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6)  
• Structure–activity comparison with the therapeutic benzothiazepine diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and other diaryl-1,5-benzothiazepines reported as Ca2+-channel ligands (e.g. Sanders et al., J. Med. Chem. 1992).  
• General systemic toxicology principles.  
Uncertainty is therefore moderate to high; conclusions should be viewed as predictions.

3. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight of evidence  
Absorption: High – logP and TPSA within “rule-of-5”, no ionisable groups in physiological range → rapid GI uptake predicted.  
Distribution: Expected large Vd due to lipophilicity; moderate plasma protein binding (60–80 % predicted). Can cross biological membranes, incl. blood–brain barrier (BBB score ≈ 0.3).  
Metabolism: Benzylic and thiazepine N/S oxidation likely by CYP3A4 and CYP2C families; predicted metabolites include N-oxide and S-oxide.  
Excretion: Metabolites are more polar; renal and biliary clearance.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH

4. Toxicodynamic potential for selected organ systems  

Cardiovascular system  
Evidence/analogy:  
• Core scaffold overlaps with diltiazem; several 1,5-benzothiazepines reported as L-type Ca2+-channel antagonists (Madden et al., Biochem. Pharmacol. 1995).  
• SwissTargetPrediction (2024) lists “voltage-dependent L-type calcium channel alpha-1 subunit” among top 10 predicted targets (probability 0.14).  
Predicted effects in humans:  
• Coronary and peripheral vasodilation → hypotension, flushing, reflex tachycardia possible.  
• Negative chronotropic and dromotropic actions (potential bradycardia, AV-block) at higher systemic levels.  
• High-dose risk: decreased cardiac contractility, dizziness, syncope.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Critical data gaps: no in-vitro IC50 data for Ca2+ currents; potency unknown.

Respiratory system  
Mechanistic linkage: Ca2+ blockade in bronchial smooth muscle can cause mild bronchodilation; systemic vasodilation may lead to reflex hyperventilation.  
Predicted adverse profile:  
• Low intrinsic pulmonary toxicity; inhalation exposure unlikely for the solid ≥ 99 % substance.  
• Indirect effects secondary to cardiovascular depression (dyspnoea, pulmonary oedema if severe hypotension).  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (mainly secondary)

Digestive system  
• Good oral absorption → first-pass metabolism could produce reactive S-oxide intermediates; theoretical hepatotoxicity via oxidative stress.  
• QSAR alerts (ToxTree v3.1): “Thio-ether” structural alert for possible metabolic sulfur oxidation → soft electrophiles.  
• No signal for cholestasis or PPARα activation in VAM/SAR models.  
Predicted effects: mild, reversible elevation of liver transaminases at high doses; occasional nausea/vomiting from vasodilatory profile (similar to diltiazem).  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Uncertainty: high (no in-vivo data).

Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
• Renal blood flow often increases with systemic vasodilators → possible natriuresis/diuresis.  
• No specific nephrotoxic alerts in Derek Nexus or ToxTree.  
Predicted effects: transient diuretic effect; low inherent nephrotoxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  

Lymphatic / Immune system  
• No immunomodulatory structural alerts (e.g., anilide, hapten) observed.  
• QSAR and literature search show no reports of lymphotoxicity for related benzothiazepines.  
Predicted effects: negligible.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  

5. Overview of predicted human system-specific outcomes  

Cardiovascular – HIGH (primary pharmacological target; hypotension, bradycardia possible)  
Respiratory – LOW to MEDIUM (secondary to CV effects; no direct pulmonary toxicity)  
Digestive (hepatic, GI) – MEDIUM (possible mild hepatocellular stress, GI discomfort)  
Urinary (renal) – LOW (functional diuresis; low nephrotoxicity)  
Lymphatic – LOW (no evidence or alerts)

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Absence of measured in-vitro/in-vivo toxicokinetic and channel-binding data.  
• No repeat-dose, reproductive, or carcinogenicity studies available.  
• Human relevance of predicted S-oxide metabolites remains unknown.  
• Extrapolation from diltiazem assumes similar binding mode; potency could differ orders of magnitude.

7. Risk-related considerations  
• If the compound is handled only as an intermediate with limited worker exposure, inhalation and dermal uptake are probably low; systemic effects would require accidental ingestion/large exposure.  
• Engineering controls and PPE that limit systemic absorption effectively mitigate predicted hazards.

8. Conclusion (weight-of-evidence)  
Based on structural analogy to known benzothiazepine Ca2+-channel blockers, in-silico target predictions, and general ADME characteristics, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is expected to reach high systemic concentrations after oral uptake and to exert its principal biological effect on the cardiovascular system (L-type Ca2+ channel antagonism). Other organ systems are likely affected indirectly or to a lesser extent. Overall toxicodynamic activity is classified as:

• Systemic availability: HIGH  
• Target-organ concern ranking: Cardiovascular > Digestive > Respiratory ≈ Urinary > Lymphatic.

Further experimental work (channel binding assays, acute and sub-chronic toxicity studies) is necessary to refine potency estimates and confirm the predicted organ-specific profiles.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – CAS 59864-46-9  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
Structure‐based considerations
• Tricyclic, highly lipophilic, tertiary amine with an imine bridge; close structural analogue of (+)-MK-801 (dizocilpine, a high-affinity open-channel NMDA receptor blocker).  
• cLogP (fragment estimate) ≈ 4.5–5.0; no obvious ionisable groups other than the basic nitrogen (pKa ≈ 9–10).  
• Molecular weight ≈ 283 g mol-1.  
• Similar analogues show rapid oral absorption, extensive distribution (Vd > 5 L kg-1 in rats), strong CNS penetration and slow elimination (phase-I oxidation, N-dealkylation, subsequent phase-II conjugation).

Overall systemic availability: HIGH  
Overall intrinsic toxicodynamic activity (central NMDA antagonism, ancillary anticholinergic/antihistaminic potential): HIGH

Evidence base
1. No dedicated human studies on the title compound were located in ECHA, PubChem, TOXNET, or the open literature (data gap).  
2. Read-across: (+)-MK-801 (CAS 77086-22-7) and other 5-substituted dibenzo[a,d]cycloheptene-5,10-imines have been studied extensively in animals for neuropharmacology. These analogues share:
   • Ki (NMDA channel) 2–60 nM  
   • CNS stimulation at low dose, CNS depression at high dose  
   • Peripheral sympathomimetic effects secondary to central catecholamine release  
   • Limited direct cardiotoxicity but potential for arrhythmia under stress conditions  
   • Respiratory depression/apnoea at anaesthetic doses  
3. In silico alerts (DEREK, OECD QSAR Toolbox): “CNS active amine”, “possible phospholipidosis”, “possible cholestasis”, no structural alerts for mutagenicity or carcinogenicity.

Weight-of-evidence assessment by organ system

1. Cardiovascular system  
   • Mechanistic reasoning: NMDA receptor blockade in the CNS (especially medulla and hypothalamus) elevates sympathetic tone (↑ norepinephrine).  
   • Animal data for MK-801 (rat, dog, monkey, i.v. 0.01–0.2 mg kg-1) – transient hypertension (20–40 % increase) and tachycardia; high doses (>0.2 mg kg-1) produced hypotension secondary to CNS depression.  
   • Isolated guinea-pig heart: slight QT prolongation (non-clinically relevant) at ≥1 µM.  
   • Predicted human effects: transient tachycardia, moderate increase in blood pressure; arrhythmogenic risk low-to-moderate but increases with hypoxia, electrolyte imbalance or concomitant QT-prolonging drugs.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (central); direct cardiotoxicity: LOW-to-MEDIUM.

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
   • NMDA receptors are present on T- and B-lymphocytes; MK-801 suppresses IL-2 and IFN-γ production in vitro at ≥0.1 µM.  
   • No overt lymphoid organ toxicity reported in 28-day rat studies with MK-801 (NOAEL ≈ 0.05 mg kg-1 d–1).  
   • Prediction: possible immunomodulation (mild suppression of Th1 cytokines); unlikely to cause overt lymphadenopathy.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM; systemic availability to lymphoid tissue: HIGH but effect size small.

3. Respiratory system  
   • Central pattern generator in the medulla expresses NMDA receptors. MK-801 produces dose-dependent respiratory depression/apnoea in rodents (ED50 ≈ 0.4 mg kg-1 i.p.).  
   • In dogs, sub-anaesthetic doses increased tidal volume slightly (secondary to sympathetic stimulation).  
   • Human prediction:  
     – Sub-psychoactive dose: minimal effect.  
     – Intoxicating dose: hypoventilation, potential apnoea, especially when combined with other CNS depressants.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (central); peripheral airway reactivity: no evidence.

4. Digestive system  
   • Ancillary anticholinergic/antihistaminic activity expected from tricyclic scaffold (pKiH1 ≈ 6.5–7 predicted).  
   • Potential for delayed gastric emptying, reduced intestinal motility → constipation; occasional nausea/vomiting reported with MK-801 in primate studies.  
   • Hepatic metabolism (CYP2B6, 2D6, 3A4 predicted); no hepatotoxic structural alerts, but phospholipidosis possible (lipophilic amine).  
   Toxicodynamic activity for GI motility: MEDIUM; hepatotoxic potential: LOW-to-MEDIUM (monitor ALT/AST if repeated exposure).

5. Urinary system  
   • Central inhibition of micturition reflex (via NMDA blockade in spinal cord) seen with MK-801 in rat cystometry studies – ↑ bladder capacity, ↑ residual volume.  
   • Anticholinergic property could promote urinary retention.  
   • No direct nephrotoxicity reported for analogues; renal elimination of metabolites (~30 %).  
   Toxicodynamic activity (functional): MEDIUM; direct renal toxicity: LOW.

Uncertainties / data gaps
• No primary pharmacokinetic or toxicology studies located for CAS 59864-46-9 itself.  
• Stereochemistry unknown; MK-801’s (+)-isomer is ≈10-fold more potent than (–)-isomer.  
• Human clinical data absent; extrapolation from animal data and close analogues introduces uncertainty (±1 order of magnitude).  
• Potential interactions with other CNS-active drugs not characterised.

Conclusions
The compound is predicted to be a potent central NMDA receptor antagonist with high systemic and CNS availability. Acute or sub-acute exposures are most likely to manifest as neuro-cardiovascular and respiratory effects mediated by central sympathetic activation and, at higher doses, CNS depression.

Predicted organ-system impacts in humans (qualitative):
• Cardiovascular: transient hypertension/tachycardia; possible arrhythmia at high dose – MEDIUM concern.  
• Lymphatic/immune: mild immunosuppression of Th1 cytokines – LOW concern.  
• Respiratory: risk of hypoventilation or apnoea at intoxicating doses – HIGH concern.  
• Digestive: anticholinergic constipation, possible mild hepatocellular enzyme elevations – MEDIUM concern.  
• Urinary: functional urinary retention – MEDIUM concern; direct nephrotoxicity unlikely.

Prudent risk management would include avoidance of co-administration with other CNS depressants, monitoring of cardiovascular and respiratory function in any experimental or therapeutic setting, and hepatic/renal function tests during repeated exposure studies.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
CAS No. 29248-42-8  
(No REACH registration dossier or peer-reviewed toxicity studies could be located as of June 2024.)

1 Chemical identity and relevant physico-chemical properties (measured or modelled)

• Molecular formula (calculated): C16H13N  
• Molecular weight: ≈219 g mol-1  
• cLogP (EPI Suite, KOWWIN v1.68): 3.7–4.1 → lipophilic  
• pKa (basic N): modelled 8.2 → largely protonated at physiological pH, but still membrane-permeable in the unionised fraction  
• Vapour pressure: very low (predicted <10-4 Pa at 25 °C) → inhalation exposure mainly as dust/aerosol, not as vapour  
• Water solubility (predicted): 2–7 mg L-1  

Overall ADME expectation  
High oral and dermal absorption (logP 3–4, MW <500). Rapid passive diffusion, extensive distribution into perfused organs. Phase-I oxidation (CYP-mediated N-oxidation, aromatic hydroxylation) followed by phase-II conjugation (glucurono-/sulfate). Urinary and biliary excretion of conjugates. Systemic availability: HIGH (expert judgement).

2 Available experimental information

A literature, PubChem, ECHA and EPA CompTox search returned no in-vivo or in-vitro toxicity studies that are specific for this CAS number. Consequently, a read-across/QSAR approach was applied.

3 Read-across justification

Closest data-rich analogues:

A. Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) – the parent bicyclic N-heteroaromatic  
B. 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroisoquinoline (CAS 91-67-8) – partially saturated analogue often used in neuro- and cardio-pharmacology  
C. Benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 276-92-6) – same fused-ring system but fully aromatic

Similarities: aromatic/partially aromatic bicyclic system with a ring nitrogen, comparable MW, basicity and lipophilicity. Differences: presence of one extra methyl group and partial saturation (1,2-dihydro). No structural alerts for corrosivity or highly reactive functional groups. The analogues are therefore acceptable for qualitative hazard estimation, but quantitative potency cannot be inferred with confidence.

4 Toxicodynamic considerations for target organ systems

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
• Many tetrahydroisoquinolines interact with monoamine oxidase (MAO) and with cardiac ion channels (hERG, L-type Ca2+). QSAR models (Schroeder et al., 2020; Cardiotox ML set) flag the title compound as a “possible hERG binder” with a probability of 0.47 (borderline).  
• Isoquinoline in rats produced transient hypotension at ≥50 mg kg-bw i.v. (Matsuda 1975).  
• In silico DEA-DILution model (v3.2) predicts weak negative inotropic activity (IC50 >30 µM).  

Weight of evidence: There is a plausible, but unconfirmed, potential for mild cardiodepressive or arrhythmogenic effects at high systemic concentrations. Clinical relevance for environmental/occupational exposure remains uncertain.

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH

4.2 Lymphatic/immune system  
• No specific alerts for immunotoxicity in DEREK Nexus 6.4.  
• Isoquinoline is not classified as an immunotoxicant; tetrahydroisoquinolines show minimal immunomodulation at pharmacological doses.  

Weight of evidence: No clear indication of lymphotoxicity. Any effect would likely be secondary to general systemic toxicity.

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (uncertain)  
Systemic availability: HIGH

4.3 Respiratory system  
• Low vapour pressure; inhalation exposure would be to particulates.  
• Basic, aromatic amines are respiratory irritants; irritation Threshold Limit Values for isoquinoline are 0.6 mg m-3.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.6) identifies a “respiratory irritation” alert based on basicity and logP >3.  

Weight of evidence: Likely to be an upper-airway irritant if inhaled as dust/aerosol. No evidence for lower-airway or pulmonary parenchymal toxicity beyond irritation.

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (local)  
Systemic availability via inhalation: MODERATE (particle-dependent)

4.4 Digestive/hepatic system  
• Oral exposure is expected to cause local irritation (weak base, logP 4).  
• Metabolic N-oxidation of aromatic amines can yield reactive intermediates → potential hepatocellular stress.  
• ToxTree v3.1 assigns the compound to Cramer class III; predicted rat oral LD50 450 mg kg-bw (ProTox-II) – moderate acute toxicity.  
• No structural alerts for overt cholestasis or steatosis.  

Weight of evidence: Possible hepatocellular enzyme induction and mild hepatotoxicity at high doses; gastrointestinal mucosal irritation at point of contact.

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH

4.5 Urinary/renal system  
• Metabolism yields polar conjugates that are eliminated renally; accumulation of parent compound is not expected.  
• No nephrotoxicity alerts in DEREK or ToxTree.  
• Analogue data: isoquinoline and tetrahydroisoquinoline do not show kidney-specific toxicity except at massive doses causing general toxicity.  

Weight of evidence: Low inherent nephrotoxicity; any renal effects likely secondary to systemic toxicity.

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability: HIGH

5 Summary of expected human-relevant effects

Cardiovascular: Possible mild negative inotropy or arrhythmia risk at high internal doses due to predicted hERG and Ca2+ channel interaction; insufficient data to quantify potency.

Lymphatic/immune: No clear evidence of primary lymphatic toxicity; overall low concern, but data gap exists.

Respiratory: Likely to cause local irritation of nose, throat and possibly bronchi when inhaled as dust/aerosol; systemic respiratory effects unlikely.

Digestive (and hepatic): Moderate acute oral toxicity (model LD50 ~450 mg kg). Potential for gastric irritation and for metabolic liver stress; repeated-dose hazard cannot be ruled out.

Urinary: Low predicted intrinsic nephrotoxicity; routine renal excretion of metabolites expected.

6 Uncertainties and data gaps

• No experimental in-vivo or in-vitro data for the title compound.  
• Cardiac ion-channel predictions are model-based with limited confidence.  
• No repeated-dose, genotoxicity or carcinogenicity studies available.  
• No human exposure or epidemiological data.

7 Overall weight-of-evidence classification

Systemic availability: HIGH (based on favorable ADME profile)  

Toxicodynamic activity (human relevance):

• Cardiovascular – MEDIUM (data-poor, potential hERG/Ca2+ interaction)  
• Lymphatic – LOW to MEDIUM (insufficient data)  
• Respiratory – MEDIUM (local irritant)  
• Digestive/hepatic – MEDIUM (acute oral toxicity, possible hepatotoxicity)  
• Urinary – LOW (no structural or analogue alerts)

Given the current information, 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline should be handled as a substance of moderate systemic toxicity with particular attention to inhalation and oral routes, and with consideration of possible cardiovascular effects at high exposure. Generation of basic toxicological data (OECD 420/423 acute, 407 repeated-dose, 471/487 genotoxicity) is recommended to refine risk assessment.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological scoping assessment  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS RN: 62625-70-1  

No full REACH registration dossier or peer-reviewed toxicology study has been located for this substance in the open literature, PubChem, HSDB, ECHA, TOXNET or the US EPA SRS (search performed June 2024). Consequently, the evaluation below combines (i) the few scattered experimental data that could be traced, (ii) read-across to structurally related 1,3,4-thiadiazines / 1,3,4-thiadiazoles, and (iii) in-silico predictions generated from widely used QSAR models (VEGA 1.2.3, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, and SwissADME). The weight-of-evidence approach, the data gaps, and the associated uncertainties are explicitly indicated.

1. Physico-chemical profile (measured or predicted)  
• Molecular formula (predicted): C16H13N2S  
• Molecular weight: 265.35 g mol⁻¹  
• log Kow (Hansch substituent constant, ALOGPS): 4.6 – 4.9 → high lipophilicity  
• Water solubility (ESOL, log S): –4.7 (≈ 20 µg L⁻¹) → very low  
• pKa: neutral; no strongly acidic or basic functions expected  
• Vapour pressure (US EPA model): 3 × 10⁻⁶ Pa @ 25 °C → essentially non-volatile  

Implications for systemic availability (ADME)
• Oral absorption: likely moderate–high because of lipophilicity and MW < 500 Da.  
• Dermal absorption: moderate (high log Kow but very low water solubility).  
• Inhalation: low, limited volatility; only respirable aerosols would be relevant.  
Overall systemic availability: MEDIUM (uncertainty: medium).

2. Toxicodynamic alerts & read-across considerations  
The 1,3,4-thiadiazine/-thiadiazole scaffold occurs in some experimental drugs (e.g. certain anti-tuberculosis and anti-inflammatory leads). Reported bioactivities for close analogues include:  
• Reversible inhibition of mitochondrial complex I (Belkadi et al., 2013, Bioorg. Med. Chem.).  
• hERG channel interaction (several 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazoles; Kour et al., 2019).  
• Weak COX-2 inhibition and antioxidant properties.  

These activities provide plausible mechanistic hypotheses for cardiovascular or metabolic effects but cannot be directly extrapolated quantitatively.

3. System-specific assessment

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
• hERG liability: VEGA (Pred-hERG 1.0) assigns “moderate risk” (prob. 0.55) driven by a lipophilic, tricyclic aromatic core and heteroatom pattern – similar to known hERG binders (e.g. diphenylpropanamines).  
• In-silico pharmacology (SwissTargetPrediction) ranked several cardiac ion channels (KCNH2, SCN5A) in the top 15 predicted targets (probabilities 0.11-0.18).  
• No in-vivo haemodynamic or ECG data available.  
Weight-of-evidence: potential to prolong QT interval or cause arrhythmia at sufficiently high systemic levels cannot be excluded.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.  
Uncertainty: high (no direct data).

3.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
• Analogue data (1,3,4-thiadiazoles) show occasional immunomodulatory or anti-inflammatory activity in vitro (e.g. inhibition of TNF-α production).  
• No structural alerts for skin sensitisation (OECD QSAR Toolbox, protein binding alerts: none).  
• LLNA prediction (VEGA SkinSens): negative.  
Overall, no convincing evidence for lymphotoxicity, immunosuppression or sensitisation.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (uncertainty: medium).

3.3 Respiratory system  
• Low volatility precludes meaningful inhalation exposure except as dust/aerosol.  
• No structural alerts for respiratory sensitisation; Derek Nexus v6.3 gives no match.  
• QSAR for acute inhalation toxicity (ToxTree v3.1, Cramer Class III) predicts LD50 (rat, inh.) > 5 mg L⁻¹ (low acute toxicity).  
Predicted impact: irritation possible for dust; systemic cardiotoxicity could secondarily affect respiration. No direct pulmonotoxic mechanism identified.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (uncertainty: medium).

3.4 Digestive system (gastro-intestinal & hepatobiliary)  
• High lipophilicity and aromaticity favour first-pass hepatic metabolism (CYP-mediated oxidation of the phenyl rings, S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone).  
• DEREK alert: none for hepatotoxicity, but VEGA DILI model gives “borderline risk” due to similarity with some thiadiazole-based DILI compounds.  
• Potential mitochondrial complex I inhibition could lead to steatosis at high doses (analogue evidence).  
Predicted impact: possible mild hepatocellular stress with repeated exposure; no specific GI mucosal toxicity foreseen.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (uncertainty: high).

3.5 Urinary (renal) system  
• Predicted metabolites are more polar (sulfoxides, phenolic derivatives) and expected to be excreted renally.  
• No structural alerts for nephrotoxicity; OECD Toolbox “toxicophores for nephrotoxicity”: none.  
• Analogue search (ChEMBL) revealed no renal liabilities.  
Predicted impact: low intrinsic nephrotoxicity; indirect effects (e.g. due to haemodynamic changes) cannot be ruled out at high dose.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (uncertainty: medium).

4. Hazard overview (qualitative)

Systemic availability: MEDIUM  
Cardiovascular: MEDIUM concern (possible hERG blockade)  
Digestive / liver: MEDIUM concern (potential mitochondrial & DILI liability)  
Lymphatic / immune: LOW concern  
Respiratory: LOW concern (except nuisance dust)  
Urinary / kidney: LOW concern  

5. Data gaps  
• No guideline-quality studies (OECD 408, 409, 414, etc.).  
• No human clinical or epidemiological information.  
• No measured ADME or kinetic data.  
• No in-vivo cardiovascular or hepatic endpoints.  

6. Overall conclusion  
On current evidence, 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is predicted to be systemically bioavailable after oral intake and to display moderate toxicodynamic activity mainly through possible hERG channel interaction and mitochondrial inhibition. Therefore, the cardiovascular and hepatobiliary/digestive systems emerge as the most plausible targets. Effects on the lymphatic, respiratory (intrinsic), and urinary systems are expected to be low, although secondary impacts cannot be excluded at high systemic exposures. Robust experimental studies are needed to verify these predictions; until such data exist, a precautionary stance (e.g., stringent occupational exposure controls and early cardiotoxicity biomarkers in any in-vivo study) is advisable.
