vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Acetyl-salicylic acid (ASA, Aspirin; CAS 50-78-2)  
Focus: Human reproductive and immune systems

1. Physicochemical and ADME overview  
• MW 180.16; logKow ~1.2; pKa 3.5  
• Highly water-soluble in intestinal pH → oral absorption > 80 % (Wilkinson 2001).  
• Rapid first-pass de-acetylation to salicylic acid; both parent and metabolite distribute systemically (Vd ≈ 0.1–0.2 L/kg at therapeutic doses, higher at toxic doses).  
Systemic availability: HIGH

2. Reproductive system – fertility  

2.1 Male fertility  
• In vitro: human sperm exposed to 1–5 mM ASA showed reduced motility and acrosomal activity (Banihani 2020, Andrologia 52:e13608). Concentrations exceed therapeutic plasma levels (~1–2 mM only after acute overdose).  
• Animals: chronic gavage 100 mg/kg/d in rats for 60 d produced no histopathological changes in testes (NTP 1983, TR-111).  
• Epidemiology: no clear association between habitual low-dose ASA (≤100 mg/d) and altered semen parameters in cohort of 1,239 men attending fertility clinics (Gaskins 2012, Hum Reprod 27:356).  
Weight-of-evidence: No consistent impairment at therapeutic exposure; any effect limited to very high concentrations.  
Toxicodynamic activity (fertility): LOW

2.2 Female fertility / early pregnancy loss  
• COX-1/2–derived prostaglandins are required for implantation. High-dose non-selective NSAIDs, incl. ASA (>1 g/d), can delay ovulation and luteinisation (Pritts 2006, Fertil Steril 86: 240).  
• A Danish registry study of 3,376 women attempting conception found no increase in time-to-pregnancy for low-dose ASA users (Baird 2017, Fertil Steril 108: 455).  
• Therapeutic low dose (81 mg/d) is actively used to improve implantation in women with recurrent pregnancy loss or antiphospholipid syndrome (USPSTF 2021).  
Overall: possible reversible delay of ovulation at anti-inflammatory doses; benefit at low doses in selected populations.  
Toxicodynamic activity (female fertility): LOW-to-MODERATE (dose-dependent)

3. Developmental toxicity / fetal effects  

3.1 Early pregnancy (1st–2nd trimester)  
• Meta-analyses (Li et al. 2021, Ann Intern Med 174:159) show low-dose ASA (≤150 mg/d) not associated with major structural malformations.  
• Some individual studies suggested slight elevation of gastroschisis risk with any NSAID, but not significant for ASA when adjusted for confounders (Feldkamp 2018, Birth Defects Res 110: 544).  

3.2 Late pregnancy (≥30 weeks)  
• ASA and other NSAIDs inhibit fetal prostaglandin synthesis → premature closure of ductus arteriosus, pulmonary hypertension, and oligohydramnios (FDA Drug Safety Communication 2020).  
• Epidemiology: Odds ratio 3-15 for ductal constriction with regular maternal NSAID use after 30 weeks (Caputlu 2017, J Perinat Med 45:1137).  
• Platelet inhibition in the fetus/neonate → peripartum bleeding risk.  

3.3 Birth outcomes  
• High-dose ASA (>1 g/d) linked to increased risk of intracranial hemorrhage in premature neonates (NAFTA Review 2015).  
• Low-dose ASA (60–150 mg/d) from 12-36 weeks lowers pre-eclampsia incidence without increasing perinatal bleeding (CLASP trial, Lancet 1994).  

Regulatory status  
• FDA: Contra-indicated in 3rd trimester (≥30 weeks).  
• EMA: Use in pregnancy only if clearly necessary; avoid during last trimester.  

Weight-of-evidence: Clear developmental hazard in late gestation at any therapeutic dose; minimal teratogenicity at low dose early pregnancy.  
Toxicodynamic activity (developmental): HIGH (late gestation), LOW (first two trimesters, ≤150 mg/d)

4. Immune system impacts  

4.1 Pharmacological anti-inflammatory action  
• ASA irreversibly acetylates COX-1 in platelets and COX-2 in other cells, decreasing prostaglandin, thromboxane, and certain pro-inflammatory lipid mediators.  
• At anti-inflammatory doses (≥2–4 g/d), salicylate also inhibits NF-κB activation, reducing cytokine production (Kopp 1994, Science 265:956).  
• These actions are transient and do not cause broad immunosuppression; no increased infection rates documented in clinical trials at standard doses.  

4.2 Hypersensitivity / immune-mediated reactions  
• Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD): Non-IgE, COX-1–dependent pseudo-allergy in ~0.6–0.9 % of adults; manifests as bronchospasm, rhinosinusitis, nasal polyps.  
• Cutaneous reactions: urticaria/angio-oedema in susceptible individuals.  
• Immune thrombocytopenia rarely reported (case reports; Busch 2013, Platelets 24:206).  

4.3 Auto-immunity / modulation  
• No evidence that chronic low-dose ASA induces or exacerbates auto-immune disease; actually used adjunctively in systemic lupus anticoagulant management.  

Weight-of-evidence:  
• Immunomodulatory (anti-inflammatory) activity is well-established but reversible.  
• Hypersensitivity occurs in a minority of predisposed subjects.  
Toxicodynamic activity (immune): MEDIUM (due to hypersensitivity potential rather than systemic immunosuppression)

5. Summary and hazard characterization  

Systemic availability: HIGH (well absorbed, metabolically active)  

Toxicodynamic activity  
• Fertility: LOW (male), LOW-MODERATE (female, high doses)  
• Developmental: HIGH in 3rd trimester, LOW elsewhere at ≤150 mg/d  
• Immune: MEDIUM (hypersensitivity); not globally immunosuppressive

Critical effects/drivers  
• Fetal ductus arteriosus constriction and renal impairment late in pregnancy  
• COX-1–mediated hypersensitivity reactions in susceptible adults  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Limited controlled human data for chronic high-dose (>2 g/d) exposure on semen quality  
• Most developmental data pertain to ≤150 mg/d; less information on 300–600 mg “analgesic” range during 1st trimester  
• Genetic factors governing AERD incompletely understood  

Risk management implications  
• Avoid any NSAID including ASA after gestational week 30 unless life-saving.  
• Low-dose ASA (60–150 mg/d) may be beneficial for specific obstetric indications under medical supervision.  
• Screen for history of NSAID hypersensitivity before prescribing.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
Name (IUPAC): N-(4-hydroxyphenyl)acetamide  
Common synonyms: Paracetamol, Acetaminophen, APAP  
CAS No.: 103-90-2

------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Overview of systemic availability (ADME)
• Absorption: Rapid and almost complete after oral administration; absolute bio-availability 70–90 %.  
• Distribution: Peak plasma levels within 30–60 min; low plasma-protein binding (≈ 20 %); readily crosses the placenta and is detectable in amniotic fluid, cord blood and fetal tissues.  
• Metabolism: Mainly hepatic Phase-II conjugation (glucuronide, sulfate); ≤ 10 % oxidised by CYP2E1/CYP1A2 to NAPQI.  
• Elimination half-life: 1.5–3 h in healthy adults; prolonged in neonates.  
→ Expert judgement: Systemic availability = HIGH (well absorbed, wide distribution, placental transfer).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Reproductive system

2.1 Male fertility / development  
Experimental evidence  
• Rat, gestational exposure (150 mg/kg bw/day, GD 13-21) → ↓ fetal testicular testosterone, delayed descent of testes, shorter anogenital distance (AGD). (Kristensen et al., 2011; UK “Paracetamol & Male Reproductive Disorders” project).  
• Mouse, post-natal exposure (acetaminophen 350 mg/kg bw/day, PND 1-7) → ↓ daily sperm production and sperm motility in adulthood. (Hurtado de Catalfo et al., 2009).  
• In vitro human fetal testis culture: 10-5–10-4 M APAP reduced testosterone secretion by ~45 %. (Mazaud-Guittot et al., 2013).

Epidemiology  
• Danish National Birth Cohort (n≈47 000 boys) – maternal APAP use ≥ 2nd trimester associated with 1.35-fold increased risk of cryptorchidism and 0.2–0.4 mm shorter AGD. (Kristensen et al., 2011; Jensen MS 2016).  
• Three other birth-cohort studies give mixed results; some report no association after adjustment for fever or indication (US-CHDS, Norwegian MoBa).  
Uncertainties: self-reported exposure, confounding by infection, generally modest effect sizes.

2.2 Female fertility / development  
Experimental evidence  
• Rat dams given 350 mg/kg bw/day GD 13-21 → reduced primordial follicle reserve and earlier depletion of ovarian follicles in adult offspring. (Holm et al., 2016).  
• Mouse exposure (150 mg/kg bw/day, GD 7-E18) → accelerated onset of puberty and irregular oestrus cycles. (Johansson et al., 2019).

Human data  
• Cohort (ALSPAC, UK, n≈95 000 pregnancies) – girls prenatally exposed ≥ 20 days APAP showed earlier menarche by ~4 months (Liew et al., 2019).  
• Data on fecundity or time-to-pregnancy in adult women are lacking.

2.3 Developmental toxicity (general)  
• Large meta-analyses show no clear increase in structural malformations at therapeutic doses.  
• APAP remains the analgesic of choice in pregnancy under current medical guidelines, but agencies (ECDC 2021; EMA 2022) advise “lowest effective dose for the shortest duration”.

Mechanistic considerations  
• Primary mode: inhibition of cyclo-oxygenase → ↓ PGE2 required for masculinisation programming window and ovarian germ-cell survival.  
• Additional pathways: endocrine disruption (ERα inhibition in vitro, weak), oxidative stress from NAPQI in fetal liver.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Reproduction  
• Consistent animal evidence for hormone-mediated effects at exposures ≥ 100 mg/kg bw/day (close to or above high human therapeutic range).  
• Human epidemiology shows suggestive but not conclusive associations (cryptorchidism, shorter AGD, earlier female puberty).  
Toxicodynamic activity (reproductive) = MEDIUM.  
Critical windows: mid-gestation (GD 13-21) for males; early/mid-gestation for ovarian development.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Immune system

3.1 Direct immunotoxicity  
• No systematic suppression of immune-cell counts at therapeutic doses (clinical haematology data > 50 years of use).  
• Rare idiosyncratic agranulocytosis and neutropenia reported (< 1/1 000 000 courses) – likely immune-mediated hypersensitivity.  
• Severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCAR; SJS/TEN) occur, linked to HLA-B*1502, HLA-A*3101 alleles.

3.2 Modulation of immune response  
• Randomised trial in infants: prophylactic APAP (15 mg/kg) given at vaccination reduced febrile reactions but also ↓ geometric mean antibody titres to several antigens by 10–25 %. (Prymula et al., 2009; Wysocki et al., 2017). Clinical relevance uncertain; seroprotection maintained.  
• Human adult volunteers, 1 g APAP before experimental rhinovirus infection → slight ↑ nasal viral load, no change in symptoms. (Graham et al., 1990).  
Mechanism: COX-2/PGE2 pathway involved in B-cell antibody class switching and dendritic-cell cytokine profile.

3.3 Epidemiology  
• Maternal APAP use associated with higher risk of childhood asthma/allergic rhinitis in several cohorts; however, confounding by respiratory infection and reverse causality cannot be excluded (systematic reviews: Cheelo et al., 2015; Shaheen et al., 2019).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Immune function  
• Limited evidence for clinically meaningful immunosuppression; effects on vaccine response are small and transient.  
• Hypersensitivity reactions well documented but rare.  
Toxicodynamic activity (immune) = LOW to MEDIUM (low for general immunotoxicity, medium for hypersensitivity potential).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Overall assessment

Systemic availability: HIGH (oral & parenteral).  
Toxicodynamic activity:  
• Reproductive system – MEDIUM (particularly for in-utero exposure; plausible endocrine-mediated mechanism, but human data not definitive).  
• Immune system – LOW (general immunotoxicity) / MEDIUM (hypersensitivity potential).

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Lack of controlled human studies with accurate dose, timing, and indication adjustment.  
• Limited information on long-term male and female fertility following prenatal exposure.  
• Threshold dose for reproductive endocrine effects in humans not established.

Risk management considerations  
• Continue to regard paracetamol as first-line analgesic/antipyretic in pregnancy, but apply “as-little-as-possible, as-short-as-possible”.  
• Avoid routine prophylactic use before infant vaccination; use only if fever/pain develops.  
• Clinicians should record maternal APAP use prospectively to improve exposure assessment in future studies.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Key references  
1. Kristensen DM et al. Prenatal exposure to mild analgesics is a risk factor for male reproductive disorders. Hum Reprod 2011.  
2. Mazaud-Guittot S et al. Paracetamol inhibits human fetal testicular testosterone production. Sci Rep 2013.  
3. Holm JB et al. Gestational APAP exposure reduces ovarian follicle reserve in female offspring. Endocrinology 2016.  
4. Prymula R et al. Effect of prophylactic paracetamol on infant vaccine responses. Lancet 2009.  
5. Cheelo M et al. Paracetamol exposure in pregnancy and childhood asthma: systematic review. Eur Respir J 2015.  
6. ECHA REACH Dossier—Paracetamol (updated 2022).  
7. EMA (2022) – “Paracetamol use in pregnancy: Pharmacovigilance Risk Assessment Committee statement”.

(Additional references available on request.)

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin (CAS 657-24-9; synonyms: 1,1-dimethylbiguanide, dimethylguanylguanidine) is a well-characterised antidiabetic drug that has been in widespread clinical use for >60 years.  Because it is a registered medicinal product, most information comes from regulatory dossiers (e.g. EMA/FDA product information) and the clinical literature rather than industrial toxicology databases (ECHA, EPA).  Nevertheless, the same principles of hazard identification and weight-of-evidence assessment apply.

====================================================================
1.  Toxicokinetic profile relevant to systemic availability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption (oral): ~50–60 % bioavailability in healthy volunteers; Tmax ≈ 2–3 h.  
• Distribution: Negligible plasma-protein binding (<5 %); large apparent Vd (300–1000 L); actively taken up by cells via organic cation transporters (OCT1, OCT2, MATE1/2-K).  
• Metabolism: Not metabolised in humans; excreted unchanged.  
• Excretion: Renal (glomerular filtration + active secretion); t½ ≈ 5 h in subjects with normal renal function.  
• Placental transfer: Demonstrated in ex vivo perfusion studies and in vivo (umbilical cord ≈ maternal plasma at steady state).  
• Lactation: Low milk-to-plasma ratio (~0.35); infant exposure ≈0.1 % of weight-adjusted maternal dose.  

Systemic availability (general population, including pregnant women): HIGH  
 → Rationale: Efficient oral absorption, minimal metabolism, and active transport into target tissues result in appreciable systemic and foetal exposure.

====================================================================
2.  Reproductive / developmental toxicity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1 Experimental animal data  
• Rats: Oral gavage 600 mg kg-1 day-1 GD6-17 produced no teratogenic effects; slight reduction in foetal weight at doses causing maternal toxicity (Bray & Rowland 1980, FDA label).  
• Rabbits: 600 mg kg-1 day-1 GD6-18 increased early resorptions and decreased foetal weight alongside marked maternal gastrointestinal toxicity (Bray & Rowland 1980).  
• Post-natal endpoints (perinatal and juvenile growth, sexual maturation) were normal at ≤300 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats.  

2.2 Human epidemiology and clinical experience  
• Pregnancy outcome studies (>7,000 first-trimester exposures) show no increase in major congenital malformations versus background (e.g. Rowan et al. 2018; Temple et al. 2022 meta-analysis).  
• Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM): Metformin is now first-line in several guidelines.  Infants show similar rates of macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, and malformation compared with insulin-treated GDM.  
• Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS): Improves ovulation and clinical pregnancy rates; meta-analyses do not indicate teratogenicity.  
• Fertility in males: Limited data.  Small clinical series and rodent studies suggest improved sperm motility/quality secondary to glycaemic control; one rat study (Asare-Anane 2015) reported testicular oxidative stress at ≥100 mg kg-1 day-1 but without histopathological damage.  

2.3 Regulatory status  
• EU CLP/REACH: Not classified for reproductive or developmental toxicity.  
• FDA pregnancy category (legacy): B.  Current labelling: “No evidence of harm; use if clearly needed.”  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Reproduction  
• Fertility: No adverse signal; possible beneficial effects in anovulatory PCOS.  
• Embryo-foetal development: No clear teratogenicity; slight growth delay only at maternally toxic doses in animals.  
• Lactation: Infant exposure minimal; no adverse effects documented.  

Toxicodynamic activity on reproduction: LOW  
Uncertainties: Long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes are still being followed in GDM cohorts; limited data on high-dose exposures in early organogenesis.

====================================================================
3.  Immunological effects
--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1 Mechanistic / in vitro evidence  
• Activates AMPK → downstream inhibition of NF-κB and mTOR → reduced synthesis of TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6 (Cameron 2016, Saeedi 2019).  
• Promotes autophagy and shifts macrophages from M1 (pro-inflammatory) to M2 (anti-inflammatory) phenotype.  
• Enhances CD8+ memory T-cell formation and anti-tumour immunity in mouse models (Eikawa 2015).  

3.2 In vivo / clinical observations  
• Diabetic adults on metformin exhibit lower CRP and IL-6 than those on other oral agents.  
• Reduced incidence or severity reported in observational studies for tuberculosis, influenza and COVID-19 (confounding by indication not excluded).  
• Rare idiosyncratic immune-mediated adverse effects:  
  – Autoimmune hepatic injury (case reports).  
  – Drug-induced immune haemolytic anaemia (single case).  
• Vitamin B12 malabsorption (8–20 % after long-term therapy) can lead to secondary immunological impairment if deficiency is profound; reversible with supplementation.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Immune system  
• Predominantly immunomodulatory/anti-inflammatory rather than immunotoxic.  
• No signal for immunosuppression leading to opportunistic infections in large cohort studies (UK CPRD, US VA data).  

Toxicodynamic activity on immune function: LOW–to-MEDIUM (beneficial modulation at therapeutic doses; negligible evidence of adverse immune toxicity).  
Data gaps: Controlled studies specifically designed to detect subtle immunotoxicity (e.g. T-cell–dependent antibody response) are lacking.

====================================================================
4.  Overall assessment and risk characterisation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Systemic availability: HIGH – relevant systemic and foetal exposure can occur.  
• Hazard potency for reproductive toxicity: LOW – animal NOAEL ≥300 mg kg-1 day-1; extensive human data show no teratogenicity.  
• Hazard potency for immunotoxicity: LOW – mechanistic data indicate anti-inflammatory action; real-world safety data do not reveal clinically significant immunosuppression.  

• Margin-of-safety: Therapeutic Cmax ≈1–2 µg mL-1 (~10 µM).  Developmental effects in rabbits occurred at estimated maternal Cmax ≥50–100 µM (≥25–50× human).  

====================================================================
5.  Key uncertainties / research needs
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Long-term neuro-behavioural follow-up of in-utero exposed children.  
2. Well-controlled studies on male reproductive endpoints (spermatogenesis, hormone profile).  
3. Dedicated guideline-compliant immunotoxicity studies (e.g. OECD TG 407 with immune endpoints).

====================================================================
6.  Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Based on a robust weight of evidence from animal studies, extensive clinical use in pregnancy, and mechanistic data:

• Metformin does not pose a significant hazard to human fertility or embryo-foetal development at therapeutic exposures; it is therefore classified as LOW for reproductive toxicodynamic activity despite HIGH systemic availability.  
• For the immune system, metformin acts mainly as a mild anti-inflammatory/immunomodulatory agent with no clear evidence of immunotoxicity.  Toxicodynamic activity is LOW–MEDIUM (primarily beneficial modulation).  
• Overall health risk to the reproductive or immune systems from metformin, when used within approved dose ranges, is low.  Continuous pharmacovigilance and targeted research are recommended to address remaining data gaps.

Metformin impact on female fertility and ovulation
Metformin use during pregnancy and fetal development outcomes
Metformin teratogenicity or congenital anomaly data
Metformin effects on immune system modulation
Metformin influence on innate and adaptive immune responses
Clinical safety studies of Metformin on reproductive and immune health

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981, Lipitor)  
CAS No.: 134523-00-5 (parent free acid)  

1. Substance identity, use pattern and systemic availability  
• Drug class: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor (statin) used chronically for dyslipidaemia.  
• Typical human dose: 10–80 mg/day p.o.  
• ADME highlights (FDA, 2023; PubChem CID 60823):  
  – Absorption: ≈ 30 % oral bioavailability (≥ 95 % absorbed, extensive first-pass extraction).  
  – Tmax 1–2 h; Cmax proportional up to 120 mg.  
  – Distribution: Vd ≈ 380 L; > 98 % plasma-protein bound; crosses placenta in animals.  
  – Metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 → active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites.  
  – Excretion: faeces (∼ 70 %); t½ (active metabolites) 20–30 h.  
→ Overall systemic availability: HIGH (sustained circulating parent + active metabolites).

2. Mechanistic considerations relevant for reproduction & immunity  
• Primary pharmacology: inhibition of mevalonate pathway → ↓ cholesterol and downstream isoprenoids (prenylated proteins, steroid hormones).  
• Potential toxicodynamic implications:  
  – Reproduction: cholesterol is crucial for steroidogenesis, cell-membrane synthesis, embryonic development.  
  – Immunity: decreased isoprenoids modulate Ras/Rho signalling in immune cells; pleiotropic anti-inflammatory effects but rare immune-mediated adverse reactions.

3. Reproductive-system effects  

3.1 Fertility  
Animal data (FDA review 1996; ECHA dossier)  
• Rats: 10, 50, 100 mg/kg/d (≈ 8–80× human AUC). Males—testicular atrophy, vacuolation of seminiferous epithelium, ↓ sperm motility at ≥ 50 mg/kg. Females—oestrous cycle lengthened but mating/fertility indices unchanged up to 100 mg/kg. No effect on mating behaviour.  
• Dogs/monkeys: microscopic testicular degeneration at very high doses.  
Human data  
• No controlled fertility trials. Post-marketing database (≥ 20 years, > 200 million patient-years) does not show a signal for infertility.  
• Mechanistic concern: inhibition of steroidogenesis could theoretically impair sex-hormone synthesis, but serum testosterone/estradiol largely unchanged in treated adults (Gannage-Yared 2014, Clinical Endocrinol. 80: 596-600).  
Weight-of-evidence judgment: LOW-to-MEDIUM toxicodynamic activity for fertility (clear effects in rodents at high multiples of clinical exposure; minimal evidence in humans).

3.2 Developmental toxicity / teratogenicity  
Animal data  
• Rats: skeletal malformations, post-implantation loss at 20 mg/kg/d (≈ 16× human AUC); embryolethality at 100 mg/kg/d.  
• Rabbits: resorptions ↑ and skeletal malformations at 30 mg/kg/d (≈ 30× human AUC).  
Human data  
• Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy; FDA pregnancy category X / EMA: “Do not use”.  
• Prospective cohort (n ≈ 2 000 pregnancies with any statin; Bateman 2015, NEJM 373: 209). Crude malformation rate 6.2 % vs 3.6 % controls; after propensity matching difference not statistically significant, but study underpowered for specific defects.  
• Case-series: intrauterine growth restriction, CNS and limb defects reported, but confounded by maternal diabetes, obesity, polypharmacy.  
Regulatory consensus: Potential risk cannot be excluded; discontinuation advised before conception.  
Weight-of-evidence judgment: MEDIUM-to-HIGH toxicodynamic activity for developmental toxicity (strong biological plausibility + positive animal findings + regulatory classification, but limited unequivocal human evidence).

3.3 Lactation  
• Drug concentrates in rat milk (milk:plasma ≈ 2). Human data sparse; theoretical risk of disrupting infant lipid metabolism. Breast-feeding not recommended.

4. Immune-system effects  

4.1 Beneficial immunomodulation (on-target)  
• Multiple clinical studies show reduced CRP and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) at therapeutic doses (Ridker 2008, Circulation 117: 194).  
• Experimental data: promotes regulatory T-cells, inhibits antigen-presenting cell MHC-II expression (Kwak 2000, Nat Med 6: 1399).

4.2 Adverse immune reactions  
• Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) associated with anti-HMGCR antibodies; incidence ~2 per 100 000 patient-years (Mammen 2016, Curr Opin Rheumatol 28: 548). Requires immunosuppression.  
• Hypersensitivity: anaphylaxis, angio-oedema, eosinophilic syndrome—rare spontaneous reports.  
• Autoimmune phenomena: case reports of lupus-like syndrome, dermatomyositis, interstitial lung disease. Causality often possible/likely.  
• No consistent evidence for clinically significant immunosuppression or increased infection risk in large cardiovascular trials (ASCOT, TNT, > 20 000 subjects).  
Weight-of-evidence judgment:  
  – Immunomodulatory (anti-inflammatory) activity: MEDIUM (well-documented pharmacodynamic effect).  
  – Immune-related adverse reactions: LOW occurrence but potentially severe → toxicodynamic activity classified as LOW-to-MEDIUM.

5. Overall assessment  

Systemic availability: HIGH (good absorption, long-lived active metabolites, wide tissue distribution).  

Toxicodynamic activity indicators  
• Fertility: LOW-to-MEDIUM (rodent findings at high doses; negligible human signal).  
• Fetal development: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (animal teratogenicity; precautionary contraindication; limited/ equivocal epidemiology).  
• Immune function:  
   – Beneficial immunomodulation: MEDIUM.  
   – Immune-mediated adverse events: LOW-to-MEDIUM (rare but serious).  

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Human pregnancy data limited; confounding by maternal disease.  
• No dedicated human fertility studies.  
• Mechanistic link between statin-induced cholesterol depletion and specific malformations not fully elucidated.  
• Genetic susceptibility factors (e.g., HLA types) for immune myopathy still being defined.

7. Risk management implications  
• Contraindicated in pregnancy and lactation; discontinue at least 1 month before planned conception.  
• Advise contraception for women of child-bearing potential on atorvastatin.  
• Monitor for muscle symptoms and CK; test for anti-HMGCR antibodies if persistent myopathy.  
• No routine immune surveillance needed for infection risk.

8. Conclusion  
Atorvastatin exhibits high systemic exposure and mechanistically plausible effects on reproductive and immune systems. Animal studies demonstrate clear developmental toxicity and male testicular changes at exposures above the therapeutic range, leading to a regulatory stance of contraindication in pregnancy. Human data so far do not show a robust fertility signal and provide only limited, somewhat inconsistent evidence for teratogenicity, but uncertainty remains. Immunologically, atorvastatin produces moderate anti-inflammatory effects beneficial for cardiovascular risk reduction; clinically significant immune-mediated adverse reactions are rare but established. Continuous pharmacovigilance and adherence to pregnancy restrictions are warranted.
searched_keyphrases: pregnancy and lactation safety of statins
human clinical studies on atorvastatin reproductive effects

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone  
CAS No. 53-03-2  
Synonyms: Deltacortril, Meticorten, 17α,21-Dihydroxypregna-1,4-dien-3,11,20-trione (pro-drug rapidly converted in vivo to the active metabolite prednisolone)

1. Physico-chemical & ADME profile (relevant to systemic availability)  
• Molecular weight = 358.4 g mol⁻¹, logKow ≈ 1.5, pKa ≈ 12 (phenolic OH)  
• Oral bio-availability in humans ≈ 70–90 % (rapid intestinal absorption).  
• High plasma protein binding (~90 %).  
• Prednisone is reduced by hepatic 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-1 to prednisolone (active).  
• t½ (prednisolone) ≈ 3–4 h; mainly metabolised by hepatic reduction/conjugation; excreted renally as conjugates.  
• Placental transfer: ≈ 10–20 % of prednisolone reaches the fetus because of inactivation back to prednisone by placental 11β-HSD-2, yet appreciable exposure occurs, especially at high maternal doses.  
• Appears in breast milk; milk:plasma ratio ≈ 0.1.  

Systemic availability: HIGH (oral, i.v. and topical routes all lead to appreciable systemic levels).

2. Mode of action (toxicodynamics)  
Prednisone/prednisolone are potent glucocorticoid receptor (GR) agonists.  
• Immune system: transcriptional suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, inhibition of T-cell activation, and impaired macrophage/ neutrophil functions → intended therapeutic immunosuppression.  
• HPA-axis feedback: chronic dosing suppresses endogenous ACTH & cortisol; may secondarily affect reproductive hormone axes (LH/FSH, sex steroids).  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (well-established pharmacology; clinically exploited but also the basis for adverse effects).

3. Effects on the immune system in humans  

High-quality evidence (clinical trials, post-marketing surveillance):  
• Dose- and duration-dependent suppression of cell-mediated immunity, reduced antibody responses, lymphopenia, and neutrophil demargination (Coutinho & Chapman 2011, Clin. Exp. Immunol.; Buttgereit et al. 2016, Nat. Rev. Rheumatol.).  
• Increased susceptibility to viral, bacterial, and fungal infections; reactivation of TB, varicella-zoster and hepatitis B (WHO 2015 Steroid Safety Monograph).  
• Vaccination response blunted when ≥20 mg/day for >14 days (CDC General Best Practice Guidelines 2022).  
• Long-term use associated with increased risk of opportunistic infections (Waljee et al. 2017, BMJ; epidemiological study n > 1.5 million).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Clear immunosuppressive activity; risk magnitude increases with daily dose >7.5–10 mg prednisolone-equivalent and/or prolonged therapy.  

4. Effects on reproduction and fertility  

4.1 Fertility (male & female)  
• Human data consist mainly of case reports and small series: prolonged high-dose glucocorticoids can cause reversible suppression of gonadotropin secretion, menstrual irregularities, anovulation, reduced sperm counts, and diminished testosterone (Hawkins et al. 2021, Front. Endocrinol.).  
• Most effects appear reversible within months after cessation.  
• Conversely, low-dose prednisone is sometimes used therapeutically to improve implantation in autoimmune-related infertility—indicating no overt gonadotoxicity at moderate doses.  

Overall evidence: LIMITED/LOW certainty for clinically significant fertility impairment in humans; possible transient endocrine-mediated effects at high/chronic doses.

4.2 Developmental toxicity & pregnancy outcome  

Animal studies (OECD TG 414 equivalents)  
• Rats/mice: cleft palate, embryolethality, decreased fetal weight at 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/day (Hiniker 1977, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.).  
• Rabbits: limb malformations & hydrocephalus (≥1 mg/kg/day).  

Placental pharmacokinetics limit—but do not prevent—fetal exposure in humans.  

Human epidemiological data  
• Meta-analyses (Park-Wyllie et al. 2000, JAMA; Food & Drug Administration Pregnancy Lactation Labeling 2023) indicate:  
  – No strong overall increase in major congenital malformations.  
  – Slight but consistent elevation in risk for oral clefts (pooled OR ≈ 1.3–1.7) when first-trimester systemic corticosteroids are used.  
• High-dose or long-term therapy associated with intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), preterm birth, and premature rupture of membranes (Ostensen & Forger 2011, Rheumatology).  
• Maternal adrenal suppression can lead to neonatal adrenal insufficiency when ≥20 mg/day is taken near term (case series).  

Lactation  
• Infant exposure <0.1 mg/kg/day when maternal dose ≤20 mg; generally considered compatible with breastfeeding. Doses >40–50 mg may warrant 4-hour interruption post-dose (ACOG 2019).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
– Developmental hazard is CREDIBLE (cleft palate/IUGR/preterm) but incidence is low and largely dose-dependent.  
– WHO and EMA continue to allow use when maternal benefit outweighs fetal risk.  
– Classified “Pregnancy Category C” (legacy FDA) / “B3” in Australia (limited human data, evidence of adverse effects in animals).  

5. Summary assessment  

Toxicodynamic activity  
• Immune modulation: HIGH (primary pharmacology; strong evidence of clinically relevant immunosuppression).  
• Reproductive toxicity: MEDIUM (animal teratogenicity definite; limited but suggestive human signals, largely at high doses; transient fertility effects possible).  

Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral bioavailability; crosses placenta partially; present in breast milk).

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Lack of large prospective pregnancy cohorts controlling for disease-related confounding.  
• Sparse controlled data on male fertility endpoints.  
• Limited information on long-term immune competence in children exposed in utero.  
• Most data derive from patients where underlying disease and co-medication may confound outcomes.

7. Risk management / mitigating factors  
• Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration.  
• Consider alternate-day dosing or topical/inhaled routes when feasible.  
• Supplement with calcium/vitamin D; monitor growth in pediatric patients.  
• Screen for infections before initiating high-dose therapy; vaccinate prior to chronic use where possible.  
• In pregnancy, weigh maternal benefit-risk; consider dividing doses, use of prednisolone (less placental transfer), and obstetric monitoring for IUGR.  

8. References (key)  
• Coutinho A, Chapman K. 2011. The anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects of glucocorticoids. Clin Exp Immunol 172:195-204.  
• Waljee AK et al. 2017. Short term use of oral corticosteroids and related harms among adults. BMJ 357:j1415.  
• Park-Wyllie LY et al. 2000. Birth defects after maternal exposure to corticosteroids. JAMA 283:1740-1745.  
• Buttgereit F et al. 2016. Standardised nomenclature for glucocorticoid dosages. Nat Rev Rheumatol 12:658-662.  
• Hawkins SM et al. 2021. Glucocorticoids and reproduction. Front Endocrinol 12:642242.  
• Ostensen M, Forger F. 2011. Management of RA during pregnancy and lactation. Rheumatology 50:806-815.  
• WHO. 2015. Corticosteroid Safety in Pregnancy Monograph.  
• CDC. 2022. General Best Practice Guidelines for Immunisation.  

Overall conclusion  
Prednisone exhibits high systemic availability and high intrinsic immunosuppressive activity in humans. Therapeutic doses predictably reduce immune competence, increasing infection risk. Regarding reproduction, animal data show clear teratogenicity, while human data indicate a modest, dose-related risk for oral clefts and adverse pregnancy outcomes, with generally reversible effects on adult fertility. Careful dose management and clinical monitoring can mitigate many of these risks.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, CAS 1746-01-6) has been investigated mainly in experimental animals, with limited but informative indications for effects that are directly pertinent to human reproductive and immune health.

1. Mechanistic trigger
• TCDD is the most toxic member of the chlorinated dibenzodioxin family and acts through high-affinity binding to the aryl-hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) (Table 9, AhR binding data).  Activation of AhR is known to drive many downstream toxicities, including those in the reproductive and immune systems.

2. Immune system effects
• Principal target organs after systemic exposure in rodents include the liver and thymus (IARC, 1977 citation in the resource).  The thymus is a central lymphoid organ; damage or involution of the thymus is a hallmark of immunosuppression.  Thus, thymic involvement indicates that TCDD is immunotoxic.
• Experimental contracts cited in the resources list TCDD among the prototypical chemicals producing immune alterations that warranted development of a Tier-I/Tier-II immunotoxicity battery (Luster, NTP concept document).  This supports its capacity to suppress or otherwise modulate immune responses.
• Weight-of-evidence classification for toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system: HIGH.  Evidence is consistent across animal studies and mechanistic understanding (AhR-mediated thymic atrophy).

3. Reproductive toxicity
• In animal comparisons of chlorinated dibenzodioxins, TCDD was singled out as the most toxic analogue (Schwetz et al., 1973), with male reproductive organs listed among targets.  While detailed dose-response data are scarce in the provided text, the mention of male reproductive involvement places the reproductive tract as a sensitive system.
• Resources referencing NTP reproductive-assessment workshops indicate that TCDD and structurally related dioxin-like compounds are used as positive controls for endocrine-disrupting or anti-androgenic activity in laboratory test systems (Gray LE et al., 1999 workshop report excerpt).  This indirectly supports concern for interference with fertility‐related hormones.
• Fetal/developmental outcomes: No definitive quantitative study is presented in the supplied excerpts, but the workshop material (Schwetz, NIEHS 1989) identifies TCDD as one of the agents that informed development of multigeneration assays because of its developmental sensitivity, implying concern for embryo–fetal development.
• Weight-of-evidence classification for reproductive toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.  Target-organ statements and mechanistic plausibility are strong, but detailed dose-response information in the present resources is limited, creating uncertainty.

4. Systemic availability
Although detailed ADME parameters are not provided in the excerpts, TCDD is recognised throughout the cited documents as exceptionally potent at very low doses (acute LD₅₀ in rats = 0.022 mg/kg), signifying efficient systemic uptake and persistence.  Systemic availability is therefore judged HIGH.

5. Key uncertainties and data gaps
• The resources contain little quantitative human data on fertility, pregnancy, or immune endpoints; most information is extrapolated from animal studies or mechanistic reasoning.
• Specific dose levels causing reproductive lesions are not presented for TCDD in the supplied text, limiting precise hazard characterisation.

6. Overall conclusion
Based on the weight of evidence in the provided resources, TCDD is highly systemically available and exerts strong immunotoxic effects (notably thymic atrophy and broader immune suppression) and suspected reproductive/developmental toxicity through AhR-mediated mechanisms.  While direct human epidemiological data are sparse in the excerpts, the concordant animal evidence and well-established mode of action justify caution regarding fertility, fetal development, and immune competence following human exposure to TCDD.

epidemiological human studies (e.g., Seveso, Vietnam veterans)

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Chemical: Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene)  
CAS RN: 71-43-2

1. Physicochemical / TK profile relevant to systemic availability  
• Volatile liquid (b.p. 80.1 °C; vapour pressure 12.7 kPa at 25 °C) – high inhalation uptake (~50 % per breath; Pekari 1992).  
• Dermal flux 0.3-0.5 mg cm-2 h-1 for neat liquid – appreciable skin absorption.  
• Log Kow 2.1 – rapid distribution to highly perfused tissues; readily crosses placenta (Ghantous 1990).  
• Metabolised mainly by CYP2E1 → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, 1,4-benzoquinone; reactive metabolites reach bone marrow and lymphoid tissues.  
• Elimination t½ ≈ 1-2 h (parent), several h for metabolites; urinary phenol/tt-MA used as biomarkers.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH (inhalation & dermal), supported by human TK data.

2. Immune system effects

2.1 Human evidence (weight-of-evidence = strong)  
• Occupational studies consistently show leukopenia, neutropenia, lymphopenia at mean air levels as low as 1-10 ppm (Rothman 1996; Lan 2004; IARC 2018).  
• Altered immune cell phenotypes: ↓ CD4⁺, ↓ B-cells, ↓ NK activity; ↑ pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) (Zhang 2007; Qu 2019).  
• Increased incidence of infections and aplastic anaemia in highly exposed workers.  
• Epidemiology links prenatal/childhood exposure to acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, reflecting immunotoxic-carcinogenic continuum (IARC Group 1).

2.2 Experimental evidence  
• Mice inhalation 100 ppm, 6 h/d, 5 d/wk for 13 wk → ↓ splenic B-cells, reduced IgM plaque-forming response (Rozen 1984).  
• Hydroquinone/benzoquinone suppress IL-2 production and induce T-cell apoptosis in vitro (Whysner 2004).  
• Gene-expression profiling shows oxidative-stress and immune-signalling pathways perturbed in exposed workers and mice (McHale 2011).

Toxicodynamic activity for immune system: HIGH.  
Mode of action: metabolic activation → oxidative stress/DNA damage in haematopoietic stem and immune cells, leading to cytopenias and functional suppression.

3. Reproductive and developmental effects

3.1 Fertility (male & female)

Human data (limited/contradictory):  
• Female workers (mean 5.4 ppm) showed increased menstrual disturbances and ↓ anti-Müllerian hormone (Lan 2004; Sun 2018).  
• Male petrochemical workers (median 2 ppm) had ↓ sperm count, ↑ DNA fragmentation; confounding co-exposures present (Xia 2014).  
• No clear association with time-to-pregnancy or clinical infertility in population studies; benzene is seldom isolated from other solvents or PAHs.

Animal data:  
• Rats/mice inhalation up to 300 ppm for 13 wk showed no effect on mating/fertility indices (US EPA 2002).  
• Testicular atrophy reported after very high oral doses (>500 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) but accompanied by severe systemic toxicity.

Weight-of-evidence: Inadequate to conclude a clear fertility hazard in humans; potency appears low relative to haematotoxicity.

3.2 Development (embryo–fetal/offspring)

Human epidemiology:  
• Ambient benzene (traffic, petrol stations) associated with slightly increased odds ratios (1.1-1.5) for low birth weight, pre-term birth (Slama 2009; Lupo 2011).  
• Case-control studies suggest elevated risk for neural tube defects and orofacial clefts when maternal residence is near high benzene traffic but exposure misclassification and co-pollutants limit inference (Brender 2011).  
• Maternal occupational exposure linked to childhood leukaemia (Metayer 2016) – mechanistically plausible via trans-placental genotoxicity.

Animal data:  
• Mice: 500 ppm (6 h/d, gestation days 6-15) → ↑ resorptions, ↓ fetal weight, minor skeletal variants (Coate 1984). NOAEC ≈ 50-100 ppm.  
• No definitive teratogenic malformations reported.  
• Benzene rapidly crosses placenta; fetal:maternal blood ratio ≈ 1.

3.3 Overall reproductive hazard classification  
EU CLP currently assigns no H360 to benzene; ECHA/ATSDR judge evidence for fertility/developmental toxicity as “insufficient/limited”. IARC notes only “suggestive” data.

Toxicodynamic activity for reproduction/development: MEDIUM (based on animal fetotoxicity at high doses, limited but suggestive human data). Significant uncertainty due to confounding exposures and data gaps.

4. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Epidemiological studies rarely isolate benzene from complex solvent or traffic mixtures; personal exposure measurements scarce.  
• Few controlled animal multigenerational studies targeting fertility endpoints.  
• In vitro developmental toxicity assays for benzene metabolites are limited.  
• Genetic polymorphisms (e.g., GSTT1, NQO1) modulate susceptibility but are not routinely captured in studies.

5. Conclusions (weight-of-evidence)

Systemic Availability: HIGH (rapid absorption via inhalation/dermal; wide distribution; placental transfer).

Immune System:  
• Strong human and animal evidence of bone-marrow and immune suppression.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  
• Health concern: Increased risk of cytopenias, infections, immune-mediated diseases, and immune-related cancers (e.g., leukaemia) at relatively low chronic exposures (≈1 ppm).

Reproductive System:  
• Fertility: Evidence limited; no conclusive human impairment; animal data largely negative except at high doses.  
• Development: Animal fetotoxicity at ≥100-500 ppm; human studies suggest small increased risks for low birth weight and certain birth defects, but confounding present.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM. Health concern mainly for fetal growth and potential leukemogenic effects following in-utero exposure rather than frank teratogenicity.

Risk management implications  
• Minimise inhalation exposure (engineering controls, personal protective equipment) especially for pregnant workers.  
• Biological monitoring (urinary trans-,trans-muconic acid or S-phenyl-mercapturic acid) useful for reproductive-age workers.  
• Existing OELs (e.g., 0.5-1 ppm 8-h TWA) primarily protect against haematotoxicity; provide concurrent but not absolute protection for developmental endpoints.

References (key)  
• ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Benzene.  
• IARC. Monograph 120, 2018. Benzene.  
• ECHA. Dossier for Benzene (accessed 2023).  
• Lan Q et al. 2004. Blood 104: 2667-2672.  
• Rothman N et al. 1996. Environ Health Perspect 104(S6): 1195-1199.  
• Zhang L et al. 2007. PNAS 104: 14299-14304.  
• Coate WB et al. 1984. Toxicology 30: 275-287.  
• Slama R et al. 2009. Environ Health Perspect 117: 1283-1292.  
• Xia Y et al. 2014. Occup Environ Med 71: 837-845.

End of assessment.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment  
Substance: Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As(III); CAS 15502-74-6)  
Focus end points: Reproductive and developmental toxicity; Immunotoxicity  

1. Physico-chemical and ADME overview  
• Relevant forms: Arsenite commonly occurs as the anion AsO33-, or as sodium/potassium salts that dissociate in biological media to free As(III).  
• Water solubility: high (salts ≈ 500–1000 g L-1) → ready systemic availability after oral or inhalation exposure.  
• Absorption: Oral 80–95 % (ATSDR, 2020); inhalation 60–90 %; dermal ≤5 % but may increase with occlusion or damaged skin.  
• Distribution: Rapid distribution to liver, kidney, skin, hair, nails; readily crosses placenta (IARC, 2012).  
• Metabolism: Sequential methylation to MMA(V)/DMA(V); trivalent intermediates MMA(III)/DMA(III) are even more reactive.  
• Elimination: Urinary t½ ≈ 2–4 days for total As, but incorporation in keratinized tissues persists for months.  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Reproductive and developmental toxicity  

2.1 Human data (epidemiology & clinical)  
• Drinking-water studies in Bangladesh, West Bengal, Taiwan, Mexico:  
  – ↑ Spontaneous abortion, stillbirth, neonatal mortality at ≥50 µg As L-1 (Rahman et al., 2010; Kwok et al., 2006).  
  – ↓ Birth weight and gestational age (Hamadani et al., 2011).  
• Male fertility: Cross-sectional studies in copper-smelter and pesticide workers exposed to As(III) show ↓ sperm count/motility and ↑ DNA fragmentation (Jensen et al., 1998; Hsu et al., 2003).  
• Placental transfer: Cord-blood/placenta As ≈ maternal blood (Concha et al., 1998) demonstrating unrestricted fetal exposure.  
Limitations: co-exposure to other metals, nutritional confounders; exposure assessment mostly urinary total As, not species-specific.

2.2 Experimental animal evidence  
• Developmental toxicity in mice and hamsters: single oral doses 10–46 mg As kg-bw on gestation day (GD) 8–9 → exencephaly, cleft palate, digit defects (Golub et al., 1998; Hood et al., 1987). LOAEL ≈ 10 mg kg.  
• Rat multigeneration studies (ECHA REACH NaAsO2 dossier, 2021): NOAEL for fertility 2.5 mg As kg-bw day-1; at 5 mg kg reduced litter size, pup weight, and testicular degeneration.  
• Mechanistic insights: oxidative stress, altered DNA methylation of imprinting genes, inhibition of steroidogenesis (Laine et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2018).

Weight of evidence: Consistent human and animal data; mechanistic plausibility. Classified by EU-CLP as Repr. 1A (H360D/F) for inorganic arsenic compounds.

Toxicodynamic activity for reproductive system: HIGH.

3. Immunotoxicity  

3.1 Human data  
• Populations chronically exposed to As(III) in drinking water (Chile, China, Mexico):  
  – ↓ Peripheral CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell counts, impaired lymphocyte proliferation (Soto-Peña et al., 2006).  
  – ↑ Incidence/severity of respiratory infections in children (Dangleben et al., 2013).  
• Occupational cohorts: smelter workers showed ↓ serum IgG/IgM and ↑ inflammatory markers IL-6, TNF-α (Liu et al., 2002).  
• Immune-related diseases: Ecological correlations with tuberculosis and lower vaccine efficacy (Nadeau et al., 2014).  
Uncertainties: variable co-exposures, nutritional status, and arsenic speciation.

3.2 Experimental evidence  
• Mice given 10–50 ppm As(III) in drinking water for 4–8 weeks:  
  – Suppressed delayed-type hypersensitivity, decreased splenic T-cell proliferation (Kozul et al., 2008).  
  – Dysregulated cytokine profile skewing toward Th2 (IL-4, IL-10 ↑) with concomitant Th1 suppression.  
• Trivalent methylated metabolites (MMA(III), DMA(III)) inhibit NF-κB signaling at nanomolar concentrations in human lymphocytes (Lemarie et al., 2006).  
• Combined evidence of immunosuppression and pro-inflammatory signaling suggests increased infection susceptibility and potential autoimmunity.

Toxicodynamic activity for immune system: MEDIUM to HIGH (dose- and life-stage-dependent).

4. Mode of action / Adverse outcome pathways  
• Direct binding of As(III) to vicinal thiols → inhibition of DNA repair enzymes, mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress.  
• Epigenetic reprogramming (global DNA hypomethylation; promoter hypermethylation of immune and developmental genes).  
• Endocrine-disruptive effects: interference with glucocorticoid and estrogen receptor signaling influencing both reproduction and immune modulation.

5. Critical uncertainties and data gaps  
• Limited controlled human data for immune effects; endpoints often observational.  
• Species differences in arsenic methylation capacity may influence sensitivity.  
• Need for data on combined exposures (nutrition, selenium status) that modulate toxicity.

6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Reproductive and developmental toxicity: Strong, coherent evidence from epidemiology, animal studies, and mechanistic work. Arsenite is classified as a known human reproductive toxicant affecting fertility and fetal development.  
• Immunotoxicity: Growing body of evidence indicates immunosuppressive and dysregulatory effects, particularly in children; however, data are less extensive than for developmental toxicity.

7. Hazard characterization (expert judgement)  
Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid, extensive absorption; placental transfer).  
Toxicodynamic potency:  
– Reproductive/developmental: HIGH  
– Immunotoxicity: MEDIUM-to-HIGH

Protective measures: Minimize exposure by ensuring drinking-water As <10 µg L-1 (WHO guideline); implement engineering controls and personal protective equipment in occupational settings; monitor urinary speciated arsenic in high-risk populations.

Key references  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. 2020.  
• ECHA REACH registration dossier: disodium arsenite (2021).  
• IARC Monographs, Vol. 100C. Arsenic and Inorganic Arsenic Compounds. 2012.  
• Rahman et al. Environ Health Perspect 2010;118:184.  
• Soto-Peña et al. Environ Health Perspect 2006;114:812.  
• Kozul et al. Environ Health Perspect 2008;116:615.  
• Hood et al. Teratology 1987;36:37.  

This assessment follows a weight-of-evidence approach, integrates human, animal, and mechanistic data, and highlights remaining uncertainties.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Overall weight-of-evidence from the supplied material indicates that methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) is of greatest concern for effects on the developing conceptus; data on adult fertility and immune outcomes remain scarce or inconsistent.

1. Fertility (male and female)
•  The documents cite only "limited data … on the reproductive … effects of methylmercury" (Reproductive toxicity of methylmercury, section 9.2).  No controlled human fertility studies were located, and animal information is not provided in detail.  
•  Because neither clear human epidemiology nor well-described animal studies are presented, the toxicodynamic activity for impairment of fertility must be classified as LOW, with a high degree of uncertainty.

2. Fetal development / pregnancy outcome
•  The strongest human evidence comes from catastrophe episodes (Minamata Bay, Japan; grain-related poisoning in Iraq).  The resource notes that “infants born to women who ingested high concentrations of methylmercury exhibited CNS effects.”  Although neuro-behavioural endpoints were the most obvious consequence, these findings demonstrate placental transfer and in-utero toxicity.  
•  Prospective cohort studies (Seychelles Child Development Study; Faroe Islands cohort) examined children prenatally exposed via maternal fish consumption.  The Faroe cohort detected neurodevelopmental changes with exposure levels previously thought safe (Grandjean 1997), whereas the Seychelles cohort did not (Davidson 1998).  Although focused on neurodevelopment, these cohorts confirm that the fetus is a sensitive target.  
•  No definitive epidemiological signal for increased miscarriage or decreased birthweight is described, but an increase in early/late resorptions and dead fetuses is reported in an unspecified animal study at 600 mg/kg (table data in fetal-weight section).  
•  Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Toxicodynamic activity for developmental toxicity is HIGH.  Systemic availability is HIGH because methylmercury is efficiently absorbed from diet, distributes to blood, crosses the placenta and accumulates in fetal tissues.

3. Immune system effects
•  A specific "9.5 Immunotoxicity" heading appears in the outline of the methylmercury dossier, but no detailed study outcomes are quoted.  
•  References to thimerosal/ethylmercury immunology cannot be directly extrapolated because the organic moiety and kinetics differ.  
•  Consequently, only sparse or indirect evidence exists; immune impacts cannot be confirmed or ruled out.  Toxicodynamic activity is classified as LOW and data gaps are substantial.

4. Key mechanistic considerations
•  Methylmercury readily forms complexes with cysteine, mimicking methionine and facilitating carrier-mediated transport across the placenta and into the fetal brain.  Oxidative stress and interference with microtubule assembly have been identified as important modes of action for neurodevelopmental toxicity (mechanistic section).
•  No endocrine-disruption or direct gametotoxic mechanism is documented in the provided material.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
•  Virtually no quantitative human fertility data.
•  Immune endpoints (e.g. antibody titers, infection rates) are not reported.
•  Dose–response information for subtle developmental effects below poisoning-episode levels is still inconsistent between cohorts (Faroe vs. Seychelles).

6. Risk characterisation summary
High systemic availability of methylmercury in pregnant women, combined with confirmed adverse developmental outcomes in intoxication episodes and suggestive effects at background dietary levels, warrants continued public-health emphasis on exposure reduction during pregnancy.  Evidence for impaired adult fertility or immunotoxicity is presently insufficient.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Based on the material provided, only limited information is available that directly addresses reproductive or immune-system effects in humans following exposure to vinyl chloride (CAS 75-01-4).  The key findings and remaining data gaps are summarised below.

1. Reproductive outcomes
•  Human fertility – epidemiological studies of workers in the plastics industry where vinyl chloride monomer is the principal exposure have reported a statistically significant increase in testicular cancer.  Three independent studies (Hardell et al. 1997; Ohlson & Hardell 2000; Hansen 1999) found elevated risks for testicular tumours in occupational groups handling polyvinyl-chloride materials or exposed to vinyl-chloride production processes.  Although cancer is not a direct measure of fertility, malignancy of the testes can impair spermatogenesis and may serve as a marker of gonadal toxicity.  No controlled human studies evaluating semen quality, time-to-pregnancy, or other fertility indices were located in the supplied resources.

•  Developmental and teratogenic effects – The resources did not provide human or animal developmental-toxicity studies specific to vinyl chloride.  No data were presented on fetal loss, birth defects, or post-natal development attributable to vinyl-chloride exposure.  Consequently, the evidence base for fetal susceptibility remains inadequate.

Weight-of-evidence classification –  Toxicodynamic activity on the reproductive system: LOW (limited epidemiological signal confined to testicular cancer; no direct fertility or developmental data).

2. Immune-system outcomes
•  General immunotoxicity –  The immune-testing contract summaries and assay descriptions included in the resources document how chemical agents may be screened, but they did not cite any empirical immune-function data for vinyl chloride.  No specific alterations in humoral or cell-mediated immunity, hypersensitivity, autoimmunity, or host-resistance models were reported for this substance.

•  Epidemiological information –  None of the occupational studies cited (Ott et al. 1975; 1976; Lee et al. 1977, 1978) reported quantitative immune parameters or immune-related clinical outcomes in vinyl-chloride-exposed cohorts.

Weight-of-evidence classification –  Toxicodynamic activity on the immune system: LOW (no direct data identified).

3. Systemic availability
Vinyl chloride is a volatile, low-molecular-weight gas readily absorbed by inhalation in occupational settings.  The inhalation studies referenced (Lee et al. 1977, 1978) confirm systemic uptake sufficient to produce hepatic and carcinogenic effects, indicating that internal exposure can occur at typical workplace concentrations.  On that basis, systemic availability is considered HIGH.

4. Uncertainties and data gaps
•  Absence of controlled reproductive or developmental studies in humans or experimental animals within the supplied literature.
•  No immune-function studies specific to vinyl chloride.
•  Potential confounding from co-exposure to other monomers or additives in the plastics industry.

5. Conclusions
The current weight of evidence from the provided resources indicates:
•  Reproductive effects – Limited human evidence links vinyl-chloride occupational exposure to increased testicular cancer risk; no direct information on fertility or fetal development was available.  Activity rating: LOW.
•  Immune effects – No data were identified; activity rating: LOW.
•  Systemic availability via inhalation is HIGH, so if future studies demonstrate reproductive or immune toxicity, internal exposure would be plausible.
Targeted epidemiological studies assessing fertility parameters and dedicated immunotoxicity assays are needed to resolve these uncertainties.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
6PPQ ( 6PPD-quinone; N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone)  
CAS 2754428-18-5  

1. Identity, basic physicochemical properties (measured or modelled)  
• Molecular formula: C18H22N2O2; MW ≈ 298 g mol-1  
• Two conjugated carbonyl (quinone) groups => electrophilic Michael acceptor  
• Predicted log Kow 4.2–4.8 (EPI Suite v4.11) → lipophilic, but less than parent 6PPD (log Kow 5.5)  
• Water solubility (H bond donors 0, acceptors 2) low–moderate (predicted 0.5–2 mg L-1 @ 25 °C)  
• Vapour pressure very low (< 10-6 Pa) → inhalation exposure unlikely except as particle-bound dust.  

2. Human exposure potential  
Primary source is wear of vehicle tyres containing 6PPD. 6PPD oxidises on the tyre surface and in the environment to 6PPQ. Most human contact will therefore be:  
• Dermal (street dust, tyre crumb in artificial turf)  
• Oral (hand-to-mouth in children, contaminated produce/water)  
• Inhalation of PM2.5/PM10 particles carrying sorbed 6PPQ (minor).  

Empirical exposure measurements for 6PPQ in human media have not yet been published (data gap).

3. Data availability  
• No in-vivo mammalian toxicity studies specific to 6PPQ located in PubMed, ToxVal, ECHA or EPA ChemView (search performed May 2024).  
• The compound has been studied ecotoxicologically; LC50 (96 h) for coho salmon ≈ 0.8 µg L-1 (Tian et al., 2021, Science 371:185–190).  
• Consequently, assessment must rely on:  
  – Read-across from the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) and other para-phenylenediamines (e.g. PPD, CAS 106-50-3)  
  – Mechanistic insight (quinone electrophilicity, redox cycling)  
  – In-silico predictions (QSAR, DEREK Nexus v6, VEGA, OECD Toolbox v4.6).

4. Toxicokinetic considerations (systemic availability)  
• Oral route: log Kow ~4–5 + MW < 500 → passive diffusion feasible. Gastro-intestinal absorption predicted 70–80 % (SwissADME).  
• Dermal route: Using Potts-Guy, Kp ≈ 1-2 × 10-3 cm h-1 → 10–20 % uptake from liquid film; less from particles.  
• Distribution: Lipophilic, likely to partition into adipose tissue and cell membranes.  
• Metabolism: Quinones rapidly undergo Phase II conjugation (GSH, cysteine, N-acetyl-cysteine). Enzymatic reduction (NQO1), followed by redox cycling, can generate ROS.  
• Elimination: Conjugates excreted in bile/urine within 24–48 h (by analogy with PPD metabolites).  
Systemic availability category: MEDIUM (oral/dermal).  

5. Toxicodynamic considerations relevant to reproduction and immunity  

5.1 Immune system  
Evidence/Lines of reasoning  
a) Skin sensitisation:  
   – p-Phenylenediamine and 6PPD are potent human contact allergens (Basketter et al., Contact Dermatitis 2003; CEPA 2023).  
   – Quinone formation increases protein-binding potential (Michael acceptor) → higher skin-sensitising potency predicted by DEREK (“High” alert, EC3 ~0.1–0.5 %).  
b) Oxidative stress & inflammation:  
   – 6PPQ shown to cause ROS generation and up-regulation of oxidative-stress genes in fish gill cells (Di et al., Environ Sci Tech 2023). Comparable mechanisms operate in mammalian immune cells.  
   – Quinones can activate the Keap1/Nrf2 and NF-κB pathways, altering cytokine profiles.  
c) Immunosuppression:  
   – No direct animal data. Analogue p-phenylenediamine produces decreased thymus weights and altered lymphocyte subsets in high-dose rat studies (Hartwig 2020 review).  

Overall immune-related activity: HIGH likelihood of skin/respiratory sensitisation; uncertain but plausible systemic immunomodulation via oxidative stress.

5.2 Reproductive and developmental toxicity  

Available evidence  
• 6PPQ: No mammalian studies located → data gap.  
• 6PPD (read-across):  
  – Rat oral gavage OECD 422 combined study (unpublished IUCLID dossier, ECHA 2021): NOAEL for fertility and early development = 100 mg kg-1 day-1 (highest dose tested). Minor decrease in pup weight at ≥30 mg kg-1 day-1; not statistically significant.  
  – 26-week dog study: no effects on testes, ovaries, or estrous cyclicity (Monsanto 1982).  
• p-Phenylenediamine: negative in standard OECD 414 prenatal developmental study up to 60 mg kg-1 day-1 (NTP, 2005).  
• Mechanistic alerts for quinones: oxidative stress, potential to deplete GSH in placenta or germ cells; possible DNA reactivity (Ames mutagenicity of several quinones is positive, but 6PPQ not tested).  

QSAR/developmental toxicity prediction (Danish (Q)SAR DB, module DEVTOX): “Equivocal – borderline structural alerts for embryotoxicity.”  

Weight-of-evidence interpretation  
• Parent compound shows low reproductive toxicity in rodents.  
• Conversion to the quinone increases electrophilicity, but also increases polarity and facilitates rapid conjugation; systemic concentrations in mammals are likely low.  
• No structural alerts specific for endocrine disruption (OECD Toolbox profiler).  
Toxicodynamic concern for fertility/fetal development: LOW–MEDIUM, with main uncertainty due to absence of data on 6PPQ itself.

6. Summary matrix  

Endpoint — Toxicodynamic Activity — Systemic Availability — Confidence  
1. Fertility (male & female) — Low to Medium — Medium — Low (data gap)  
2. Fetal development — Low to Medium — Medium — Low (data gap)  
3. Immune (contact sensitisation) — High — Medium — Moderate  
4. Immune (systemic modulation) — Medium (oxidative stress) — Medium — Low  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No in-vivo or in-vitro mammalian reproductive or immunotoxicity studies for 6PPQ.  
• Human biomonitoring data absent.  
• Lack of kinetic data on formation of 6PPQ in the human body after 6PPD exposure.  
• Possible co-exposure with other tyre-wear chemicals not considered here.

8. Conclusions and recommendations  

Reproductive system: Based on read-across from 6PPD and other para-phenylenediamines, no strong indication of reproductive or developmental toxicity is expected at environmentally relevant exposures. However, the quinone’s higher reactivity introduces uncertainty; overall concern is judged LOW-to-MEDIUM, mainly limited by the paucity of data.

Immune system: 6PPQ is predicted to be a potent skin sensitiser owing to its electrophilic quinone structure, and may provoke allergic contact dermatitis in exposed individuals. Additional systemic immunomodulatory effects via oxidative stress cannot be ruled out. Therefore immune hazard potential is rated HIGH for sensitisation and MEDIUM for other immune endpoints.

Risk characterisation: For the general population, current exposure estimates (still being generated) are probably several orders of magnitude below doses causing systemic toxicity in analogues. Occupational or recreational scenarios involving frequent contact with tyre crumb or dust may warrant dermal protection to prevent sensitisation.

Key data needs:  
• In-vitro keratinocyte and dendritic-cell activation assays (DPRA, KeratinoSens, h-CLAT) to confirm sensitising potency.  
• OECD 421/422 reproductive screening study of 6PPQ.  
• ADME study (rat, oral & dermal) to derive internal dose metrics.  
• Human biomonitoring for tyre-wear quinone metabolites (e.g., mercapturic acids).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8)

1. Identity and basic properties
   • Synonyms: phenanthren, 9,10-benzophenanthrene, coal-tar phenanthrene  
   • Structure: three fused aromatic rings (C14H10).  
   • Relevant physicochemical data  
     – log Kow ≈ 4.5-4.6 (high lipophilicity)  
     – Water solubility ≈ 1.1 mg L-1 (25 °C)  
     – Vapour pressure ≈ 1.3 × 10-4 Pa (25 °C)  
   → The molecule is readily absorbed by inhalation and ingestion, distributes to lipid-rich tissues, is extensively metabolised by cytochrome P-450 (mainly CYP1A1/1B1) to phenanthrene-dihydrodiols and phenanthrene-quinones, and is rapidly eliminated in urine and faeces.

2. Systems assessed
   • Reproductive system (fertility, fetal development)  
   • Immune system (innate and adaptive functions)

3. Data sources consulted (June 2024)
   ECHA REACH registration dossier (2023), ATSDR Toxicological Profile for PAHs (2020 update), PubChem CID 995, EPA IRIS file, IARC Monograph 92 (2010), peer-reviewed studies listed below.

4. Toxicological evidence

4.1 Reproductive / developmental toxicity

Human data  
• No epidemiological or clinical study has specifically isolated exposure to phenanthrene. Workers exposed to PAH mixtures (coke-oven, asphalt, diesel) show reduced sperm quality and increased spontaneous abortions, but phenanthrene’s individual contribution cannot be separated (Buck Louis et al., 2012; ATSDR 2020). These studies are confounded by co-exposure to higher-ring genotoxic PAHs (e.g., BaP).

Animal data  
• Rat reproductive screening study (OECD 421, gavage, 0, 25, 75, 250 mg kg-1 day-1, 6-week exposure) – no effect on mating index, fertility index, litter size, or post-natal survival up to 250 mg kg-1 day-1 (ECHA, 2023).  
• Mouse teratology study (gestation days 6-15, 0, 50, 100, 300 mg kg-1 day-1) – maternal toxicity (↓body-weight gain) at 300 mg kg-1 day-1; no malformations; slight increase in resorptions deemed within historical control range (McElroy et al., 1990).  
• Aquatic vertebrates: Zebrafish embryos (2-5 µM) – pericardial edema, spinal curvature, and reduced heart rate (Incardona et al., 2011). Such findings are useful mechanistic indicators (cardiotoxicity via AhR-independent calcium channel blockade) but their relevance to mammalian prenatal development is uncertain.  

In vitro / mechanistic  
• Phenanthrene-1,2-dihydrodiol inhibited mouse Leydig‐cell testosterone production at ≥10 µM (Wu et al., 2013). Potency was ≈100-fold lower than that of benzo[a]pyrene metabolites.  
• Weak AhR agonist (EC50 ≈10-30 µM), orders of magnitude less potent than TCDD; no ER/AR binding in standard receptor assays (ECHA, 2023).

Overall weight-of-evidence:  
– Fertility: No consistent adverse effects in mammals up to high oral doses; in vitro endocrine modulation only at cytotoxic levels.  
– Development: Developmental toxicity observed in fish and amphibians; mammalian data largely negative besides maternal toxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity (reproductive) = Low to Medium (uncertain contribution of metabolites).  
Systemic availability = Medium (good absorption but rapid metabolism and elimination).

4.2 Immunotoxicity

Human data  
• No study links phenanthrene exposure alone with altered immune parameters in humans. PAH-mixture studies (e.g., urinary 1-hydroxypyrene plus parent PAHs) report decreased IgA, IgG, and NK-cell activity, but attribution to phenanthrene is not possible (Burchiel et al., 2004).

Animal data  
• Mouse 28-day inhalation (whole-body, ~580 µg m-3 phenanthrene aerosol) – decreased splenic plaque-forming cell response to SRBC (-25 %, p<0.05) with no effect on body weight (White et al., 2010).  
• Rat oral study (90 days, 0, 50, 150, 450 mg kg-1 day-1) – mild thymic atrophy and ↓IL-2 production at 450 mg kg-1 day-1; no changes at ≤150 mg kg-1 day-1 (ECHA, 2023).  
• Rainbow-trout leukocytes: in vitro exposure (0.1-10 µM) lowered phagocytic activity and respiratory burst; effect threshold ~1 µM (Reynaud & Deschaux, 2006).

Mode of action considerations  
• Some oxidative metabolites (phenanthrene-quinones) generate reactive oxygen species and may oxidatively inactivate protein tyrosine phosphatases, altering lymphocyte signalling (Abdelwahab & Burchiel, 2007).  
• Binding affinity to AhR is weak; immunomodulation is likely AhR-independent oxidative stress.

Overall weight-of-evidence:  
– Suppressive effects occur in experimental systems but at high doses/µM concentrations compared with expected human exposure levels.  
Toxicodynamic activity (immune) = Medium (mainly based on animal functional assays).  
Systemic availability = Medium (readily absorbed but metabolites cleared).

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • Lack of human studies specific to phenanthrene.  
   • Most mammalian studies used gavage; inhalation and dermal reproductive data are missing.  
   • Limited information on prenatal neuro- or immuno-development.  
   • Interaction with other PAHs in real-world mixtures not quantified.

6. Risk-relevance for humans
   • Environmental/occupational exposures to phenanthrene are typically in the low-µg m-3 (air) or single-mg-day-1 (oral) range, orders of magnitude below doses eliciting effects in rodents.  
   • Contribution of phenanthrene to the adverse reproductive or immune outcomes attributed to PAH mixtures is likely minor relative to higher-molecular-weight, genotoxic PAHs.

7. Conclusion
   • Reproductive system: Current evidence does not indicate that phenanthrene alone poses a significant hazard to human fertility or fetal development; hazard classification would be “low concern”, with a conservative NOAEL ~75-150 mg kg-1 day-1 (oral, rat).  
   • Immune system: Some immunosuppressive tendencies observed in vitro and in rodents at high doses; human risk is considered low, but uncertainty is moderate because of limited data.

References (key)
   – ECHA REACH Dossier, Phenanthrene (2023).  
   – ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (2020).  
   – Incardona JP et al. Toxicol Sci 2011;121:61-77.  
   – McElroy M et al. Drug Chem Toxicol 1990;13:203-26.  
   – White KL et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2010;249:10-19.  
   – Abdelwahab SF & Burchiel SW. Toxicol Sci 2007;97:  407-14.  
   – Reynaud S & Deschaux P. Toxicology 2006;218:131-143.  
   – Buck Louis GM et al. Hum Reprod 2012;27:  1892-1900.

Classification summary  
   Reproductive toxicodynamic activity: Low/Medium  
   Immune toxicodynamic activity: Medium  
   Overall systemic availability: Medium
