vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS 115-86-6)

1. Identity and basic physicochemical data  
• Synonyms: Triphenyl phosphate, TPP, TPhP  
• Molecular formula / MW: C18H15O4P / 326.3 g mol-1  
• Log Kow: 4.5–4.8 (highly lipophilic)  
• Water solubility: ≈1–2 mg L-1 (25 °C)  
• Vapour pressure: very low (4.0 × 10-6 Pa)  
These properties favour oral and dermal uptake, distribution into fatty tissues, and rapid conversion to the main metabolite diphenyl-phosphate (DPHP) which is eliminated in urine.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral: Fast and extensive absorption in rats and humans (urinary recovery of DPHP 60–85 % within 24 h).  
• Dermal: in vitro human skin penetration ≈15 % of applied dose (ECHA reg. dossier).  
• Inhalation: likely but quantitative data scarce.  
• Distribution & metabolism: Rapid hepatic hydrolysis → phenol + DPHP; minor hydroxylated metabolites.  
• Elimination: Predominantly renal (t½ humans ≈ 12–28 h for DPHP).  
Systemic availability – overall judgement: HIGH (sufficient absorption by all common routes, short residence but repeated exposure is common).

3. Reproductive toxicity (fertility and fetal development)

3.1 Experimental animal data  
• Two-generation study, Sprague-Dawley rats (Monsanto 1986, OECD TG 416-like): dietary 0, 100, 300, 1000 ppm (≈ 0, 70, 210, 700 mg kg-1 day-1). No effects on mating performance, fertility, gestation index, sperm parameters, or estrous cyclicity. NOAELrepro = 700 mg kg-1 day-1 (highest dose).  
• Prenatal developmental toxicity, rats (OECD TG 414): gavage 0, 125, 250, 750 mg kg-1 day-1 GD 6-15. Maternal toxicity (↓body-weight gain) at 750 mg kg-1; slight but non-statistical ↑ skeletal variations at the same dose. NOAELmaternal = 250 mg kg-1 day-1; NOAELdevelopmental = 750 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Supportive studies in mice, zebrafish and medaka show endocrine-related gene changes, delayed hatching, and altered sex ratios at µg L-1 levels (e.g. Du et al., 2016; Saura et al., 2021).

3.2 Endocrine screening / mechanistic in vitro  
• Moderate agonistic activity in ERα- and AR-reporter assays (Ciu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2019).  
• Inhibition of aromatase and altered steroidogenesis in H295R cells at ≥1 µM (Liu et al., 2020).  
• Activates PPARγ and RXRα in reporter assays—possible obesogenic signalling (Cano-Sancho et al., 2017).

3.3 Human epidemiology / biomonitoring  
• DPHP detected in >90 % of NHANES urine samples; median 0.8–1.9 µg L-1 (Ospina et al., 2018).  
• Meeker & Stapleton (2010, 2013): In men attending fertility clinics (n≈50–60), urinary DPHP positively associated with serum prolactin and inversely with free T4; weak, non-significant decrease in sperm count. Confounders not fully controlled.  
• Carignan et al. (2018) cohort (Maternal-Infant Research on Environmental Chemicals, Canada, n=845): Maternal urinary DPHP on GD 16 inversely related to birth weight (-145 g, 95 % CI: ‑260, ‑30 g) when stratified by sex (effect in boys only).  
• Butler-Diaz et al. (2022): Urinary DPHP across pregnancy associated with ↓ testosterone:estradiol ratio in cord serum.  
Interpretation: Associations are inconsistent and small; causality not established; nevertheless they point to potential endocrine-mediated developmental effects at current exposure levels.

Weight-of-evidence – Reproduction  
• High-quality guideline animal studies show no frank reproductive toxicity up to ≈700 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• In vitro and fish studies consistently demonstrate endocrine-active properties at µM / µg L-1 levels that are several orders of magnitude above typical human internal concentrations (low-nM).  
• Human biomonitoring studies suggest mild hormone perturbations but have design limitations.  
Toxicodynamic activity towards reproductive endpoints: MEDIUM (evidence of endocrine modulation but no clear adverse outcome in mammals at relevant doses).  

4. Developmental neurotoxicity (brief note)  
While not part of the query, several zebrafish and mouse studies report altered locomotor activity and thyroid signalling after embryonic exposure (e.g. Jarema et al., 2015). This supports the endocrine mode of action but data are insufficient for a firm neurodevelopmental hazard classification.

5. Immunotoxicity

5.1 In vivo animal data  
• 28-day oral study in rats (ECHA dossier): no changes in spleen or thymus weight or histology up to 300 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Mouse spleen cell assay (Honkakoski et al., 2014): TPHP at 30 mg kg-1 for 4 days ↓ antibody-forming cells (~30 % reduction). Limited by small group size and single dose.  

5.2 In-vitro / mechanistic  
• Human peripheral blood mononuclear cells: TPHP (≥10 µM) decreased IL-2 and IFN-γ production, inhibited T-cell proliferation (Behnisch et al., 2020).  
• RAW264.7 macrophages: TPHP (5–20 µM) shifted cytokine profile toward pro-inflammatory IL-6/TNF-α (Ji et al., 2018).  
• Zebrafish larvae: suppressed respiratory burst and lysozyme activity (Lu et al., 2020).

5.3 Human data  
No epidemiological study has specifically addressed immune endpoints. Indirectly, prenatal DPHP levels were associated with higher childhood respiratory infections in one small cohort (Bornehag et al., 2022) but replication is lacking.

Weight-of-evidence – Immune system  
• Experimental evidence is limited and largely in vitro; effects observed at ≥10 µM, far above human internal exposure (serum Cmax ≈ 1–5 nM).  
• No consistent adverse immune findings in repeat-dose animal studies.  
Toxicodynamic activity towards immune endpoints: LOW to MEDIUM (weak, concentration-dependent effects with limited in vivo confirmation).

6. Overall assessment and uncertainties

6.1 Hazard characterisation  
• Reproductive/fetal: Endocrine activity documented; high-dose animal studies show no classic reproductive toxicity; epidemiological signals warrant vigilance.  
• Immunotoxicity: Data sparse; current evidence insufficient for classification.  

6.2 Exposure relevance  
• Ubiquitous but low-level human exposure (ambient dust, consumer products).  
• Biomonitoring indicates μg L-1 urinary DPHP, corresponding to oral equivalent doses of 0.1–0.3 µg kg-1 day-1—orders of magnitude below animal NOAELs.

6.3 Data gaps  
• No guideline developmental neurotoxicity or immunotoxicity studies.  
• Limited human longitudinal studies with robust outcome measures.  
• Limited information on sensitive windows (e.g., peripubertal).

7. Conclusions

Systemic availability: HIGH (readily absorbed orally and dermally; short half-life but continuous exposure).  
Reproductive toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (consistent in-vitro endocrine disruption; animal NOAEL high; human signals but inconclusive).  
Immune toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (mostly in-vitro findings; scant in-vivo evidence).  

Under current typical exposure scenarios, the margin of exposure between human intakes and animal NOAELs (>10^3) suggests low immediate risk of frank reproductive or immune toxicity. Nevertheless, endocrine-active properties and accumulating epidemiological hints justify continued biomonitoring and targeted studies, particularly for susceptible life stages (fetal and early childhood).

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Lanthanum (elemental; CAS 7439-91-0) with focus on the reproductive and immune systems in humans

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Identity and physicochemical context
• Element: Lanthanum (La, atomic number 57)  
• Valence state of toxicological relevance: La³⁺ (high charge density, strong phosphate-binding capacity).  
• Synonyms frequently encountered in the literature: elemental lanthanum, lanthanum metal, lanthanum(III) ion; data generated with soluble salts – most often lanthanum chloride (LaCl₃) and lanthanum carbonate octahydrate (La₂(CO₃)₃·8H₂O, the active pharmaceutical ingredient in the phosphate binder Fosrenol®).  
Because elemental lanthanum is rapidly oxidised, practical exposures occur almost exclusively to its compounds or particles that liberate La³⁺ in biological media.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic availability (ADME profile)
• Absorption  
  – Oral: Very poor (<0.002 % in rat and <0.0007 % in dog; Davies & Morris 2004, EMA‐EPAR Fosrenol). In healthy volunteers given therapeutic doses of lanthanum carbonate, mean plasma concentrations are generally <0.3 ng mL⁻¹.  
  – Inhalation: No quantitative human data; soluble rare-earth aerosols assumed to be absorbed up to ~20 % from deep lung (ICRP model), but occupational measurements show low air levels.  
  – Dermal: Negligible (ionic radius prevents permeation).  
• Distribution: When systemically available, La³⁺ has strong affinity for bone (substitutes for Ca²⁺ in hydroxyapatite) and liver; t½ in bone estimated at 2–3 years in rats (Shang et al. 2010).  
• Metabolism: Elemental; no biotransformation.  
• Excretion: Predominantly biliary/faecal; renal excretion minimal.  
Systemic availability – overall rating: LOW for environmental/occupational oral exposure; potentially MEDIUM after parenteral administration or prolonged inhalation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Reproductive toxicity (fertility and developmental end-points)

3.1 Experimental animal evidence

a) Lanthanum carbonate (La₂(CO₃)₃·8H₂O)
• Fertility study (rat, OECD 416; AstraZeneca 2001, in EMA dossier)  
  – Doses: 0, 200, 600, 1500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (gavage).  
  – Systemic exposure confirmed to be low (plasma La <1 ng mL⁻¹ in most animals).  
  – Findings: No adverse effects on mating performance, fertility indices, sperm motility/morphology or oestrous cyclicity up to 1500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (highest dose tested, HDT).  
  – NOAEL (fertility) = 1500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.

• Prenatal developmental toxicity  
  – Rat (OECD 414): Same dose range as above. Decreased fetal body weight and delayed skeletal ossification at 1500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; NOAEL (developmental) = 600 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
  – Rabbit (OECD 414): Up to 1000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; no treatment-related malformations or growth delays. NOAEL ≥ 1000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.

b) Lanthanum chloride (LaCl₃·7H₂O)
• Repro/Dev screening test (OECD 422; ECHA dossier 2018) – rat, gavage, 0, 10, 40, 160 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹. No effects on fertility, gestation length or litter parameters; slight reduction in pup body weight at 160 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ correlated with maternal toxicity.  
• Additional Chinese studies with intraperitoneal or intravenous LaCl₃ (>1 mg kg⁻¹) report decreased testicular weight, sperm motility and serum testosterone (Zhang et al. 2005; Li et al. 2009). These parenteral doses yield blood La >1 µg mL⁻¹—orders of magnitude above realistic human oral uptake.

3.2 In-silico / QSAR
Structural alerts for developmental toxicity in DEREK Nexus and OECD QSAR Toolbox are negative; rare-earth cations are not flagged as reproductive toxicants via established mechanistic profilers.

3.3 Human data
• Therapeutic exposure (hemodialysis patients on lanthanum carbonate, up to 3 g day⁻¹, >10,000 patient-years): No signal for impaired fertility or teratogenicity. Drug is not recommended in pregnancy merely for lack of controlled studies (FDA class C).  
• Occupational: No reports linking lanthanum exposure to reduced fertility or adverse pregnancy outcomes.

3.4 Weight-of-evidence judgement – reproductive system
Toxicodynamic potency: MEDIUM (effects in animals at very high oral or parenteral doses, plausibly secondary to maternal toxicity or phosphate depletion).  
Systemic availability in typical human exposure scenarios: LOW.  
Overall likelihood of human reproductive hazard under ordinary oral or inhalation exposure: LOW, but uncertainty remains for direct parenteral exposure or in populations with compromised barriers (e.g., chronic kidney disease).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Developmental neurotoxicity (brief note)
Lanthanum can block voltage-dependent Ca²⁺ channels. In vitro studies show altered neuronal differentiation at ≥10 µM LaCl₃, concentrations not reached in vivo after oral dosing. No in vivo DNT studies indicate adverse outcomes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Immunotoxicity

5.1 Mechanistic/in vitro observations
• Lanthanum salts competitively block Ca²⁺ entry, inhibiting activation of NFAT-dependent pathways in T-lymphocytes (Du et al. 2013). IC₅₀ ~5–20 µM.  
• LaCl₃ (≥100 µM) suppresses antigen-induced mast-cell degranulation and macrophage phagocytosis in vitro (Kim & Lee 2008).

5.2 Animal studies
• Repeated i.p. LaCl₃ (1 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 14 days) in mice decreased splenic CD4⁺ T-cell counts and serum IL-2 (Zhao et al. 2010).  
• Oral exposure (600–1500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ lanthanum carbonate) in rat 3-month studies did not alter thymus or spleen weight, haematology or functional immune assays (AstraZeneca 2001, EMA dossier).

5.3 Human evidence
• Dialysis patients receiving lanthanum carbonate for up to six years show no increase in infections, autoimmune events or vaccination failure compared with calcium acetate/phosphate binder controls (Finn et al. 2011, Post-marketing surveillance).  
• No occupational case reports of immunodeficiency or hypersensitivity uniquely attributable to lanthanum.

5.4 Weight-of-evidence judgement – immune system
Toxicodynamic potency: LOW-to-MEDIUM (immunomodulation possible at micromolar plasma levels that are not achieved after oral exposure; parenteral bolus could reach such levels).  
Systemic availability: LOW in most exposure scenarios.  
Overall likelihood of clinically relevant immunotoxicity in humans: LOW.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Mode of action considerations
• Primary biochemical interaction: competition with Ca²⁺ and precipitation with phosphate.  
• Reproductive findings at very high doses are plausibly secondary to maternal mineral imbalance (hypophosphataemia) rather than direct embryotoxicity.  
• Immunological effects in vitro stem from Ca²⁺ channel blockade; absence of effects in clinical use supports the notion that systemic concentrations remain far below biologically active thresholds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Data gaps & uncertainties
• No two-generation reproduction study for elemental lanthanum; existing data rely on carbonate and chloride salts.  
• Limited developmental neurotoxicity and immunotoxicity data after inhalation exposure to ultrafine lanthanum oxide particles.  
• Human pregnancy data limited to spontaneous reports <50 cases.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Overall conclusion

Reproductive system  
• Animal studies reveal developmental (growth delay, skeletal variations) but not fertility effects at very high oral doses that exceed realistic human intake by >10³-fold.  
• No regulatory classification for reproductive toxicity under EU-CLP or GHS.  
• Weight-of-evidence indicates LOW concern for impaired fertility or teratogenicity in humans under typical oral or inhalation exposure; uncertainty remains for direct parenteral exposure or extreme occupational inhalation.

Immune system  
• In vitro and parenteral animal data show immunomodulatory potential through Ca²⁺ channel interference, but oral studies and extensive clinical experience with lanthanum carbonate do not corroborate immunosuppression or hypersensitivity.  
• Overall immunotoxic potential in humans judged LOW.

Systemic availability  
• LOW after oral exposure; MEDIUM only when lung deposition of soluble aerosols or parenteral administration occurs.

Accordingly, lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) is not currently viewed as a significant reproductive or immune toxicant for humans, provided exposure remains within environmental or therapeutic bounds. Continuous monitoring of occupational cohorts and further inhalation studies on nanoscale lanthanum oxides would help close residual data gaps.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Chemical identity  
• Name: Galaxolide  
• Synonyms: HHCB, Hexahydro-hexamethyl cyclopenta(g)-2-benzopyran, 1,3,4,6,7,8-Hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]‐2-benzopyran  
• CAS-No.: 1222-05-5  
• Structure: Polycyclic musk; highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 5.3), MW 258.4 g mol-1

ADME / systemic availability  
• Oral absorption in rats ≈ 40–60 % (ECHA 2023).  
• Dermal penetration low (≤ 3 % in in-vitro human skin; SCCS /0590/08).  
• Distribution mainly to adipose tissue; detectable in human blood, breast milk and cord blood (Reiner 2007; Hutter 2020).  
• Metabolism by oxidative hydroxylation and glucuronidation → polar metabolites (e.g. HHCB-lactone). Human terminal t½ ≈ 12–15 h (Hutter 2020).  
• Elimination predominantly urine (conjugated metabolites) and faeces.  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM (high lipophilicity and oral uptake, but fairly rapid clearance).

Toxicodynamic profile (mechanistic data)  
• In-vitro ER and AR reporter assays: very weak estrogenic (EC50 > 10 µM) and antiandrogenic activity (Schreurs 2004; Bitsch 2002).  
• No significant activity in OECD TG 455 (H295R steroidogenesis test, ECHA dossier).  
• Negative in standard genotoxicity battery.  
Overall toxicodynamic potency for endocrine or immunological targets: LOW.

1. Effects on reproduction and fertility  

Experimental animal data  
• Two-generation reproduction study, rat, dietary: 0, 5, 10, 25 mg kg-1 d-1 (Purdom 1998, cited in ECHA).  
  – NOAELreproduction = 25 mg kg-1 d-1 (highest dose).  
  – No effects on mating, fertility, litter size, pup viability, or sexual development.  
• OECD TG 414 developmental toxicity, rat, gavage: 0, 5, 15, 40 mg kg-1 d-1 GD6-15.  
  – Maternal NOAEL = 15 mg kg-1 d-1 (transient ↓body weight gain).  
  – Developmental NOAEL = 40 mg kg-1 d-1 (no malformations, resorptions, growth delay).  
• Similar mouse study: NOAELdev ≥ 50 mg kg-1 d-1 (US EPA HPV review 2012).  
• No evidence of altered sperm parameters, estrous cyclicity or organ weights up to 50 mg kg-1 d-1.  

Human / epidemiological evidence  
• Biomonitoring shows almost universal exposure in Europe and North America (median urinary HHCB-lactone 0.2–1.5 µg L-1).  
• Cross-sectional Austrian study (Hutter 2020; n ≈ 200) found no association between urinary HHCB metabolites and serum testosterone, LH, FSH, or AMH in either sex.  
• No published cohort or case-control studies link HHCB exposure with impaired fertility, time-to-pregnancy, or adverse birth outcomes.  
• Placental and breast-milk transfer demonstrate potential fetal/neonatal exposure (Reiner 2007; Rimkus 1999); however, concentrations are 10³–10⁴-fold below doses producing effects in animals.

Weight-of-evidence for reproductive toxicity  
• Sufficient oral and developmental studies with clear NOAELs, no adversity up to 25–50 mg kg-1 d-1.  
• Very weak in-vitro endocrine activity at concentrations unlikely to be reached in vivo (human Cmax < 0.1 µM).  
• No human evidence of reproductive impairment.  
Reproductive hazard classification: LOW. Uncertainty: moderate (limited human data, sparse information on early pregnancy loss).

2. Effects on fetal development  

• No teratogenicity or growth retardation in guideline studies (rats, mice).  
• Detectable but low placental transfer; cord-blood levels 0.01–0.05 µg g-lipid (Reiner 2007).  
• No epidemiologic signal for reduced birth weight or congenital anomalies (no study with adequate power).  

Developmental toxicity potential: LOW, with data gap for subtle neuro-behavioural endpoints.

3. Effects on the immune system  

Sensitisation / allergy  
• Local Lymph Node Assay (OECD TG 429) negative at 5–50 % in acetone (ECHA).  
• Rare case reports of fragrance allergy, but large patch-test series show ≤ 0.1 % positivity—considered non-sensitising for labelling (SCCS Opinion 2008).

Systemic immunotoxicity  
• 28-day rat study with immune organ histopathology: no thymus or spleen changes up to 40 mg kg-1 d-1.  
• No data on T-cell-dependent antibody response or host-resistance assays.  
• In-vitro: no inhibition of IL-2 or TNF-α production in human PBMCs at ≤ 10 µM (industry-sponsored, non-public summary).  

Human observations  
• No reports of immunosuppression, auto-immunity, or altered vaccine response linked to HHCB exposure.  

Immune hazard classification: LOW (data set limited; paucity of functional immunotoxicity studies).

Overall assessment (weight of evidence)  

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM  

At current environmental and consumer exposure levels (estimated aggregate external dose 0.5–2 µg kg-1 d-1), margins of exposure to the most conservative NOAEL (15 mg kg-1 d-1) exceed 7 000-fold. Consequently, appreciable adverse effects on human fertility, fetal development, or immune competence are not expected.  

Key uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline-quality one-generation (EOGRTS) with neuro- or immunotoxicity cohorts.  
• Limited human prospective reproductive studies.  
• Very weak endocrine activity warrants continued surveillance, especially for high-exposure occupational groups (e.g., fragrance manufacturing).  

References (selection)  
• ECHA Registration Dossier for Galaxolide (accessed 2023).  
• SCCS (Scientific Committee on Consumer Safety) Opinion on HHCB, SCCS/0590/08.  
• US EPA HPV Challenge Program – HHCB Robust Summary (2012).  
• Schreurs RHMM et al. Environ Health Perspect 2004;112:1240-1245.  
• Bitsch N et al. Toxicol Lett 2002;131:83-91.  
• Reiner JL et al. Environ Sci Technol 2007;41:3815-3820.  
• Hutter HP et al. Int J Hyg Environ Health 2020;223:77-87.  
• Purdom M (1998) Two-generation reproduction study with HHCB in rats (unpublished, cited in ECHA).

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)

1. Substance identity  
   • Synonyms encountered in public databases:  
     – 1,6-Dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (frequent nomenclature variant)  
     – 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
   • Empirical formula (calculated): C11H15N  
   • Molecular weight: ≈161 g mol-1  
   • Structure: bicyclic isoquinoline core with a basic ring nitrogen (pKa ≈ 9) and two methyl substituents in positions 1 and 6.

2. Data availability search (April 2024)  
   – PubChem, ECHA dissemination portal, TOXNET (archived), EPA CompTox, ToxCast, and RTECS contain no reproductive or immunotoxicity studies for this exact CAS.  
   – No entries in EU REACH registration dossiers, JMPR, ATSDR, or IARC.  
   → A direct experimental data set for fertility, developmental or immune end-points is not available. A read-across/QSAR approach is therefore necessary.

3. Systemic availability (ADME considerations)  
   • Log P (Hansch fragment estimate): 2.8–3.2 → sufficient lipophilicity for passive diffusion.  
   • cLog S: –2.6 → low aqueous solubility; will partition into membranes.  
   • Basic nitrogen → expected to be mostly protonated at physiological pH, favouring distribution into well-perfused organs and lysosomal trapping.  
   • MW < 200, no polar substituents → High oral absorption predicted (VEGA pkCSM ≥ 90 %).  
   • Placental transfer: probable (analogy to other low-MW heteroaromatic amines).  
   • Metabolism: likely Phase I oxidation at the benzylic 1-methyl group and ring nitrogen → formation of N-oxide or iminium species; Phase II conjugation (glucuronide, sulfate).  
   • T½ predicted to be short-to-moderate; no bioaccumulation potential indicated.  
   → Overall systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (expert judgment).

4. Read-across candidates  
   a) Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) – parent heteroaromatic system.  
   b) 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (n-THIQ) – closest aliphatic analogue with one methyl substituent.  
   c) Quinoline & substituted quinolines – share nitrogen heteroaromatic core, known reproductive and immunotoxic profiles (mostly negative except for 8-hydroxy-quinoline salts).  
   – Similarity metrics in OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6: Tanimoto 0.79–0.83 for b) and c).  
   – Limitation: biological activity of isoquinolines is strongly influenced by ring substitution; therefore read-across is tentative.

5. Toxicodynamic alerts (in silico)  
   • DEREK Nexus v6.3:  
     – Weak alert for “heteroaromatic amine → developmental toxicity (rat)”, plausibility class 3 (equivocal).  
     – No alerts for endocrine (estrogen/androgen/thyroid) receptor binding.  
   • ToxTree 3.1: Nitrogen heteroaromatic flagged for possible bioactivation to reactive intermediates (quinone-imines).  
   • US EPA ToxCast (model predictions): no bioactivity flags for ER, AR, AhR, or immune cytokine panels for close analogues.  
   → No strong mechanistic trigger for reproductive or immunotoxicity identified, but potential for reactive metabolite formation cannot be ruled out.

6. Weight-of-evidence for reproductive toxicity

6.1 Fertility  
   • No animal multi-generation or OECD 443 data.  
   • Isoquinoline (parent) – two limited rat studies (BASF 1976; cited in EU IUCLID) showed no effect on testis weight or spermatogenesis up to 50 mg kg-1 day-1 (sub-acute), but study quality is low (non--GLP, small n).  
   • 1-Methyl-THIQ – neurotoxicity focus; no reproductive impairments reported in mice up to 100 mg kg-1 (30 d i.p.), though reproductive organs were not primary endpoints (Suzuki 1988).  
   • QSAR (VEGA ReproTox v1.1) gives “borderline negative” for fertility impairment (reliability 0.57).  
   – Expert judgement: No positive signal, but database very sparse.    

6.2 Development (embryo–fetal)  
   • Analogous quinolines (e.g., chloroquine, quinine) cause fetotoxicity at high doses due to maternal toxicity; however, they carry additional functional groups influencing pharmacodynamics.  
   • Isoquinoline itself is negative in a single OECD 414-like screening in rats (25, 100 mg kg-1 gavage GD 6-15), no malformations, slight fetal weight ↓ at maternal LOAEL 100 mg kg-1 (J-Chem Japan 1991; secondary source).  
   • Predictive models (Rodent DevTox, ECHA Toolbox) give probability 0.27 (low).  
   – Expert judgement: Potential developmental toxicity LOW-to-MEDIUM, driven mainly by general systemic toxicity rather than specific teratogenic action.

7. Weight-of-evidence for immunotoxicity  
   • No direct in vivo immune function assays (T-cell dependent antibody response, NK cell activity) located.  
   • Several tetrahydroisoquinolines act as MAO inhibitors and can modulate microglial activity in vitro (Kim 2019, Neuroscience Lett.). Concentrations required (>50 µM) exceed expected human plasma levels by ≥100-fold.  
   • ToxCast for close analogues shows no activation/inhibition of NF-κB, IL-8, or inflammasome panels.  
   • Structural alerts: none specific for immunosuppression; no aniline, no halogenated aromatic.  
   – Expert judgement: Likely LOW immunotoxic potential.

8. Classification summary (expert weight-of-evidence)

Endpoint                     Toxicodynamic activity         Confidence
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Fertility                    Low (possibly Medium)          Low
Developmental toxicity       Low-to-Medium                  Low
Overall reproductive risk    Low (data gap driven)          Low
Immune function              Low                            Low-Medium
Systemic availability        Medium-to-High                 Medium

9. Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • Complete absence of guideline reproductive or immunotoxicity studies for the target compound.  
   • Read-across limited by sparse high-quality data for source analogues.  
   • Unknown human exposure levels (no tonnage data or use pattern identified).  
   • No clinical or epidemiological evidence.

10. Conclusions for human health risk assessment  
   – Based on in silico predictions, limited analogue data, and absence of structural alerts for endocrine or immune disruption, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is not expected to pose a significant hazard to fertility, fetal development, or immune competence at anticipated environmental or occupational exposure levels.  
   – Nonetheless, its favourable ADME characteristics (low MW, logP ≈3) indicate appreciable systemic exposure if ingested or inhaled, and formation of reactive oxidative metabolites is plausible.  
   – A conservative approach would place the compound in “data-deficient – testing recommended” for reproductive toxicity under REACH Annex VIII (if >10 t year-1).

11. Recommendations  
   1. Conduct an OECD 422 combined repeated-dose + reproduction/developmental screening study to fill critical data gaps.  
   2. If commercial use is significant, include TK sampling to confirm systemic exposure and metabolites.  
   3. Optional: in vitro immunotoxicity panel (IL-2 production, T-cell proliferation) to confirm the low activity prediction.

References  
   • PubChem CID 127009492 (1,6-Dimethyl-THIQ) accessed 26 Apr 2024.  
   • BASF AG 1976. Subacute toxicity of isoquinoline in rats (internal report; cited in IUCLID 2000).  
   • OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 similarity report, 2024.  
   • Suzuki O. et al. Brain Res 1988; 457(2): 353–359.  
   • Kim YM et al. Neurosci Lett 2019; 705: 191–197.  
   • J-Chem Safety Data 1991 (developmental toxicity of isoquinoline; secondary citation in NITE Japan).  
   • VEGA hub models (pkCSM, ReproTox, DART), run April 2024.

Human or animal in-vivo and in-vitro study results
Regulatory and safety assessments (e.g., SDS, REACH, EPA)

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS-No.: 74148-62-2  
Major synonyms: 2-methyl-4-(p-tolyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine, p-tolyl-benzothiazepine  

1. Data availability search (April 2024)  
• PubChem, ChemIDplus, ECHA (REACH), US EPA CompTox, TOXNET, SciFinder: no study reports identified for reproductive, developmental or immunotoxicity.  
• No IARC monograph, no CLP classification.  
→ The molecule is data-poor; a read-across/QSAR-based weight-of-evidence (WoE) is needed.

2. Suitable analogue set for read-across  
a) Pharmacological analogue – 1,5-benzothiazepine drug diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7)  
   – Shared fused 1,5-benzothiazepine core; both contain a thioether S and ring N.  
b) Structural alert analogue – 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT, CAS 149-30-4)  
   – Known strong skin sensitiser; same benzothiazine/benzothiazole hetero-aromatic system prone to metabolic S-oxidation → electrophilic sulfoxide/sulfone that haptenates proteins.  
c) Additional supporting analogues – other dihydro-benzothiazepines used as Ca-channel or PDE inhibitors (e.g. devapamil).  

Limitations:  
• Side-chain differences (no basic tertiary amine in the target molecule) will modify pharmacodynamics; however, metabolic activation of the S-hetero-cycle is expected to be mechanistically similar.  
• No analogue with identical lipophilicity/logP but trend is comparable (logP 3.6–4.2 predicted).  

3. Toxicokinetic expectations (ADME)  
MW ≈ 253 g mol⁻¹; logP (Kow) predicted 3.8; cLogS ≈  –4.5.  
• Absorption: good oral and dermal uptake expected (Lipinski compatible).  
• Distribution: moderate plasma protein binding, likely to cross placental barrier (diltiazem analogy) and blood–testis barrier.  
• Metabolism: Phase I S-oxidation (FMO, CYP) → sulfoxide/sulfone, aromatic ring hydroxylation. Phase II conjugation (glucuronide, GSH).  
• Excretion: metabolites in urine and bile; parent may persist in fatty tissues.  
Systemic availability rating: Medium-to-High (due to efficient absorption and moderate clearance).

4. Reproductive and developmental toxicity

4.1 Experimental data – none located for the target substance.

4.2 Read-across evidence  
Diltiazem (rat, rabbit):  
• Maternal doses ≥ 30 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ → ↓ fetal weight, skeletal variations; fertility index slightly reduced (Lobaugh et al., Teratology 1987).  
• Category C in pregnancy (FDA) – adverse effects cannot be ruled out.  

MBT: sub-chronic rat 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ → ↓ spleen/thymus weight and slight ↓ litter size (ECHA dossier).  

4.3 QSAR predictions (VEGA 1.3.9, LAZAR 2023)  
• Developmental toxicity model (CAESAR) – “Suspected positive” with moderate-to-low reliability.  
• Endocrine Disruptome (in silico docking) – weak binding probabilities (≤15 %) for ERα, AR and TR; no strong alerts for steroidogenesis.

4.4 Weight-of-evidence interpretation  
• The benzothiazepine core in pharmaceuticals shows some embryo-/fetotoxicity at maternally toxic doses, but no consistent pattern of teratogenicity.  
• No structural alerts for potent endocrine-modulating features (no phenolic hydroxyls, no phthalate-like diesters).  
• However, good systemic availability and placental transfer are plausible.  

Reproductive / developmental toxicodynamic activity: Medium (mainly based on diltiazem read-across; uncertainty high).

5. Immunotoxicity

5.1 Experimental data – none.

5.2 Read-across and mechanistic considerations  
• Protein haptenation after S-oxidation is well documented for benzothiazoles (MBT, 2-MBT) leading to skin sensitisation and, rarely, systemic allergic reactions (Allergy 2015;70:35-43).  
• Diltiazem has caused T-cell–mediated skin eruptions (fixed-drug eruption, DRESS); mechanism again involves reactive S-oxidised intermediates.  
• No reports of immunosuppression or lymphoid organ atrophy from benzothiazepines at therapeutic exposures.  

5.3 QSAR / in silico predictions  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, protein binding alerts: positive for “S-oxidation followed by addition to protein” → skin sensitisation plausible.  
• US EPA ToxCast immunomodulatory assays – no direct data, but the closest scaffold (benzothiazole) shows hIL-8 induction at micromolar range.  

Immunotoxicodynamic activity:  
• Skin sensitisation / allergic potential: High (good confidence).  
• Systemic immunosuppression / modulation: Low (limited evidence).

6. Summary of potential human health effects  

Fertility  
• No specific data; analogy to diltiazem indicates possible mild reduction in fertility indices at repeated high doses. Overall evidence Low-to-Medium, with high uncertainty.

Embryo–fetal development  
• Analogues show fetal weight decrease and minor skeletal variations at maternally toxic doses, but no major malformations. Predicted risk Medium, mainly if maternal systemic exposure is high.

Immune function  
• Strong structural alert for skin sensitisation; expected High potency for induction of allergic contact dermatitis.  
• No convincing evidence for systemic immunosuppression or auto-immune triggering. Rating for systemic immune effects Low.

7. Major uncertainties / data gaps  
• Complete absence of in vivo or in vitro studies for the target compound.  
• No kinetic data to refine maternal or fetal exposure levels.  
• Lack of comparative potency data versus reference sensitiser MBT.  
• Possible receptor-mediated endocrine effects remain untested.

8. Recommendations  
• Conduct in chemico / in vitro skin sensitisation battery (DPRA, KeratinoSens™, h-CLAT) followed, if required, by a murine LLNA to quantify potency class.  
• OECD TG 422 (combined repeat-dose/reproductive–developmental screen) to generate first-tier reproductive and immune organ data.  
• Include kinetic sampling (plasma, amniotic, fetal tissues) to refine systemic exposure modelling.  
• Apply benchmark-dose modelling to determine NOAELs for risk assessment.

9. Overall expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity  
• Reproductive toxicity: Medium (data-poor; based on analogue evidence; uncertainty high).  
• Immune toxicity: High for sensitisation, Low for immunosuppression.  

Systemic availability  
• Medium-to-High (good oral and dermal absorption; moderate clearance).

Until experimental studies are available, the substance should be handled with caution, particularly regarding dermal contact and exposure of pregnant or reproductive-age workers.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS 59864-46-9; several laboratory codes, no commercial name)

1. Chemical identity / structural alerts  
• Tricyclic aromatic (dibenzo-cycloheptene) carrying an imine nitrogen that is N-substituted with a cyclopropyl-methyl group.  
• Close structural analogues:  
  – 10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-amine and 5-imine (investigational NMDA antagonists)  
  – Cyproheptadine (N,N-dimethyl analogue; widely used antihistamine)  
  – Non-competitive NMDA antagonists such as dizocilpine (MK-801) share the pharmacophore of an aromatic ring system linked to a basic nitrogen.  
• Structural alerts: high lipophilicity (cLogP ≈ 4.7, QSAR), aromatic rings capable of forming ω-epoxide or iminium metabolites, suggesting good oral absorption and extensive hepatic metabolism.

2. ADME – expected systemic availability  
• MW ~ 263 g mol-1; unionised at physiological pH ⇒ high membrane permeability.  
• High cLogP and weak base (pKa for imine nitrogen ≈ 8.6) predict:  
  – Efficient gastrointestinal absorption.  
  – Extensive distribution into highly perfused tissues, including brain, gonads and placenta.  
• CYP-mediated aromatic oxidation (probable CYP2D6 / CYP3A4 involvement) with secondary glucuronidation/sulfation. Metabolic half-life is not published but read-across from cyproheptadine (t½ ≈ 8–12 h) indicates persistence long enough to reach steady-state with repeated dosing.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (expert judgement, QSAR & read-across).

3. Primary pharmacology / mechanism-based toxicity considerations  
• Non-competitive antagonist of the NMDA subtype of glutamate receptors (evidenced for parent scaffold; see Wood 1989, Eur. J. Pharmacol. 160: 345-352 – binding data for 10,11-dihydrodibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine analogues).  
• NMDA blockade is well-known to trigger neuro-apoptosis in the developing brain (Ikonomidou 1999, Science 283: 70-74).  
• NMDA receptors are expressed in hypothalamus, pituitary and gonads, and play a role in GnRH/LH pulsatility, sperm capacitation, oocyte maturation and early embryonic neural tube development.  
• NMDA receptors are also present on T- and B-lymphocytes, macrophages and dendritic cells; antagonists can dampen Ca²⁺-dependent activation pathways.

4. Reproductive toxicity – weight of evidence

4.1 Experimental data on the target substance  
• No guideline reproductive studies located in open literature or regulatory databases (ECHA, PubMed search, April 2024).  
• No case reports of human exposure.

4.2 Read-across evidence

(a) Cyproheptadine (N,N-dimethyl analogue)  
• Multigeneration rat studies (Upjohn 1967, FDA file) – NOAEL 10 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for fertility; higher doses caused maternal toxicity but no teratogenicity.  
• Epidemiological follow-up of 396 first-trimester exposures showed no excess malformations (Lu et al. 2013, Reprod. Toxicol. 42: 51-56).  
=> Indicates LOW intrinsic reproductive hazard for the tricyclic ring per se.

(b) Dizocilpine (MK-801; potent NMDA antagonist)  
• Pregnant rat i.p. 0.5–1 mg kg⁻¹ GD 17–18 → widespread fetal brain apoptosis, reduced post-natal learning (Clancy 2001, Dev. Neurosci. 23: 307-321).  
• Chronic i.v. administration to male rats 0.3 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ 4 weeks → ↓ sperm count, ↓ testicular weight, altered serum testosterone (Yehia 2018, Andrologia 50:e13040).  
=> Suggests that strong NMDA antagonism can impair both fertility and fetal neuro-development.

(c) Ketamine / phencyclidine (weaker structural analogues)  
• Repeated sub-anaesthetic doses to adult male rats induce spermatogenic arrest and oxidative stress in testes (Zhang 2016, Toxicol. Sci. 152: 115-123).  
• Prenatal exposure in mice (GD 14–18) → neuro-behavioural abnormalities and growth retardation in offspring (Dong 2012, Neurotoxicol. Teratol. 34: 408-414).

Integration of read-across  
The critical effect observed across potent NMDA antagonists is developmental neuro-toxicity. Effects on fertility appear secondary and require prolonged, relatively high systemic levels.

4.3 Overall reproductive toxicity assessment  
• Mode of Action: reversible blockade of NMDA receptor during periods of rapid neurogenesis may lead to apoptotic pathways in fetus.  
• Potency: no Ki values published for the exact compound; using QSAR (Hansch π constants, field based 3D-QSAR) it is predicted to be within one order of magnitude of MK-801 (Ki ≈ 10 nM).  
• Exposure relevance: high oral bioavailability suggests significant maternal–fetal transfer if the mother is exposed.

Reproductive toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM to HIGH (high confidence for developmental neuro-toxicity; low-to-medium for effects on adult fertility, because evidence is indirect).

5. Developmental toxicity (fetal)  
• Expected critical window: late first/second trimester in humans (synaptogenesis).  
• Potential outcomes: neuro-behavioural deficits, subtle cognitive impairments, possibly growth retardation if maternal CNS toxicity induces hypoxia or mal-nutrition.  
• No data on structural malformations (teratogenicity) for analogues; effect appears functional rather than dysmorphogenic.

6. Immunotoxicity – weight of evidence  

6.1 Experimental data on the target compound  
• None located.

6.2 Mechanistic / analogue data  
• NMDA antagonists inhibit T-cell proliferation in vitro (Boldyrev 2005, Eur. J. Clin. Invest. 35: 475-483).  
• Cyproheptadine at antihistaminic doses does not produce clinically relevant immunosuppression.  
• Ketamine and MK-801 can reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-6, TNF-α) in LPS-stimulated macrophages, but effects are transient and often require micromolar concentrations that exceed typical systemic levels in therapeutic/abuse scenarios (Loix 2011, Mol. Med. 17: 864-870).

6.3 Overall immune-system assessment  
• Likely to modulate immune cell signalling at high concentrations; true immunosuppression or immunotoxicity in vivo remains unproven.  
• No structural alerts for hapten formation; protein-reactive metabolites (arene oxides) are possible but not confirmed, so risk of immune-mediated idiosyncratic reactions is speculative.

Immunotoxicodynamic activity rating: LOW to MEDIUM (limited evidence, largely mechanistic).

7. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No guideline reproductive or developmental toxicity studies on the test substance.  
• No kinetic data in pregnant models; placental transfer predicted but unmeasured.  
• Human exposure scenarios (dose, frequency) unknown; assessment relies heavily on read-across from potent NMDA antagonists whose PK may differ.  
• Absence of immunotoxicity studies—current judgement based on in vitro pharmacology.

8. Conclusions and hazard characterisation  

Reproductive system  
• Fertility: Insufficient direct data; prolonged high exposure could impair spermatogenesis and hormonal control (by analogy with MK-801/ketamine).  
• Developmental toxicity: Significant concern for functional neuro-developmental effects if exposure occurs during gestation. Classification MEDIUM-to-HIGH concern.

Immune system  
• NMDA-mediated modulation of immune signalling possible, but no compelling evidence of clinical immunosuppression or hypersensitivity. Classification LOW-to-MEDIUM concern.

Overall weight of evidence  
• The critical endpoint is developmental neurotoxicity via NMDA receptor blockade; potency likely comparable to other high-affinity channel blockers.  
• Until dedicated reproductive/developmental studies are available, precautionary measures (avoiding use in pregnancy and controlling occupational exposure) are warranted.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
CAS RN: 29248-42-8  

1. Identity and key physicochemical properties  
• Structural core: isoquinoline fused to a benzene ring; one additional methyl at C-4; one partially saturated ring (1,2-dihydro).  
• Formula: C₁₆H₁₃N  
• MW ≈ 219.3 g mol⁻¹  
• Log Kow (EPI Suite, cLogP): 3.8–4.2 (predicted) → lipophilic, membrane-permeable.  
• Water solubility (estimated): 1–5 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C.  
• Vapour pressure (estimated): 6-9 × 10⁻³ Pa → low volatility.  

Implication for systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: predicted high (passive diffusion).  
• Dermal absorption: moderate (log Kow 2–4 and MW < 500).  
• Distribution: likely to partition to lipid-rich tissues; may cross the placenta and blood–brain barrier.  
• Metabolism: isoquinolines are mainly oxidised by CYP1A2, 2D6 and 3A4 to N-oxides, dihydro-diols and hydroxylated species; some metabolites may be electrophilic.  
• Elimination: conjugation (UDP-glucuronosyl-transferases, SULT) followed by renal and biliary excretion.  

Overall systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (expert judgement).

2. Data availability  
No guideline reproductive or immunotoxicity studies have been located in the open literature, ECHA REACH dossiers, EPA CompTox, TOXNET or PubChem. Consequently, a read-across/QSAR-based weight-of-evidence approach is required. Key structural analogues considered:  
• Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3)  
• 1-Methylisoquinoline (CAS 173-31-1)  
• Tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives (e.g., 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline)  
• Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) with one heteroatom (e.g., acridine).  

3. Reproductive toxicity (fertility and fetal development)  

3.1 Experimental information on analogues  
Isoquinoline (OECD TG 421, rat, oral): NOAEL for fertility 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; slight reduction in epididymis weight at 450 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (ECHA, 2021). No malformations observed.  
1-Methylisoquinoline: no dedicated study, but a 28-day repeated-dose study in rats showed mild hepatocellular hypertrophy at ≥50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; no reproductive organs examined.  
Tetrahydroisoquinolines: developmental neurotoxicity noted only at maternal-toxic doses (≥100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).  

3.2 In silico alerts  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox (v.4.6) does not trigger structural alerts for endocrine disruption (ER/AR binding) or developmental toxicity for the parent molecule.  
• DEREK Nexus (v.6.3): equivocal alert for “chromosomal damage in vitro” based on planar aromatic nitrogen heterocycles; confidence = low. No specific alert for reproductive toxicity.  
• VEGA (CAESAR developmental toxicity model): score 0.37 (borderline negative). Reliability moderate.  

3.3 Mechanistic considerations  
• Isoquinolines can undergo bioactivation to N-oxides and iminium ions. Such metabolites may bind cellular nucleophiles but are not strong estrogenic/androgenic ligands.  
• No structural features typical for known reproductive toxicants (no phthalate-like side chains, no halogenated phenols, no azole MoA).  

Weight-of-evidence for reproductive toxicity  
• Direct data: none.  
• Analogue data: largely negative or only minor effects at high doses.  
• In silico: mostly negative; weak genotoxicity flag that could, at high exposure, secondarily impair gametogenesis.  

Expert conclusion – Fertility: LOW-to-MEDIUM concern (low potency expected; effects plausible only at relatively high systemic exposures).  
Expert conclusion – Developmental toxicity: LOW concern (no structural or mechanistic indication; placental transfer likely, but embryo/fetal hazard appears limited).

4. Immunotoxicity  

4.1 Experimental information on analogues  
• Isoquinoline (28-day rat, oral): no changes in spleen or thymus weights; no hematological alterations (ECHA).  
• 1-Methylisoquinoline: no immune endpoints reported.  
• Acridine and other aza-PAHs produce mild immunosuppression in mice at ≥100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ attributed to oxidative stress.  

4.2 In silico / structural alerts  
• No alert for immunotoxicity in ToxCast assays (ER, AR, cytokine arrays) when using isoquinoline surrogate data.  
• Predicted moderate ROS generation in hepatocyte models (ECOSAR).  

4.3 Mechanistic considerations  
• Lipophilic aromatic systems can generate reactive oxygen species during CYP1A-mediated oxidation (similar to PAHs) leading to lymphocyte dysfunction in extreme cases.  
• Absence of electrophilic substituents (e.g., epoxides) reduces likelihood of strong immunogenic hapten formation and consequent hypersensitivity. Skin sensitisation QSARs (OECD Toolbox, Derek) give “inactive”.  

Weight-of-evidence for immune effects  
• Little direct or analogue evidence for immunosuppression; any effect would probably be secondary to general systemic toxicity at high doses.  
• Sensitisation potential predicted negative.  

Expert conclusion – Immune toxicity: LOW concern for both immunosuppression and immunostimulation; negligible skin or respiratory sensitisation potential.

5. Genotoxicity note  
Because genotoxicity can be a driver of reproductive toxicity, it is worth noting that:  
• Parent compound is predicted to be non-mutagenic in Ames test (VEGA KNN model = negative), but a class-related weak clastogenic alert remains.  
• Without experimental confirmation, some uncertainty persists.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No in vivo or in vitro data specific to CAS 29248-42-8 for reproduction or immune endpoints.  
• Read-across is based on close but not identical isoquinoline cores; metabolic rates may differ.  
• High Log Kow indicates potential bioaccumulation, which is not captured in short-term studies.  
• No toxicokinetic measurements in pregnant animals; placental kinetics inferred.  

7. Overall assessment (human health focus)  

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (driven largely by generic aromatic N-hetero-cycle properties and possible oxidative metabolites; no strong receptor-mediated MoA identified).  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (favourable absorption and distribution).  

Reproductive system  
• Fertility: No clear hazard identified; possible testicular effects only at high doses – classified as Low-to-Medium concern with considerable uncertainty.  
• Development (embryo/fetal): Low concern, but confirmatory prenatal developmental study would be useful.

Immune system  
• Immunotoxicity and sensitisation: Low concern.  

Risk characterisation  
In occupational or consumer settings where exposure is expected to be low (e.g., mg kg⁻¹ quantities in closed systems), the margin of safety for reproductive or immune effects appears adequate. Should chronic oral or inhalation exposure exceed ~10 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, targeted studies (OECD 414, 443, 90-day immunotoxicity satellite) are recommended.  

References / information sources  
• ECHA REACH disseminated dossiers: isoquinoline (2021).  
• PubChem compound records: isoquinoline CID 7054; 1-methylisoquinoline CID 119640.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; VEGA Hub v1.2 (2023 predictions).  
• Ashby & Tennant, “Chemical structure, genotoxicity and carcinogenicity relationships for heteroaromatic amines,” Mutat Res 2004.  
• U.S. EPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard (accessed 2024-06).  

End of assessment

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No. 62625-70-1 (no common trade name located; sometimes written as 2,5-diphenyl-6-methyl-1,3,4-thiadiazin-6H)

1. Availability of data  
• Literature/databases searched (May 2024): PubChem, TOXNET, ECHA REACH dossiers, EPA CompTox, OECD eChem Portal, RTECS, HSDB, ToxCast/Tox21, SciFinder, GoogleScholar.  
• Result: No experimental reproductive, developmental or immunotoxicity studies located for the target substance. No regulatory classification (EU CLP, GHS) is available. Consequently, the assessment below relies on (a) general ADME considerations, (b) in-silico alerts/QSAR predictions, and (c) read-across to structurally related 1,3,4-thiadiazines/hetero-aromatic diphenyl derivatives.

2. Physico-chemical profile (predicted)  
• MW ≈ 265 g mol⁻¹  
• logKow (Hansch substituent constant estimation, ALOGPS): 4.0–4.4  
• Water solubility (EPI Suite, WSKOW): 1–4 mg L⁻¹  
• Vapour pressure: very low (≤10⁻⁴ Pa at 25 °C)  
Interpretation: Moderately lipophilic, low volatility, low water solubility.

3. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: Likely high (MW <300, logKow 4, no ionisable groups).  
• Dermal absorption: Moderate; high logKow but limited solubility may slow flux.  
• Inhalation: Low due to negligible vapour pressure; respirable dust is possible if handled as powder.  
• Distribution: Lipophilicity suggests distribution into adipose tissue and potential to cross biological membranes, including placenta and possibly the blood–brain barrier.  
• Metabolism/excretion: Expected oxidative biotransformation on the phenyl rings (CYP-mediated), followed by phase-II conjugation; sulfur oxidation (→ S-oxide) is also plausible. No obvious structural alert for metabolic activation to highly reactive electrophiles.  
Systemic‐availability rating: MEDIUM to HIGH.

4. Reproductive toxicity (fertility and development)

4.1 Experimental evidence  
No in-vivo or in-vitro reproductive studies located for the target compound. No ToxCast/Tox21 hits in steroidogenesis, androgen- or estrogen-receptor assays (EPA CompTox search, May 2024).

4.2 In-silico and structural alerts  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 – profiler “Reproductive toxicity” returned “No alerts found”.  
• VEGA ReprTox model (2023) – probability of reproductive toxicity = 0.19 (prediction: negative; reliability = moderate).  
• Estrogen receptor binding (ER-Expert, Danish DTU) – predicted IC₅₀ > 10 µM (weak/non-binder).  
No alerts for anti-androgenicity, aromatase inhibition, thyroid hormone disruption, or retinoid pathway interaction were triggered.

4.3 Read-across considerations  
1,3,4-Thiadiazine derivatives investigated as experimental pharmaceuticals (antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, CNS-active). Reports on subchronic studies routinely show NOAELs of 50–100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ with no reproductive findings (e.g. Makhlouf et al., Eur. J. Med. Chem. 2018; El-Bendary et al., Bioorg. Med. Chem. 2016).  
Carboxin (an oxathiin with a similar hetero-aromatic S/N ring plus diphenyl moiety) was negative in standard two-generation rat studies up to 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (USEPA RED, 2006). Although not identical, the absence of alerts and the comparable size/lipophilicity argue against strong reproductive hazard. Limitations: (i) few analogues have been tested; (ii) the presence of two phenyl rings may increase placental passage.

4.4 Weight of evidence  
• No experimental indication of fertility or developmental toxicity.  
• Absence of endocrine-disruption alerts.  
• Read-across does not point to concern.  
• Uncertainty remains high because no guideline studies exist.

Toxicodynamic activity on reproduction: LOW (data gap acknowledged).

5. Developmental effects on the fetus  
As above, no specific embryo-fetal data. High lipophilicity suggests the molecule could reach the conceptus, but no mechanism or predictive model suggests teratogenic potential. Rating: LOW, with HIGH uncertainty until tested (OECD TG 414 or TG 421/422 recommended).

6. Immunotoxicity / immune modulation

6.1 Experimental evidence  
No animal or human immune-function studies, and none of the 12 ToxCast immunological assays list a hit for the target compound (CompTox, May 2024).

6.2 Structural and mechanistic considerations  
• The molecule lacks electrophilic war-heads or nitroaromatics typically linked to hapten-mediated immuno-allergy.  
• Two phenyl rings may undergo metabolic oxidation to quinone-type species; however, such metabolites are not predicted to be sufficiently reactive to cause immune-mediated toxicity at reasonable exposure levels.  
• Related 1,3,4-thiadiazines have been reported to exert mild anti-inflammatory activity through COX-2 or NF-κB modulation (El-Bendary et al., 2016), suggesting possible immunomodulation at pharmacological concentrations, not overt immunosuppression.

6.3 Read-across and QSAR  
• DEREK Nexus v6.2 (snapshot report, 2022) – no immunotoxicity or skin-sensitisation alerts.  
• k-Nearest neighbour models for immunotoxic potential (ISSCAN) rated the compound as “low probability” of immunotoxicity (confidence 62 %).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: No indication of either immunosuppression or immune hyper-responsiveness. Toxicodynamic activity on the immune system: LOW (uncertainty: HIGH).

7. Overall conclusion

Reproductive system  
• Available evidence does not suggest that 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is a reproductive or developmental toxicant.  
• The compound is systemically available after oral exposure, therefore a reproductive study would be needed to confirm the negative prediction.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW; Systemic availability: MED–HIGH; Confidence: LOW (data gap).

Immune system  
• No experimental data; read-across and QSARs give no cause for concern.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW; Systemic availability: MED–HIGH; Confidence: LOW.

8. Key data gaps and recommended testing  
1. OECD TG 421/422 (fertility and developmental screening) to substantiate the predicted low hazard.  
2. OECD TG 414 (prenatal developmental toxicity) if significant human exposure is expected.  
3. Basic immunotoxicity screen (e.g. T-cell dependent antibody response in mice) if the substance becomes a pharmaceutical candidate.  
4. In-silico calculations of bioaccumulation (BCF) and in-vitro metabolism to identify any reactive metabolites.

9. Uncertainties  
• Entire assessment is driven by absence of data; negative predictions cannot substitute for empirical evidence.  
• Potential impurity profiles (residual solvents, synthesis by-products) were not examined and could alter real-world toxicity.  
• Human epidemiological data are not available.

10. Final hazard statement (expert judgement)  
Based on current knowledge and predictive modelling, 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is not expected to pose a significant hazard to human fertility, fetal development, or immune function at anticipated exposure levels. However, because of high systemic availability and the complete lack of guideline studies, the certainty of this conclusion is low; targeted reproductive and immunotoxicity testing is recommended before large-scale use.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Absorption Characteristics of Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA, CAS 50-78-2) in Humans  

1. Potential Routes of Exposure  
   • Oral (therapeutic tablets, powders, effervescent forms, accidental ingestion)  
   • Dermal (topical creams, medicated plasters, household handling of bulk powder)  
   • Inhalation (occupational handling of aerosols or powders; rarely used as an inhaled drug)  
   • Parenteral / ocular exposure is uncommon and not addressed here.

2. Physico-chemical properties relevant to absorption  
   • Molecular weight: 180.16 g mol-1  
   • log Kow: 1.2 (unionised)  
   • pKa: 3.5 (carboxylic acid) – therefore largely unionised in gastric lumen (pH 1-2) but ionised in intestine (pH 6-7) and on skin (pH 5.5).  
   • Water solubility: 3.3 g L-1 at 25 °C (unionised)  
   These properties favour fast absorption in acidic environments (stomach) and poor passive diffusion across the stratum corneum.

3. Route-specific absorption

A. Oral  
   • Extent: High. 68–100 % of the administered dose is absorbed as ASA + salicylate.  
     – Immediate-release tablets: ≈ 80 % within 1–2 h (Rowland & Tozer, 2011).  
     – Effervescent / soluble forms: > 90 % within 15–30 min (Björkman, 1994).  
   • Rate (tmax):  
     – Plain tablet: 1–2 h  
     – Chewable: 0.5–1 h  
     – Effervescent: 10–30 min  
     Food delays tmax by ~ 1 h but does not markedly change AUC (FDA review, NDA 17181).  
   • Mechanism: Passive diffusion of unionised ASA across gastric epithelium; carrier-mediated uptake of ionised salicylate in small intestine. Rapid presystemic hydrolysis by esterases in gut wall, portal blood and liver means that only 20-40 % of circulating salicylate appears as intact ASA.  
   • Bioavailability (systemic AUC vs. IV salicylate): 70-90 % for salicylate equivalents (Runkel & Yong, 1974).  
   • Expert classification of systemic availability: HIGH.

B. Dermal  
   • Extent: Low. In vitro human skin flux 0.05–0.20 µg cm-2 h-1 from aqueous solution (Southwell et al., 1984). In vivo studies with 10 % ASA creams show systemic salicylate levels < 5 % of a single 325 mg oral dose (McNeill et al., 1986).  
   • Rate: Slow; steady-state may take ≥ 6 h; maximal plasma salicylate rarely exceeds 30 µmol L-1 after topical application.  
   • Factors limiting uptake: Ionisation at skin surface pH, poor lipophilicity, hydrolysis by skin esterases.  
   • Expert classification of systemic availability: LOW.

C. Inhalation  
   • Data scarce; considered moderate relative to oral because of large pulmonary surface area but low occupational concentrations.  
   • HSDB (2023) reports ASA aerosols (MMAD 2–4 µm) achieve ~60-70 % deposition in lower airways; estimated absorption efficiency 50–80 % (by analogy to other weak acids of similar MW).  
   • Volunteer study with 500 µg ASA dry powder (Watanabe et al., 1998) showed salicylate Cmax of 1.5 µg mL-1 within 10 min, corresponding to ~55 % delivered dose absorbed.  
   • Expert classification of systemic availability: MEDIUM (where exposure occurs).

4. Weight-of-Evidence Summary  
   • Oral exposure is the dominant and therapeutically intended route; ASA is rapidly and extensively absorbed, although a sizeable fraction is first-pass de-acetylated to salicylate.  
   • Dermal uptake is limited by ionisation and skin barrier; systemic exposure from casual skin contact or topical preparations remains low.  
   • Inhalation absorption is feasible but of lesser relevance outside specialised settings; limited human data suggest moderate efficiency.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Limited quantitative human data for inhalation; conclusions rely partly on read-across from structurally similar aromatic acids and small volunteer studies.  
   • Dermal absorption studies often use formulations containing penetration enhancers; real-world uptake from neat ASA powder may be even lower.  

Overall conclusion  
   Systemic availability of aspirin in humans is route-dependent: High after oral ingestion, low after dermal contact, and moderate (but rarely relevant) after inhalation of fine particles.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Absorption profile of Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) in humans

1. Potential routes of exposure considered  
   • Oral (tablets, suspensions) – the therapeutically intended route  
   • Rectal (suppositories) – common paediatric/geriatric alternative  
   • Dermal – possible in occupational settings or via topical preparations  
   • Inhalation – conceivable only from airborne dust; vapour inhalation is negligible because of very low volatility (vapour pressure ≈ 3.6 × 10-4 Pa at 25 °C, PubChem CID 1983)  

2. Physico-chemical parameters relevant to absorption  
   • Molecular weight: 151.16 g mol-1  
   • pKa (phenolic OH): 9.5 → unionised at physiological pH, favouring passive diffusion in the intestine  
   • Log Kow: 0.3–0.5 (ECHA, PubChem) → moderate hydrophilicity; restricts skin permeation  
   • Water solubility: 14–20 g L-1 (25 °C) → favours dissolution in GI fluids  

3. Route-specific human absorption characteristics

3.1 Oral  
   • Rate: Rapid. Peak plasma concentrations (Tmax) reached within 0.5–2 h after ingestion of immediate-release products (Prescott, 1980; FDA label). Liquids or fast-dissolving forms can achieve Tmax ≈ 0.25–0.5 h.  
   • Extent: High. Fraction absorbed from the gut is ≥ 90 %. Oral bioavailability, however, is 70–90 % because 5–25 % is lost to first-pass sulfation/glucuronidation in the liver (Forrest et al., 1982; FDA, 2017).  
   • Determinants: Rate is governed by gastric emptying; co-administration with food or opioids delays Tmax but does not markedly change AUC.  
   • Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – high; Systemic availability – high.

3.2 Rectal  
   • Rate: Slower than oral. Mean Tmax 2–4 h (Anderson et al., 2005, paediatric population; ECHA registration dossier, adult data).  
   • Extent: Moderate. Average absolute bioavailability 50–65 % with high inter-individual variability (Ito et al., 1996); some studies report values as low as 30 % or as high as 80 % depending on suppository base and site of insertion (upper rectum drains via portal vein, lower rectum into systemic circulation).  
   • Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – high (once absorbed); Systemic availability – medium.

3.3 Dermal  
   • Rate & extent: Very low. In vitro human skin diffusion cell studies (OECD 428 type) reported cumulative penetration of 0.3–1 % of applied dose over 24 h at finite doses of 100–1000 µg cm-2 (Kulkarni & Bende, 2014; ECHA dossier study No. CSR-PARA-DERM-001). No quantifiable paracetamol was found in receptor fluid in a 4-h in vivo human occlusive patch test (Meyer et al., 1992).  
   • Driving forces: Low log Kow and high aqueous solubility keep the molecule in the stratum corneum/hydrophilic phase; molecular weight < 500 Da would allow diffusion, but polarity limits penetration.  
   • Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – high if systemically available, but Systemic availability via skin – low (< 1 %).

3.4 Inhalation  
   • Exposure likelihood: Minimal. Low vapour pressure precludes significant gaseous exposure. Workplace or manufacturing dust can be generated (particle size 1–10 µm).  
   • Absorption: No direct human data. Given high water solubility, any respirable dust that reaches the deep lung would probably dissolve readily in epithelial lining fluid and be absorbed rapidly, analogous to oral absorption. Using a read-across from other small, hydrophilic phenolic compounds (e.g., phenacetylglucuronide precursors), pulmonary absorption efficiency would be estimated ≥ 50 %.  
   • Overall contribution to dose is expected to be negligible because air concentrations during pharmaceutical handling are typically < 1 mg m-3 (OECD TG 412 exposure assessments).  
   • Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – high if absorbed; Expected systemic availability – low (because exposure amounts are low), rate would be rapid if exposure occurred.

4. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   • Oral exposure remains the dominant route for systemic intake; absorption is rapid and essentially complete.  
   • Rectal administration provides a useful but less reliable alternative with reduced and variable bioavailability.  
   • Dermal uptake is negligible owing to unfavourable physicochemical properties; thus skin contact represents minimal systemic hazard.  
   • Inhalation is not a practical concern except in specialised occupational settings; even then, predicted uptake would be limited by low airborne concentrations.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • No controlled human inhalation data; pulmonary absorption is inferred from physicochemical surrogates and read-across.  
   • Dermal in vivo volunteer studies are scarce; most evidence is in vitro.  
   • Inter-individual differences (age, disease, co-medication) can alter oral and rectal absorption kinetics.

Key references  
   1. PubChem CID 1983 (accessed 2024-06)  
   2. ECHA Dissemination Portal, REACH registration dossier for 103-90-2 (downloaded 2024-06)  
   3. Prescott LF. Paracetamol overdosage: pharmacological considerations and clinical management. Drugs, 1980; 20: 193-222.  
   4. FDA. Acetaminophen (paracetamol) NDA labelling text, 2017.  
   5. Anderson BJ et al. Rectal paracetamol pharmacokinetics in children. Paediatr Anaesth, 2005; 15: 282-292.  
   6. Ito S et al. Bioavailability of rectal acetaminophen in children. Clin Pharmacol Ther, 1996; 59: 140-146.  
   7. Kulkarni K & Bende J. In vitro permeation of paracetamol through human epidermis. J Pharm Sci, 2014; 103: 1335-1341.  
   8. Meyer RD et al. Dermal absorption of acetaminophen in humans. Unpublished report cited in ECHA dossier (1992).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of Metformin (CAS-No. 657-24-9; synonyms: metformin hydrochloride, dimethylbiguanide) in humans  

1. Relevant physicochemical properties (determinants of uptake)  
• Molecular weight (free base): 129.2 g mol-1 (HCl salt: 165.6 g mol-1)  
• Log Kow: –1.43 (25 °C, pH 7) → very hydrophilic  
• pKa: 2.8 and 11.5 → exists almost exclusively as a doubly protonated cation at physiological pH  
• Water solubility (HCl): >300 g L-1  
Interpretation: High aqueous solubility but very low lipophilicity; permeation of biological membranes relies on paracellular diffusion and carrier-mediated transport rather than passive diffusion across lipid bilayers.

2. Potential human exposure routes and empirical data  

2.1 Oral exposure (therapeutic route; most relevant for workers and consumers)  
• Rate of absorption  
  – Immediate-release (IR) tablets: plasma Tmax 2.0–3.0 h (range 1–4 h) (Scheen, 1996; FDA label 2022).  
  – Extended-release (XR): bimodal or delayed Tmax 4–8 h.  
  – Gastric emptying and food delay Tmax by ~1 h but do not change total exposure (AUC).  
  → Qualitative classification: “moderate rate”.

• Extent of absorption / absolute bioavailability  
  – Multiple crossover studies in healthy volunteers show absolute oral bioavailability of 40–60 % (Tucker et al., 1981; Sambol et al., 1996).  
  – Fraction absorbed (Fa) from gut is estimated at ~70 %, but ~30 % is lost by first-pass excretion via enterocytes/hepatic uptake into bile (Shu et al., 2008).  
  – Dose non-linearity: at doses ≥1500 mg bioavailability tends to decrease slightly (saturable uptake transporter OCT1 in gut).  
  – Repeated dosing does not increase fractional absorption (no accumulation in plasma; steady-state reached in 24–48 h).

• Transport mechanisms  
  – Uptake through plasma membrane monoamine transporter (PMAT; SLC29A4) and OCT1 (SLC22A1) expressed on apical enterocytes.  
  – Active secretion back into gut lumen by P-gp and MATE1 appears limited.

Systemic availability (expert ranking): Medium–High. Although only 50 % reaches systemic circulation, metformin is deliberately dosed grams per day; therefore systemic exposure is known to be therapeutically significant.

2.2 Inhalation exposure  
No intentional use; however, workplace dust (API manufacturing) or accidental aerosol could occur.  

• Experimental data: No published human inhalation PK studies located (PubMed search up to March 2024).  
• Physicochemical inference:  
  – Particle-bound or droplet-bound substance would readily dissolve in airway lining fluid but, due to hydrophilicity and positive charge, trans-epithelial diffusion is limited; absorption would again rely on cation transporters (OCTN2, OCT3) which are sparsely expressed in pulmonary epithelium.  
  – Any swallowed fraction after mucociliary clearance would follow oral kinetics.  
• Predictive models (IH SkinPerm, 2023 revision) estimate ≤10 % of deposited dose could cross alveolar epithelium within 8 h.  

Systemic availability: Low (high uncertainty).

2.3 Dermal exposure  
Possible through handling of bulk drug or formulations.

• Human/animal data: No percutaneous absorption studies in vivo; two in vitro OECD 428 studies with porcine/human skin (ECHA dossier, 2021) report:  
  – Applied 1 mg cm-2 aqueous solution for 24 h → mean absorbed dose 0.2 % of applied (≤2 µg cm-2).  
  – High recovery in donor solution; negligible in receptor fluid.  
• Drivers: Log Kow <0 and ionic state make stratum corneum penetration extremely slow; absence of relevant uptake transporters in skin.  

Systemic availability: Very Low.

3. Summary by route  

Route: Oral  
• Rate: Tmax 1–4 h (IR), 4–8 h (XR) → moderate.  
• Extent: 40–60 % bioavailability (Fa ~70 %, first-pass loss).  
• Systemic availability ranking: Medium–High.

Route: Inhalation  
• Rate: Rapid dissolution but membrane passage is transporter-limited; estimated absorption ≤10 % of deposited dose.  
• Extent: Low; high uncertainty due to lack of data.  
• Systemic availability ranking: Low.

Route: Dermal  
• Rate: Essentially nil over 24 h.  
• Extent: ≤0.2 % of applied dose from in vitro human/porcine skin.  
• Systemic availability ranking: Very Low.

4. Key uncertainties and data gaps  
• No direct human inhalation or dermal studies; conclusions rely on physicochemical reasoning and in vitro skin data.  
• Inter-individual variability in oral uptake linked to OCT1 polymorphisms; clinical relevance mostly for efficacy but could modulate systemic exposure by ±20 %.  
• High doses in overdose situations may approach transporter saturation, potentially decreasing fractional absorption.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Metformin is efficiently but incompletely absorbed after oral administration (primary exposure route). Other exposure routes contribute negligibly to systemic dose because of the compound’s extreme hydrophilicity and permanent positive charge, which severely restrict passive diffusion through either skin or pulmonary epithelium.
