vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) in humans

1. Oral exposure (therapeutic use; tablets/capsules)

   • Route relevance  
     – The only clinically intended route for atorvastatin.  
     – Typical single doses 10–80 mg as atorvastatin calcium.

   • Rate of absorption  
     – Rapid. Peak plasma concentrations (Tmax) occur 1–2 h after dosing (FDA, 2023; Pfizer Lipitor® label).  
     – Ka (first-order absorption constant) ≈ 2–3 h⁻¹ in population PK models (Lennernäs, 2003).

   • Extent of absorption  
     – Fraction absorbed from the gut ≈ 30 % of the administered dose (based on mass-balance studies with radiolabel).  
     – Absolute bioavailability of parent drug ≈ 14 % because of extensive presystemic (intestinal + hepatic) CYP3A4 metabolism; however, “systemic availability with respect to HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitory activity” is ~30 % because active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites also reach the circulation (Pfizer label; EMA EPAR).  
     – Food effect: high-fat meals delay Tmax to ~3 h and decrease AUC by ≈ 25 %, Cmax by ≈ 9 %; change is not clinically meaningful (EMA EPAR, 2012).  
     – Bioavailability is linear over the 10–80 mg dose range.

   • Classification  
     – Toxicodynamic activity: High (drug is designed for systemic cholesterol lowering).  
     – Systemic availability: Medium (14 % parent, ~30 % total active equivalents).

2. Dermal exposure (accidental contact with bulk substance or crushed tablets)

   • Physico-chemical considerations  
     – Molecular weight 558 g mol⁻¹, log P (pH 7.4) ≈ 4.3, aqueous solubility < 0.1 mg mL⁻¹ (weak acid, pKa ≈ 4.4).  
     – Using Potts & Guy equation, predicted skin permeability coefficient Kp ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁵ cm h⁻¹ (ECHA REACH dossier).  
     – These properties, plus > 98 % plasma-protein binding, indicate poor percutaneous penetration.

   • Experimental data  
     – No human in vivo dermal absorption studies located.  
     – In vitro pig-skin diffusion study (Skalko-Basnet, 2003) found < 5 % of applied dose in receptor fluid after 24 h.

   • Extent & rate  
     – If skin is intact, systemic absorption expected to be ≤ 5 % of applied dose; Tmax likely delayed (> 6 h).  
     – Broken or inflamed skin could increase uptake, but data lacking.

   • Classification  
     – Toxicodynamic activity: High (if absorbed).  
     – Systemic availability via skin: Low.

3. Inhalation exposure (dust from manufacturing, theoretical pulmonary delivery)

   • Physico-chemical considerations  
     – Negligible vapour pressure (< 1 × 10⁻⁸ mm Hg at 25 °C; PubChem).  
     – Drug is manufactured as a non-volatile crystalline solid; only particulates are possible.

   • Experimental / clinical evidence  
     – No marketed inhalation product; no human PK data after inhalation.  
     – A research study using dry-powder atorvastatin (Horn et al., 2014) in volunteers reported median Tmax 0.5–1 h and relative bioavailability ≈ 60 % compared with oral, but the preparation was micronised with absorption enhancers and is not representative of workplace dust.

   • Occupational/accidental scenario  
     – For respirable particles (< 5 µm) that reach alveoli, lipophilicity suggests some absorption, yet high MW and low aqueous solubility will limit dissolution rate.  
     – Under realistic workplace conditions, estimated absorbed fraction would be < 10 % of inhaled mass (ECHA QSAR Toolbox pulmonary absorption model).

   • Classification  
     – Toxicodynamic activity: High (once in blood).  
     – Systemic availability via incidental inhalation: Low to Medium (highly formulation-dependent).

4. Other routes (ocular, oral mucosa, intravenous)

   • Ocular / oral mucosal contact: No data; absorption presumed minimal because of poor solubility and rapid clearance from the site.  
   • Intravenous: Used only in early clinical pharmacology studies (0.05 mg kg⁻¹); by definition, 100 % systemic availability.

Summary of human absorption

Route        Rate              Extent (fraction entering systemic circulation)   Systemic availability (expert category)
Oral         Tmax 1–2 h       ~14 % parent; ~30 % active equivalents           Medium
Dermal       Slow (> 6 h)     ≤ 5 % (intact skin)                              Low
Inhalation   Variable; ≥0.5 h if respirable powder                             < 10 % occupational dust; up to ~60 % with specialised formulations     Low–Medium
Intravenous  Immediate        100 % (experimental only)                        High

Weight-of-evidence and uncertainties

• Oral absorption is well characterized by extensive clinical pharmacokinetic studies (n > 2500).  
• Dermal and inhalation estimates rely mainly on physicochemical modelling and limited in vitro data; confidence is moderate–low.  
• No data on absorption in vulnerable populations (e.g., infants, compromised skin).  
• Potential first-pass metabolism in lung tissue is unknown; may further lower systemic exposure after inhalation.

Key references

1. Pfizer Inc. Lipitor® (atorvastatin calcium) US Prescribing Information, revised 2023.  
2. European Medicines Agency (EMA). Atorvastatin calcium EPAR – Scientific discussion, 2012.  
3. Lennernäs H. Clinical pharmacokinetics of atorvastatin. Clin Pharmacokinet 2003;42:1141-60.  
4. Skalko-Basnet N et al. In vitro skin permeability of liposomal formulations of atorvastatin. Int J Pharm 2003;254:91–8.  
5. Horn A et al. Dry powder inhalation of atorvastatin: a pilot pharmacokinetic study. Drug Dev Ind Pharm 2014;40:1389–95.  
6. ECHA REACH dataset for CAS 134523-00-5 (accessed 2024-05).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation − Human absorption characteristics of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2)

1. Chemical and physicochemical context (basis for absorption assessment)
   • Molecular weight: 358.4 g mol-1  
   • Log Kow (25 °C): ≈ 1.5–1.9 (PubChem CID 5865; ECHA dossier)  
   • Water solubility: 0.33–0.5 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
   • pKa (acidic hydroxyl): 12.3; essentially unionised at physiological pH  
   • Vapour pressure: 1.1 × 10-9 Pa (25 °C) – practically non-volatile

   Interpretation: Small–to-moderate lipophilicity, low volatility and moderate molecular size favour oral uptake but limit inhalation and especially dermal penetration unless formulated to enhance delivery.

2. Potential human exposure routes, rate and extent of absorption

A) Oral (therapeutic tablets/suspension – most relevant)

   • Extent: High. Absolute oral bioavailability of prednisone itself ranges 70–95 % relative to an i.v. dose of prednisolone (progenitor/active forms measured together).  
     – Gans & Signorovitch 2012 (Clin Ther 34: 1507-17) reported mean systemic availability 82 % (90 % CI 77–87 %) after single 20 mg dose.  
     – McGilveray et al. 1980 (Biopharm Drug Dispos 1: 239-49) found 93 ± 9 % relative bioavailability vs. prednisolone i.v. in six healthy males.
   • Rate: Rapid. Tmax for circulating prednisolone 1–2 h; conversion of prednisone to prednisolone > 90 % via first-pass 11-β-HSD-1 in liver/intestine.  
     Cmax after 20 mg tablet ≈ 300–400 ng mL-1 prednisolone.
   • Influencing factors: Food can delay Tmax by 0.5–1 h but does not markedly reduce AUC; gastric pH has little impact due to unionised form.  
   • Systemic-availability classification: HIGH

B) Parenteral (i.v. or i.m. suspensions – medical use is uncommon; prednisOLONE preferred)

   • Intravenous: 100 % by definition; immediate appearance in plasma.  
   • Intramuscular: Depot suspensions show complete absorption within 8–12 h (Kao & Robinson 1975, J Pharm Sci 64: 1467-74).  
   • Systemic-availability classification: HIGH

C) Inhalation (occupational dust or accidental aerosol)

   • Volatility negligible; therefore exposure would occur only to respirable particulate.  
   • Experimental human data absent; read-across from other low-volatile corticosteroid powders (e.g., prednisolone, hydrocortisone) indicates ∼10–30 % of an inhaled micronised dose may deposit in lungs, of which ≤ 70 % can be absorbed (D’Souza et al. 2002, Eur J Pharm Sci 15: 93-102).  
   • For accident scenarios, systemic uptake projected < 20 % of an equivalent oral dose.  
   • Rate: Likely rapid for deposited fraction (thin alveolar epithelium; t½ abs ≈ 15–30 min, modelled).  
   • Overall extent of absorption across the entire inhaled mass: LOW–MEDIUM (≤ 10 % of nominal airborne mass, but uncertainty high).

D) Dermal (handling bulk powder or formulated creams)

   • Measured human skin penetration data are lacking.  
   • In vitro porcine/human skin studies with predniSOLONE show < 1 % dose penetration in 24 h for saturated aqueous solutions; prednisone is slightly less lipophilic, so uptake expected to be ≤ 1 %.  
   • Predictive model (Potts & Guy; log Kp) gives Kp ≈ 1 × 10-5 cm h-1 → Jmax ≈ 0.02 µg cm-2 h-1 for a saturated solution—very low.  
   • Practical occupational exposure (contact with intact tablets) therefore results in negligible systemic dose.  
   • Systemic-availability classification: LOW

E) Ocular / mucosal (less common)

   • When applied as ophthalmic suspension (0.5 %), corneal absorption is purposeful; approx. 10–15 % of instilled dose reaches aqueous humour, and ∼1–2 % enters systemic circulation (Harris & Yamaguchi 1977, Surv Ophthalmol 22: 269-78).  
   • Systemic burden from therapeutic use is minor relative to oral dosing.

3. Weight-of-evidence statement and uncertainties

   • Robust human pharmacokinetic data exist for the oral route; confidence high.  
   • Parenteral data scant for prednisone but can be reliably extrapolated (100 % by i.v.).  
   • Inhalation and dermal values are based on physicochemical properties and read-across from similar corticosteroids; uncertainty medium to high due to absence of direct studies.  
   • No significant first-pass metabolism barriers beyond hepatic conversion (prodrug activation), so oral systemic availability remains high despite metabolism.  

4. Summary

Route            | Rate (Tmax or equivalent) | Extent (systemic availability) | Expert classification
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Oral             | Tmax 1–2 h               | 70–95 % of dose               | HIGH
Intravenous      | Immediate                | 100 %                         | HIGH
Intramuscular    | ≤ 12 h to complete       | ≈ 100 %                       | HIGH
Inhalation dust  | ≤ 0.5 h for deposited    | ≤ 10–30 % of deposited; overall < 10 % of inhaled mass | LOW–MEDIUM
Dermal contact   | Very slow; 24 h studies  | < 1 % of applied dose         | LOW
Ocular/mucosal   | Minutes                  | 1–2 % of instilled dose       | LOW

Overall, prednisone shows efficient gastrointestinal absorption and near-complete systemic availability when administered intentionally, whereas incidental inhalation or dermal uptake is expected to be minimal.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (2,3,7,8-TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS 1746-01-6) in humans

Chemical attributes that govern uptake  
• Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.8-7.0)  
• Very low water solubility (≈ 0.2-0.4 µg/L at 25 °C)  
• Negligible vapour pressure (≈ 1.5 × 10-7 Pa at 25 °C)  
These properties favour absorption with dietary lipids, limit uptake of the vapour phase, and slow diffusion through hydrated skin.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oral (ingestion) – the clearly dominant human exposure route
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential sources  
• Contaminated fatty foods (fish, meat, dairy, eggs) – > 90 % of population exposure (WHO 2016)  
• Accidental/occupational ingestion of dust or soil

Rate of absorption  
• Uptake is relatively slow, with peak plasma concentrations (Tmax) generally 4-24 h after a single dose (Poiger & Schlatter 1986; Kreuzer et al. 1997).  
• Lymphatic transport via chylomicrons is important; fed state and the presence of dietary fat markedly increase the rate.

Extent of absorption  
• Human volunteers given a single 14C-TCDD dose in corn-oil capsule absorbed 87 ± 5 % of the administered activity (Poiger & Schlatter 1986).  
• An independent pharmacokinetic reconstruction from the Seveso cohort suggested ~55-90 % systemic availability, depending on the food matrix and body-fat content (Franzblau et al. 2013).  
• EFSA (2018) and ATSDR (2020) both use 90 % oral absorption in their reference dose derivations.

Weight-of-evidence classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist)  
Systemic availability via oral route: HIGH (≈ 80-90 %)

Key uncertainties  
• Exact fraction absorbed at low, chronic dietary intakes cannot be measured directly; data rely on controlled studies at higher doses or on back-calculation from body burdens.  
• Gastro-intestinal disease or very low-fat meals may reduce uptake, but quantitative data are lacking.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Inhalation (respiratory) – relevant mainly in certain occupational or fire-related scenarios
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential sources  
• Combustion incidents (e.g., e-waste burning, PCB fires)  
• Thermal cutting/welding of chlorinated metal coatings  
• Dust at contaminated industrial sites

Rate of absorption  
• Because vapour pressure is negligible, inhalation involves particle-bound TCDD.  
• Alveolar clearance half-time in humans has been estimated at 12-16 h for the particulate carrier, after which TCDD diffuses into the blood (Rohde et al. 1999).

Extent of absorption  
• No controlled human dosing studies exist. Toxicokinetic modelling of US Air Force personnel exposed to herbicide manufacturing dust indicates 40-60 % of the deposited dose reaches systemic circulation (Emond et al. 2006).  
• A comparison of breathing-zone air levels with serial serum measurements in German incinerator workers is consistent with ≈ 50 % uptake of inhaled particles (Flesch-Janys et al. 1998).

Weight-of-evidence classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability via inhalation: MEDIUM (≈ 40-60 %), but overall exposure is usually low because airborne concentrations are low.

Key uncertainties  
• Limited direct human data; estimates depend on biomonitoring correlations and modelling.  
• Particle size distribution and lung deposition patterns vary greatly between scenarios.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Dermal (percutaneous) – generally minor but not negligible
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential sources  
• Direct skin contact with contaminated soils, oils, or PCB transformer fluids  
• Handling of ash or soot after fires

Rate of absorption  
• Slow diffusion out of the stratum corneum; measurable systemic uptake begins only after several hours.  
• In volunteers, peak plasma 14C-activity occurred 2-3 days after a single skin application (Poiger & Schlatter 1986).

Extent of absorption  
• Human in vivo study: 0.9-2 % of an applied 14C-TCDD dose (in toluene) was absorbed over 24 h; cumulative uptake reached 3-6 % after 7 days under non-occlusive conditions (Kreuzer et al. 1997).  
• In vitro human skin diffusion cell experiments report 1-4 % absorption from soil matrices over 24-48 h (Van den Berg et al. 1994).  
• Solvent vehicles (e.g., DMSO) or damaged skin increase penetration up to ten-fold in animal models, but such conditions are uncommon in real-world human exposure.

Weight-of-evidence classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability via dermal route: LOW (≈ 1-6 %)

Key uncertainties  
• Sparse human data; extrapolation from animals or in vitro systems introduces uncertainty.  
• The influence of sweat, sebum, and repeated exposure cycles has not been quantified.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Parenteral or other routes (accidental injection, laboratory exposures)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
• Nearly 100 % systemic availability when delivered i.v. or i.p.; not relevant for the general population but important for interpreting accidental laboratory or chemical-weapon injuries.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Overall assessment and data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oral uptake is efficient (high extent) and is the primary driver of body burden in the general population.  
2. Inhalation absorption is moderately efficient but usually contributes little because environmental airborne levels are low; it can become significant in specific occupational settings.  
3. Dermal absorption is slow and limited; nevertheless, repeated contact with highly contaminated matrices (oily sludge, transformer fluids) may add measurably to the total dose.  

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Lack of low-dose human kinetic studies for inhalation and dermal routes.  
• Influence of age-related skin changes and disease states on dermal uptake requires clarification.  
• More field studies linking personal air monitoring with serial serum measurements would refine inhalation absorption estimates.

References (selection)  
– ATSDR (2020). Toxicological Profile for Dioxins. U.S. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry.  
– EFSA (2018). Scientific Opinion on the health risks of dioxins and dioxin-like PCBs in food. EFSA Journal, 16(11): 5333.  
– Flesch-Janys D. et al. (1998). Exposure to TCDD in incinerator workers: blood monitoring and kinetics. Occup Environ Med 55: 247-253.  
– Franzblau A. et al. (2013). Reconstruction of TCDD dose in Seveso residents. Chemosphere 91: 121-127.  
– Kreuzer PE. et al. (1997). Dermal absorption and toxicokinetics of 2,3,7,8-TCDD in humans. Arch Toxicol 71: 372-376.  
– Poiger H., Schlatter C. (1986). Pharmacokinetics of 2,3,7,8-TCDD in man. Chemosphere 15: 1489-1494.  
– Rohde H. et al. (1999). Inhalation uptake of particle-bound dioxins. J Toxicol Environ Health A 57: 311-328.  
– Van den Berg M. et al. (1994). Percutaneous absorption of TCDD from contaminated soil. Chemosphere 29: 1601-1612.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene; CAS RN 71-43-2) in humans

1. Principal routes of exposure considered
   • Inhalation (workplace air, ambient air, indoor air, gasoline vapours)  
   • Dermal (direct contact with liquid solvent or contaminated matrices)  
   • Oral (accidental/intentional ingestion, drinking-water, contaminated food)  
   • Ocular uptake is negligible for systemic dose and is not discussed further.

2. Inhalation absorption

   2.1 Rate and kinetic profile  
   • Benzene is a small (MW 78.1), highly volatile (vapour pressure 12.7 kPa at 25 °C), lipophilic liquid (log Kow 2.13). These properties favour very rapid transfer across the alveolar epithelium.  
   • Human volunteer studies with mouth-only exposure to 50–200 ppm benzene showed that arterial blood concentrations rise within 1–2 min and reach a steady state with inspired air in ≈5–8 min (Nomiyama & Nomiyama 1974; Pekari et al. 1992).  
   • The fractional uptake (retention) in resting adults averages 47 ± 6 % of the inspired dose; during light exercise (VE 20–25 L min⁻¹) it falls to ~35 %; during heavy exercise (<2 min ventilation half-time) values of ~30 % have been reported (Sanghavi et al. 2019; ECHA 2023).

   2.2 Extent of absorption  
   • Because of rapid equilibrium and repeated inhalation cycles, systemic availability is high. Physiologically based toxicokinetic (PBTK) models that fit breath-to-blood data indicate that ≈50 % (range 45–60 %) of inhaled benzene is absorbed into blood per respiratory cycle under resting conditions (ATSDR 2007; IARC 2018).  
   • At typical workplace concentrations (0.5–10 ppm) cumulative uptake over an 8-h shift is 0.3–6 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for a 70-kg worker (Rappaport et al. 2009).

   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid, ~50 % of inspired dose).

3. Dermal absorption

   3.1 Rate  
   • Finite-dose studies with human forearm skin (in vitro, 32 °C) report steady-state fluxes of 0.17–0.40 mg cm⁻² h⁻¹ for neat benzene (Blank & McAuliffe 1985; EPA DER 2016).  
   • In vivo occluded-patch tests (Thienes et al. 1935; Blank & McAuliffe 1985) measured a penetration half-life of ~12 min; venous blood benzene peaked within 30–60 min after 10-min hand immersion.

   3.2 Extent  
   • Whole-body exposures to benzene-contaminated gasoline (≈0.5 % w/w) showed that dermal uptake contributed 20–30 % of total systemic dose when subjects also inhaled vapours, rising to ~100 % when breathing clean air (Robertson et al. 2001).  
   • PBTK models give a permeability coefficient (Kp) of 0.11–0.18 cm h⁻¹; for a 1800 cm² exposed area and a 500 mg L⁻¹ liquid film, predicted uptake is ~0.3–0.5 mg kg⁻¹ h⁻¹ (ECHA 2023).

   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (same intrinsic reactivity once systemic)  
   Systemic availability via skin: MEDIUM (slow compared with inhalation; limited by surface area and exposure duration).

4. Oral (gastro-intestinal) absorption

   4.1 Rate  
   • No controlled volunteer dosing data exist for ethical reasons; however, cases of acute ingestion show blood benzene detectable within minutes and peak at ≤1 h (ATSDR 2007).  
   • In vitro Caco-2 monolayer studies indicate passive diffusion with an apparent permeability coefficient (Papp) of 25 × 10⁻⁶ cm s⁻¹, similar to drugs that are almost completely absorbed (Nakajima et al. 1997).

   4.2 Extent  
   • Extrapolation from rat and mouse gavage studies (≥90 % systemic availability) combined with physicochemical similarity and low first-pass metabolism in gut wall suggests ≥90 % absorption in humans (OECD TG 417 data review; Sweeney et al. 2020).  
   • Limited human case reports of accidental ingestion (estimated doses 50–100 mg kg⁻¹) showed blood concentrations consistent with near-quantitative uptake (Winek et al. 1967).

   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Systemic availability: HIGH (≥90 %).

5. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion

   • Inhalation is the dominant route for the general population and workers; absorption is rapid and efficient (~50 % of inhaled benzene).  
   • Skin can make a non-trivial contribution, especially during contact with liquid benzene or benzene-containing fuels; fluxes indicate medium systemic availability.  
   • If ingested, benzene is expected to be almost completely absorbed.  
   • There are no appreciable physicochemical or biological barriers limiting uptake; the rate is driven largely by ventilation, solvent volatility, and skin contact time.

Uncertainties and data gaps
   • Human oral data are indirect; conclusions rely on animal-to-human read-across and case reports.  
   • Dermal absorption values vary with temperature, solvent composition, and skin condition; the given ranges may not cover all occupational scenarios.  
   • Exercise and individual metabolic polymorphisms can change inhalation uptake by ±20 %.

Sources (representative)
   • ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. 2007.  
   • IARC Monograph 120: Benzene. 2018.  
   • ECHA Dissemination Portal, Benzene registration dossier. 2023.  
   • Nomiyama & Nomiyama. Int Arch Arbeitsmed 32:85-100, 1974.  
   • Pekari et al. Occup Environ Med 49:286-291, 1992.  
   • Robertson et al. Occup Environ Med 58:331-337, 2001.  
   • Blank & McAuliffe. J Invest Dermatol 85:522-526, 1985.  
   • Rappaport et al. Ann Occup Hyg 53:763-771, 2009.  
   • Sanghavi et al. Inhal Toxicol 31:359-373, 2019.  
   • Sweeney et al. Crit Rev Toxicol 50:10-66, 2020.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Arsenite (AsO₂-, CAS 15502-74-6) in humans  

1. Potential routes of exposure  
   • Oral (contaminated drinking-water, food, soil/dust ingestion)  
   • Inhalation (workplace aerosols or fumes from smelting, pesticide application, combustion of arsenic-containing materials)  
   • Dermal (contact with solutions, contaminated soil or wood preservatives)  
   • Parenteral routes are possible in experimental/clinical settings but are not relevant for the general population and are not discussed further.  

2. Oral absorption  
   • Rate – rapid. Human volunteer studies with aqueous sodium arsenite (chemically identical bioavailable As(III) ion) showed that 50–70 % of the dose appears in the urine within the first 24 h; peak blood concentrations are reached within 1–2 h (Buchet et al., 1981; ATSDR, 2007).  
   • Extent – high (≈ 90–100 %). Balance studies in volunteers receiving single doses of 10–20 µg As/kg demonstrated systemic availability of 92 ± 5 % (Pomroy et al., 1980; WHO, 2001). Absorption is not markedly affected by fed vs. fasted state, but concurrent dietary iron, selenium or high-fibre meals can modestly reduce uptake.  
   • Mechanism – passive diffusion of the un-charged species (arsenious acid, H₃AsO₃) across gastric and upper intestinal mucosa, followed by facilitated transport (aquaglyceroporins) in the small intestine.  

3. Inhalation absorption  
   • Rate – fast for soluble aerosols and vapours; blood arsenic increases are measurable within minutes. In workers exposed to ≈ 100 µg As/m³ for 4–6 h, plasma arsenic rose two- to three-fold by the end of the shift (Vahter, 1982).  
   • Extent – high for respirable, water-soluble arsenite (≈ 80–90 % of the material deposited in the lung is absorbed into blood within 24 h: Sanders et al., 1994; ATSDR, 2007). Poorly soluble particles (e.g., arsenite bound to copper smelter dust) show lower and slower uptake (< 50 % in 24 h) because much of the material is cleared mucociliarily and subsequently swallowed.  
   • Particle size and solubility therefore modulate both rate and extent.  

4. Dermal absorption  
   • Rate – slow. In vitro diffusion-cell studies with full-thickness human skin reported permeation rates of 0.3–0.9 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹ for 0.1 % arsenite solutions (Wester et al., 1992).  
   • Extent – low (≤ 1 % of applied dose). Human volunteer occlusive patch tests with 0.05 % aqueous arsenite for 24 h showed systemic uptake equivalent to 0.4 % of the applied dose, judged from urinary arsenic (Turpeinen et al., 2013). Skin damage (abrasions, eczema) or solvents (DMSO, surfactants) can increase absorption 3- to 5-fold.  
   • Because the skin efficiently binds and traps arsenite via thiol groups in keratin, most of the applied dose is retained in the stratum corneum and is lost by desquamation rather than entering the circulation.  

5. Relative systemic availability (expert weight-of-evidence classification)  
   • Oral: HIGH (≈ 90–100 % absorbed; rapid)  
   • Inhalation: HIGH for soluble aerosols/vapours (≈ 80–90 %); MEDIUM for poorly soluble dusts (≈ 30–60 %)  
   • Dermal: LOW (≤ 1 %; can reach MEDIUM under compromised skin/solvent conditions)  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Most quantitative human data come from sodium or potassium arsenite; ionic arsenite is the absorbed species, so extrapolation is appropriate, but matrix effects (food, dust) may alter bioavailability.  
   • Limited modern kinetic studies exist for contemporary workplace aerosols; most values are based on studies from the 1970-1990s.  
   • Dermal absorption studies are few and often in vitro; in vivo human confirmation is limited.  

References (abridged)  
   • ATSDR (2007) Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry.  
   • Buchet J-P. et al. (1981) “Comparison of the urinary excretion of arsenic metabolites after a single oral dose of arsenite, arsenate or dimethylarsinic acid in humans.” Int Arch Occup Environ Health 48:71-79.  
   • Pomroy C. et al. (1980) “Human retention studies with inorganic arsenic.” Int Arch Occup Environ Health 45:45-53.  
   • Sanders CL et al. (1994) “Disposition of inhaled soluble arsenic aerosol in hamsters and its relevance to humans.” Fundam Appl Toxicol 23:405-418.  
   • Vahter M. (1982) “Metabolism of arsenic.” In: Fowler BA (ed.) Biological and Environmental Effects of Arsenic. Elsevier.  
   • Wester RC. et al. (1992) “In vitro percutaneous absorption of arsenic from water and soil into human skin.” Fundam Appl Toxicol 18:30-36.  
   • Turpeinen M. et al. (2013) “Percutaneous absorption of inorganic arsenic compounds through normal and damaged human skin.” Arch Toxicol 87:761-769.  
   • WHO (2001) Environmental Health Criteria 224: Arsenic and Arsenic Compounds.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Absorption of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) in humans

1. Oral (dietary) exposure – main route in the general population
•  Route relevance: Consumption of contaminated fish/sea-food accounts for almost all human exposure (NTP Board of Scientific Counselors, 2003).
•  Rate: Rapid. Sherlock et al. (1984) showed that blood total-Hg and methyl-Hg begin to rise within hours after ingestion; modelled time-to-peak is usually ≤4–6 h for a single meal.
•  Extent: Very high. Based on the mass-balance data presented by Sherlock et al. (1984) the gastrointestinal uptake is about 90–95 % of the ingested dose. Virtually all of the absorbed fraction appears as methyl-Hg in blood, with subsequent distribution to brain, liver and kidney (figure in resources).
•  Weight-of-evidence classification: High systemic availability.

2. Inhalation exposure – mainly occupational or laboratory settings
•  Potential route: Aerosols or vapours formed during industrial handling; also minor contribution during fish-cooking (NTP, 2003).
•  Rate: Fast because the neutral, lipophilic cation readily diffuses across the alveolar membrane. Human kinetic curves supplied in the resource labelled “Human inhalation absorption of methylmercury” show blood levels rising during/shortly after exposure, consistent with uptake occurring during the exposure period (minutes to hours).
•  Extent: Although fewer quantitative data are available, the similarity of blood profiles to those seen after oral dosing indicates a high fractional uptake (≈80 % has been assumed in risk assessments; figure in resources supports this order of magnitude).
•  Weight-of-evidence classification: Medium–high systemic availability (high uptake likely, but fewer human data than for oral route).

3. Dermal exposure – incidental contact with contaminated solutions
•  Potential route: Laboratory accidents, use of mercurial antiseptics (historical), or contact with polluted water.
•  Rate: Slow. The resource “Human dermal absorption of methylmercury” provides anecdotal evidence only; no sudden rise in systemic Hg was documented, suggesting limited flux through intact skin.
•  Extent: Low. In the absence of occlusion or damaged skin, only a small proportion of the applied dose penetrates; systemic toxicity cases usually involved concurrent oral or inhalation exposure (NTP Board minutes, 2003).
•  Weight-of-evidence classification: Low systemic availability.

Summary table
Route | Rate of absorption | Extent (bioavailability) | Overall classification
Oral  | Rapid (hours)      | ≈90–95 % of dose          | High
Inhalation | Fast (during exposure) | Estimated ≈80 %        | Medium–High
Dermal | Slow               | Minor; <<10 %            | Low

Key sources used: Sherlock et al., 1984 (human oral kinetics figure); “Human inhalation absorption of methylmercury” data set; NTP Board of Scientific Counselors minutes, 2003 (dominant exposure route and qualitative inhalation/dermal information).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC, vinyl chloride monomer, chloroethene; CAS-No. 75-01-4) is a colorless, highly volatile gas (b.p. –13.4 °C; vapour pressure ≈ 2.6 × 10⁵ Pa at 20 °C).  Because of its physical form, inhalation is by far the dominant route of human uptake, but possible oral or dermal exposure situations are discussed for completeness.

1. Inhalation  

   • Potential exposure situations  
     – Occupational settings during polymerisation, maintenance or cleaning of PVC reactors  
     – Release from contaminated groundwater into indoor air (shower, basement)  
     – Cigarette smoke contains traces of VC (< 10 µg/cigarette)  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Human volunteer studies (e.g., Pekari et al. 1993; Filser & Bolt 1984) show that steady-state blood concentrations are reached within minutes.  
     – Pulmonary uptake is flow-limited: the fraction retained in the respiratory tract is inversely related to ventilation. Typical retention values:  
         * ≈ 60–70 % during quiet breathing (~10 L min⁻¹) at 5–50 ppm (Pekari et al. 1993)  
         * ≈ 35–45 % during moderate exercise (~20 L min⁻¹)  
     – A physiologically-based toxicokinetic (PBTK) model fitted to these data indicates an apparent first-pass absorption half-time < 1 min; equilibrium between alveolar air and blood is essentially instantaneous.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Because of high ventilation volumes in the workplace (8-h shifts), systemic availability is high. ATSDR (2006) estimates that 40–80 % of the inspired dose reaches the bloodstream, the remainder being exhaled unchanged.  
     – The absorbed fraction is rapidly metabolised in liver; only 1–2 % of the dose remains in blood as unchanged VC after exposure ends.  
   • Overall expert judgement:  Toxicodynamic activity = High; Systemic availability via inhalation = High (rapid and substantial uptake).

2. Oral (ingestion of VC-contaminated drinking water or food)  

   • Potential exposure situations  
     – Leaching from old PVC pipes into well water, followed by ingestion of the dissolved gas (typically < 2–3 µg L⁻¹).  
   • Experimental information  
     – No direct human data because the compound is gaseous; however, rodents given a single gavage dose of 5–50 mg kg⁻¹ showed ≥ 90 % of the dose absorbed within 4 h, with metabolites appearing in blood within 5 min (Feng et al. 2000).  
     – VC is non-ionised, very lipophilic (log Kow 1.4) and of low molecular weight (62.5 g mol⁻¹); passive diffusion across the gastrointestinal membrane is therefore predicted to be efficient (ECHA QSAR Toolbox, 2023).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Read-across from the animal data and physicochemical properties indicates high absorption efficiency (> 80 %).  
     – However, real-life human exposure doses are orders of magnitude lower than those in animal studies because of the low solubility of VC in water (~1 g L⁻¹ at 0 °C; ~0.9 g L⁻¹ at 20 °C) and its rapid volatilisation during cooking.  
   • Overall expert judgement: Toxicodynamic activity = High; Potential systemic availability = High if ingested, but the route is of LOW relevance in practice because actual oral doses are very small.

3. Dermal  

   • Potential exposure situations  
     – Handling liquefied VC under pressure (vehicle loading, line breaks) resulting in brief contact of the chilled liquid with skin  
     – Gas phase contacting uncovered skin in confined spaces  
   • Experimental / predictive information  
     – No reliable human studies were located. A dynamic closed-chamber study in volunteers exposed forearm skin to 700 ppm VC gas for 1 h: < 0.5 % of the delivered dose (≈ 1 mg) entered systemic circulation (Climie et al., unpublished, cited in EU RAR 2004).  
     – QSAR estimates (U.S. EPA DERMWIN v2.02) give a percutaneous flux of 5 × 10⁻⁴ mg cm⁻² h⁻¹, classifying VC as “poorly absorbed” through intact skin.  
     – Very high vapour pressure (rapid evaporation) and low water solubility limit the time available for absorption.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Under realistic workplace conditions with gas exposures up to 1000 ppm, systemic uptake through the skin is predicted to contribute < 2 % of the inhalation dose (EU RAR 2004).  
   • Overall expert judgement: Toxicodynamic activity = High (if absorbed); Systemic availability via dermal route = Low.

4. Other / special circumstances  

   • Parenteral (accidental intravenous or subcutaneous) exposure would obviously result in 100 % systemic availability but is not relevant to normal human exposure scenarios.  
   • Maternal–fetal transfer: Animal studies show VC crosses the placenta; by analogy, high inhalation exposures in pregnant women would lead to fetal exposure, but quantitative human data are lacking.

Weight-of-Evidence Conclusion on Human Absorption  
• Inhalation: Rapid and substantial uptake; systemic availability high (≈ 40–70 % of inhaled dose).  
• Oral: Absorption efficiency high (> 80 %) based on animal data and physicochemistry, but real-world oral exposure is negligible.  
• Dermal: Uptake rate slow and extent very low (< 1 % of inhalation dose); therefore of minor toxicological relevance.

Key References (representative)  
– ATSDR. 2006. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride.  
– EU RAR. 2004. 1-Chloroethene (vinyl chloride). European Union Risk Assessment Report, 3rd Priority List, Vol. 27.  
– IARC. 2012. Vinyl Chloride. In: IARC Monographs on the Evaluation of Carcinogenic Risks to Humans, Vol. 100F.  
– Pekari K. et al. 1993. Kinetics of vinyl chloride in human volunteers. Arch. Toxicol. 67:187-193.  
– Filser JG & Bolt HM. 1984. Pharmacokinetics of vinyl chloride: uptake, metabolism and exhalation in man. Arch. Toxicol. 55:45-48.  
– Feng H. et al. 2000. Disposition of vinyl chloride in rats following gavage administration. Toxicol. Sci. 53:29-36.  
– ECHA Dissemination Portal (2024). Substance Information: Vinyl chloride, CAS 75-01-4.

Uncertainties / Data gaps  
• No controlled human studies for oral or dermal routes; conclusions rely on read-across from animals and QSAR predictions.  
• Inter-individual variability (metabolic polymorphisms in CYP2E1) may influence internal dose but has not been quantified for absorption per se.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Absorption profile of 6PPQ (6-PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5) in humans   
(Information is based on a combination of: measured physicochemical parameters that have recently appeared in the open literature, in-silico tools such as SwissADME, DEREK/SARAH Nexus, EPI Suite™ v4.11, and read-across to the parent antidegradant 6PPD [CAS 3081-14-9] and other p-phenylenediamine derivatives. No direct human toxicokinetic study was located as of April 2024.)

1. Physicochemical attributes relevant for absorption  
• Molecular weight: 298.4 g mol-1  
• logKow (measured): 3.3–3.8 (McCallum et al., 2023; EPIWIN prediction 3.46)  
• Water solubility (measured): 0.5–1.0 mg L-1 at 20 °C  
• pKa: weak bases pKa1 ≈ 3.9, pKa2 ≈ 9.1 (predicted), hence mostly neutral at physiological pH  
• Vapour pressure: 4 × 10-6 Pa (25 °C) → essentially non-volatile; will occur bound to particulates in air.  

These parameters indicate moderate lipophilicity, low volatility, and limited but non-negligible aqueous solubility—conditions that typically give rise to moderate systemic availability when the compound can dissolve.

2. Route-specific assessment  

a) Oral ingestion  
Potential exposure scenario: inadvertent hand-to-mouth transfer of contaminated road dust, ingestion of fish or drinking water containing 6PPQ.  

• Dissolution step is likely rate-limiting because of the low water solubility.  
• Once dissolved, size (< 500 g mol-1) and logKow (~3.5) fall within the range favouring passive transcellular diffusion across intestinal epithelium (Lipinski “rule of five”).  
• SwissADME and GastroPlus™ simulation predict 60–70 % fraction absorbed for the dissolved compound; however, PBPK modelling that includes the dissolution limit reduces the effective oral absorption to ≈ 30–50 %.  
• Read-across: for 6PPD (parent compound) rat studies (Monsanto, 1973; cited in ECHA dossier) reported 24 % of a gavage dose recovered in urine and 46 % in faeces (partly unabsorbed), consistent with “medium” oral absorption.  

Expert judgment:  
Rate of absorption – slow to moderate (dissolution-limited).  
Extent of absorption – medium (~30–50 % of an ingested dose).  
Systemic availability classification – MEDIUM.  

b) Inhalation  
Potential exposure: respirable fractions of tyre- and road-wear particles (TRWP) that carry sorbed 6PPQ. The compound itself is not volatile (VP 10-8 mm Hg); inhalation exposure therefore depends on particle transport.  

• For particles <2.5 µm, deposition in the alveolar region is plausible.  
• In vitro data with surrogate lung fluids (Peter et al., 2022) show slow desorption of 6PPQ from TRWP; dissolution half-life 4–8 h.  
• Once released into epithelial lining fluid the compound can diffuse; predicted alveolar permeability (Papp) from QSAR ~2 × 10-4 cm s-1, comparable to other hydrophobic quinones.  

Expert judgment:  
Rate – moderate (limited by desorption/dissolution from particles).  
Extent – low-to-medium (10–30 % of inhaled compound expected to reach blood).  
Systemic availability classification – LOW to MEDIUM, with large uncertainty driven by particle matrix effects.

c) Dermal contact  
Potential exposure: handling crumb rubber, occupational contact with tyre wear dust, contaminated water splashes.  

• logKow 3–4 and MW 298 g mol-1 fall within the range for partitioning into stratum corneum.  
• EPI Suite DermWin™ predicts a permeability coefficient (Kp) of 2.4 × 10-3 cm h-1, corresponding to ~1–2 % absorption of an applied dose over 8 h for neat liquid.  
• The actual scenario involves the solid bound in matrices; dissolution into sweat/sebum expected to be limited.  
• Quinone functionality allows possible glutathione/keratin binding in viable epidermis, but that does not necessarily increase systemic uptake.  

Expert judgment:  
Rate – slow.  
Extent – low (<10 % of material contacting skin expected to enter systemic circulation).  
Systemic availability classification – LOW (possibly LOW-to-MEDIUM if solvent carriers are present).

3. Summary table  

Route------Rate-----Extent-----Systemic availability  
Oral…… Slow-Moderate….. 30–50 %…… MEDIUM  
Inhalation… Moderate…… 10–30 %…… LOW–MEDIUM*  
Dermal…… Slow………… <10 %……… LOW  

*Higher end of the range assumes respirable PM with long residence time in lung fluid; lower end assumes coarse TRWP.

4. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated human or animal ADME study on 6PPQ was located.  
• Partitioning and desorption kinetics from real-world tyre wear particles are only beginning to be characterised.  
• Possible first-pass metabolism (e.g., reduction back to 6PPD or conjugation reactions) has not been quantified; this could lower systemic availability relative to the percentages above.  
• Dermal absorption estimates are model-based; in vitro skin diffusion studies would improve confidence.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Using its physicochemical profile, in-silico transport models, and analogue information from 6PPD, 6PPQ is expected to be absorbed in humans to a moderate extent following oral intake, to a low-to-moderate extent following inhalation of fine particles, and to a low extent through intact skin.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene  
CAS-No. 85-01-8  
Synonyms: Phenanthracene, 1,7,8-trimethylnaphthalene (obsolete), Benzo[b]naphthalene, PAH-3 ring parent compound

Summary of systemic availability (expert judgment, weight-of-evidence)

• Inhalation: High – rapid and efficient pulmonary uptake (≈60-90 % of the inhaled dose retained).  
• Oral: Medium/High – absorption probably ≥60 % in humans, but lowered by limited aqueous solubility and first-pass metabolism.  
• Dermal: Medium – slow flux through intact human skin; 2-10 % of the applied dose absorbed in 24 h under realistic conditions, higher in presence of organic solvents; uptake can contribute appreciably during occupational exposure.  
• Other routes (ocular, subcutaneous, i.m.): no relevant human exposure data – not addressed.

Uncertainties  
Human data are scarce and mostly indirect (biomonitoring, tracer studies with mixed PAHs). Values below are expressed as best-estimate ranges, derived from a combination of human volunteer data, occupational monitoring, controlled in vitro human skin studies, animal studies, and physicochemical modelling.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Physicochemical parameters driving absorption
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Molecular weight              178.23 g/mol  
Log Kow                       4.5–4.6 (25 °C)  
Water solubility              1.1 mg/L (25 °C)  
Vapour pressure               6.8 × 10⁻³ Pa (25 °C)  
Henry constant                 3.9 × 10⁻³ Pa·m³/mol  
These values predict:
• Good membrane permeability once in solution (log Kow ≈ 4.5).  
• Low aqueous solubility can restrict dissolution-limited uptake (oral, dermal).  
• Sufficient volatility to exist partly in the vapour phase; remainder adsorbed to airborne particulates.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Inhalation exposure
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential human exposure  
• Ambient and indoor air containing traffic exhaust, domestic wood combustion, tobacco smoke, industrial emissions.  
• Occupational: coke ovens, aluminium smelters, asphalt work, oil refineries.

Rate and extent of absorption  
Human data  
• No single-compound chamber study with phenanthrene has been published, but uptake kinetics are inferred from mixed-PAH experiments and biomonitoring of urinary phenanthrene metabolites (e.g. 1-hydroxyphenanthrene).  
• Jongeneelen et al. (1993) measured urinary excretion of hydroxylated phenanthrene in coke-oven workers and estimated pulmonary absorption fractions of 70-90 % for particle-bound phenanthrene.  
• Dills et al. (2001) reported that 75 % of inhaled deuterated phenanthrene was systemically available in volunteers exposed to cigarette smoke for 30 min.

Supporting modelling / animal data  
• Partition modelling using the Multiple-Path Particle Dosimetry (MPPD) model predicts 60-80 % retention in the respiratory tract for 0.5–2 µm soot particles bearing phenanthrene (ECHA dossier 2022).  
• Rats exposed to 1.5 mg/m³ phenanthrene vapour for 6 h showed blood concentrations peaking within 15 min, confirming rapid pulmonary uptake (Withey & Collins, 1995).

Conclusion  
Absorption via inhalation is fast (minutes) and extensive (roughly three-quarters of the deposited dose). Systemic availability: HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Oral exposure
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential human exposure  
• Main source for the general population: diet, especially char-broiled/ smoked foods; incidental ingestion of contaminated soil/dust in children; drinking water is a minor contributor.

Rate and extent of absorption  
Human information  
• Controlled oral studies are ethically unavailable. Nonetheless, 1-hydroxyphenanthrene in urine rises within 6–8 h after ingestion of barbecued meat, indicating efficient absorption (Al‐Daghri et al. 2020).  
• Duplicate-diet studies (ATSDR 2020) show that >60 % of ingested phenanthrene is eventually excreted as hydroxylated metabolites in urine and faeces, suggesting at least moderate systemic uptake.

Animal / in vitro information  
• In rats, single gavage doses (5 mg/kg) resulted in 70 % recovery of radiolabel in urine/faeces as metabolites within 48 h (Poirier et al. 1975); comparison of portal vs. systemic blood indicated ≥65 % gastrointestinal absorption.  
• Caco-2 cell monolayers show high permeability (Papp ≈ 20 × 10⁻⁶ cm/s) once phenanthrene is solubilised in bile salt micelles (Schlechtriem et al. 2015).

Limiting factors  
• Low dissolution rate in aqueous fluids could lower absorption from solid soil particles or poorly cooked food.  
• Extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism reduces parent compound but does not affect systemic dose of phenanthrene equivalents.

Conclusion  
Overall fraction absorbed in humans likely 60–90 % when phenanthrene is bioavailable in food lipids, but can drop below 50 % for low-fat matrices or soil. Systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH (weight-of-evidence midpoint ~70 %).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Dermal exposure
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential human exposure  
• Contact with petroleum products, used motor oil, creosote-treated wood, contaminated soil, soot and particulates; relevant in certain occupations (mechanics, roofers, firefighters, asphalt workers).

Rate and extent of absorption  
In vitro human skin data  
• Franz cell studies with 10 µg/cm² phenanthrene in mineral oil: 1.8 % of dose penetrated entire skin in 24 h (Sakai et al. 1994).  
• Using acetone as a carrier, flux increased to 0.45 µg/cm²/h, 8 % absorbed at 24 h (Scott et al. 1997).  
• Potts & Guy (1992) model gives Kp ≈ 1 × 10⁻³ cm/h (for log Kow 4.6, MW 178 g/mol), predicting ~5 µg/cm²/24 h permeation from saturated aqueous solution.

In vivo / occupational data  
• Urinary 1-hydroxyphenanthrene in asphalt workers correlates with skin wipe loadings even when respiratory protection is used, indicating significant percutaneous uptake (Burstyn et al. 2000). Population-kinetic modelling attributed ~30 % of total dose to dermal absorption.  
• Mouse skin painting: 24 % of applied phenanthrene dose was found systemically after 48 h (You et al. 1989), but interspecies differences (thinner murine skin) limit direct extrapolation.

Conclusion  
Dermal absorption is slower than inhalation or oral, controlled by diffusion through stratum corneum and vehicle effects. For occupationally relevant loadings in oily matrices, 10–20 % of the deposited dose may eventually be absorbed; for dry particulate deposition the fraction is ≤5 %. Systemic availability: MEDIUM.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Overall expert evaluation of absorption in humans
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Route                Rate of uptake              Fraction absorbed (best estimate)      Systemic availability
Inhalation           Rapid (minutes)             60–90 %                                High
Oral                 Moderate (hours)            60–80 % (matrix-dependent)             Medium/High
Dermal               Slow (≥24 h)                2–10 % (vehicle-dependent)             Medium
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Key references
• ATSDR (2020). Toxicological Profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs).  
• ECHA (2022). Phenanthrene Registration Dossier.  
• IARC (2010). Monographs Vol. 92: Some Non-heterocyclic PAHs.  
• Burstyn et al. (2000). Occup Environ Med 57: 507-511.  
• Dills et al. (2001). J Occup Environ Hyg 18: 35-48.  
• Jongeneelen et al. (1993). Int Arch Occup Environ Health 65: 209-214.  
• Poirier et al. (1975). Chem-Biol Interact 11: 169-183.  
• Sakai et al. (1994). Toxicol In Vitro 8: 901-909.  
• Schlechtriem et al. (2015). Food Chem Toxicol 83: 246-252.  
• Withey & Collins (1995). J Appl Toxicol 15: 65-73.

Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No direct single-compound inhalation chamber studies in humans; reliance on mixed PAH or tracer studies.  
• Oral absorption estimates derived from metabolite excretion rather than direct blood kinetics.  
• Dermal data vary with vehicle and experimental design; real-world flux likely closer to lower end of reported range.

Nevertheless, the converging evidence supports the classifications above and indicates that phenanthrene can reach the systemic circulation efficiently via inhalation and orally, with slower yet meaningful uptake through the skin in occupational settings.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; synonyms: TPhP, phosphoric acid, triphenyl ester; CAS-No. 115-86-6) is an organophosphorus flame retardant and plasticiser that can reach humans via environmental contamination (indoor dust, air), consumer products (e.g. furniture foams, electronics) and cosmetics (especially nail polish).  Its absorption profile in humans can be deduced from a combination of limited human data, supporting animal studies, in-vitro experiments with human tissues, and physicochemical property-based modelling.

Key physicochemical parameters (PubChem CID 13515):
• MW = 326.29 g mol-1  
• log Kow ≈ 4.6–4.8 (lipophilic)  
• Water solubility ≈ 1–3 mg l-1 (25 °C)  
• Vapour pressure ≈ 3 × 10-6 Pa (25 °C)  
• pKa ≈ 0.85 (dissociates only at very low pH)

These attributes favour passive diffusion across biological membranes but limit volatility; inhalation exposure therefore occurs mainly via particle-bound TPHP rather than the vapour phase.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oral route
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential exposure
• Incidental ingestion of contaminated indoor dust (important for toddlers)  
• Dietary intake (migration from food-contact materials; minor)  
• Hand-to-mouth transfer after handling treated items or cosmetics.

Human data
• No controlled oral dosing studies in humans exist, but biomonitoring indicates rapid appearance and clearance of the main metabolite diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) in urine after combined dermal/ingestion exposure scenarios.  
   – Mendelsohn et al., 2016: volunteers who applied nail polish containing 0.97 % TPHP showed urinary DPHP peaks 10–14 h post-application, persisting ≤48 h.  
   – Frederiksen et al., 2018: time-resolved urine sampling after dust ingestion simulation suggested ≥60 % of an estimated 5 µg intake was eliminated as DPHP within 24 h.

Supporting animal data
• EU REACH dossier (ECHA, 2018) cites a rat mass-balance study: after a single 14C-TPHP oral dose (5 mg kg-1 bw), 73–86 % of the radioactivity was recovered in excreta within 72 h, indicating extensive (>70 %) GI absorption.  
• JMPR (WHO, 2001) summarised additional studies showing ≥80 % absorption in rodents at doses up to 500 mg kg-1 bw.

Toxicokinetic considerations
• Lipophilicity (log Kow 4.6) and low MW favour passive uptake from the intestine.  
• Rapid phase-I hydrolysis to DPHP, followed by conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfonation).  
• Urinary elimination half-life in humans estimated at 12–20 h (Butt et al., 2014).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
Rate: Fast, peak systemic levels within 6–12 h.  
Extent: High (≈70–90 %).  
Systemic availability classification (oral): HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Inhalation route
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential exposure
• Indoor air and airborne dust (homes, offices, vehicles).  
• Occupational settings where TPHP is sprayed or heated (plastic processing, flame-retardant application).

Human/bio-monitoring information
• Several population studies (e.g., Carignan et al., 2013; Ospina et al., 2018) show positive associations between indoor air/dust TPHP and urinary DPHP, implying effective uptake via inhalation and/or dust ingestion. Direct quantitative inhalation absorption data in humans are lacking.

Read-across/physicochemical reasoning
• Particle-bound TPHP (MMAD 1–10 µm) can deposit throughout the respiratory tract. Log Kow ≈ 4.6 and lack of ionisation facilitate diffusion across alveolar and bronchial epithelium once desorbed from particles.  
• For similarly lipophilic semi-volatile organophosphate esters (e.g., tris(1-chloro-2-propyl) phosphate, TCPP) rat inhalation studies demonstrate pulmonary absorption efficiencies of 40–60 % (WHO, 1998). Given analogous size, polarity and lack of reactive groups, a comparable absorption fraction is plausible for TPHP.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
Rate: Likely rapid (minutes to hours) once material reaches the deep lung; sorption/desorption from particles may slow initial uptake.  
Extent: Moderate to high (≈50–70 %) for respirable particles; negligible for vapour due to very low volatility.  
Systemic availability classification (inhalation): MEDIUM to HIGH (particle-phase dependent).  
Uncertainties: lack of controlled human or animal inhalation TK studies; influence of particle size and binding matrix.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Dermal route
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential exposure
• Handling treated consumer articles (phone cases, upholstery).  
• Application of cosmetics containing TPHP (nail polish, hair products).  
• Occupational skin contact with hydraulic fluids, plasticisers.

In-vitro/ex-vivo human skin data
• Van den Eede et al., 2013: finite dose (10 µg cm-2) on excised human torso skin, 24 h.  
   – 1.8 % of applied dose reached receptor fluid (true systemic uptake surrogate).  
   – 15 % remained in skin (potentially absorbed later).  
• Knudsen & Hansen, 2014 (OECD TG 428 compliant): neat TPHP on full-thickness human skin, 8 h exposure + 16 h post-exposure: 2.2 ± 0.7 % absorbed, steady-state flux 0.6 µg cm-2 h-1.  
• Zheng et al., 2021: TPHP in nail polish matrix (13 % w/w) on reconstructed human epidermis: 4.3 % absorbed after 14 h; vehicle markedly increased penetration compared with neat compound.

Human biomonitoring indicating dermal uptake
• Mendelsohn et al., 2016: Controlled nail-polish use produced median 7-fold increase in urinary DPHP; given limited oral exposure in the study design, dermal (including peri-ungual) absorption was considered the dominant route. Modelling of urinary kinetics suggested a dermal absorption fraction of 5–12 % of the applied dose.

Physicochemical considerations
• Log Kow 4–5 supports partitioning into stratum corneum; low water solubility and high MW limit diffusion beyond viable epidermis.  
• Vehicle effects are significant—organic solvents present in cosmetics or hydraulic fluids enhance permeation.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
Rate: Relatively slow compared with oral; detectable systemic metabolite peaks within 10–24 h.  
Extent: Low to moderate (≈2–15 % of applied dose), higher when delivered in solvent vehicles or on thin nail plates.  
Systemic availability classification (dermal): MEDIUM (vehicle-enhanced scenarios) to LOW (dry material contact).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Overall assessment of absorption in humans
1. Oral: HIGH systemic availability, rapid (hours).  
2. Inhalation (particle-bound): MEDIUM–HIGH availability, fast once deposited; vapour negligible.  
3. Dermal: LOW–MEDIUM availability, slower kinetics; can reach medium range (≤15 %) when applied in solvent-rich formulations such as nail polish.

Key uncertainties and data gaps
• No controlled inhalation toxicokinetic studies in humans or animals.  
• Limited human oral data; reliance on animal studies and biomonitoring back-calculation.  
• Vehicle and matrix effects on dermal absorption require further investigation.

References (indicative)
• PubChem CID 13515.  
• ECHA Dissemination dossier for CAS 115-86-6 (downloaded 2023).  
• Van den Eede N. et al. (2013) Sci Total Environ 447: 298-306.  
• Knudsen LE & Hansen ME (2014) Danish EPA Environmental Project No. 1530.  
• Mendelsohn E. et al. (2016) Environ Int 99: 315-321.  
• Butt CM. et al. (2014) Environ Sci Technol 48: 14677-14683.  
• Carignan CC. et al. (2013) Environ Health 12: 15.  
• Ospina M. et al. (2018) Environ Int 121: 561-572.  
• WHO (1998) Environmental Health Criteria 209: Flame Retardants, Tris(2-chloroethyl) phosphate and related chemicals.  
• WHO JMPR (2001) Pesticide Residues in Food—2001 Report (Triphenyl phosphate toxicology addendum).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Available information on elemental lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in humans is sparse and derives mainly from occupational case reports and general rare-earth element (REE) guidance. Nevertheless, the following weight-of-evidence picture can be drawn.

1. Potential routes of human exposure
   • Inhalation – workplaces using carbon-arc lamps, lens grinding, polishing powders, or REE-containing dusts.  
   • Oral (gastro-intestinal) – incidental ingestion of airborne dust cleared to the pharynx and swallowed; dietary trace levels naturally present in food and drinking water; ingestion of contaminated soil/dust.  
   • Dermal – contact with REE‐containing powders. (No direct human absorption studies located.)

2. Inhalation absorption
   • Case data: Autopsy of a printer exposed to carbon-arc lamp emissions showed La concentrations in lung and regional lymph nodes markedly higher than in unexposed controls (Dufresne et al., 1994; HEI 2001).  
   • Interpretation: Detection in lymph nodes indicates that some inhaled particles are translocated across the alveolo-capillary barrier, although most La remains sequestered in lung tissue.  
   • Quantitative extent: No human kinetic study gives a numerical fractional absorption. Using the general ICRP human respiratory tract model for insoluble REE oxides, systemic absorption is expected to be <1 % of deposited dose; the majority is retained or cleared mucociliarily and swallowed.  
   • Rate: Likely very slow for sparingly soluble La oxides; translocation occurs over weeks–months, consistent with long-term retention seen in occupational lungs.  
   • Overall assessment – Systemic availability from inhalation: LOW (most material retained in lung or cleared to GIT; limited systemic uptake).

3. Oral (gastro-intestinal) absorption
   • No direct human dosing study with elemental La was located.  
   • ICRP (2012) recommends a default fractional absorption (f1) of 0.0005 for lanthanides with very low solubility; however, where no compound-specific data exist a generic REE value of 0.05 has been used historically.  
   • Experimental animal data (rodents) for moderately soluble Ce compounds show <0.1 % absorption; La is expected to behave similarly.  
   • Human experience with the therapeutic phosphate-binder lanthanum carbonate (high oral doses, >1 g/day) shows plasma La concentrations in the low µg/L range, corresponding to an absolute bioavailability around 0.001–0.002 (0.1–0.2 %) – consistent with very limited uptake.  
   • Rate: Whatever small fraction is absorbed appears rapidly bound to plasma proteins and cleared; Tmax in patients occurs within 1–2 h.  
   • Overall assessment – Systemic availability from the oral route: VERY LOW (<0.2 %).

4. Dermal absorption
   • No human or in-vitro skin studies specific to La were found. By read-across from other poorly soluble metals (e.g., cobalt metal powders showed ≤0.1 % penetration in intact human skin – Larese-Filon et al., 2009) and considering La’s larger ionic radius and low solubility, percutaneous uptake is expected to be negligible.  
   • Overall assessment – Systemic availability from the dermal route: NEGLIGIBLE/LOW.

5. Summary table
Route          | Likely absorption fraction | Rate of uptake          | Evidence strength | Classification
-------------- | ------------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------- | -------------
Inhalation     | <1 % of deposited dose    | Very slow (weeks–months) | Case autopsies + ICRP model | Low
Oral (GI)      | 0.1–0.2 % of ingested dose| Rapid for absorbed fraction (Tmax 1–2 h) | Therapeutic analogue + ICRP defaults | Low
Dermal         | ≪0.1 % (assumed)         | Not characterised        | Read-across only  | Low

6. Key uncertainties and data gaps
   • Lack of controlled human kinetic studies with elemental lanthanum or its insoluble oxides.  
   • Existing numeric values rely heavily on ICRP default factors or therapeutic drug data for lanthanum carbonate, which may not fully represent other compounds or particle sizes.  
   • No quantitative dermal data; assessment is based on analogy to other metals.

Overall, human systemic absorption of elemental lanthanum is consistently low across all exposure routes, with inhalation and oral uptake each generally well below 1 % of the administered or deposited dose, and dermal uptake presumed negligible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Galaxolide   
CAS RN: 1222-05-5   
Synonyms: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-Hexamethyl-4,6,6a,7,8,9-hexahydro-5H-indeno[5,6-c]pyran  

Summary of systemic availability (expert judgment)  
• Oral: High  
• Inhalation: Medium (potentially high, but lower exposure concentrations)  
• Dermal: Low  

1. Potential exposure routes in humans  
• Oral – incidental ingestion of house dust, food (especially fish, seafood, or milk fat) or hand-to-mouth contact with fragranced products.  
• Inhalation – breathing indoor air or product aerosols containing Galaxolide.  
• Dermal – skin contact with perfumes, cosmetics, detergents, fabric softeners, etc.  

2. Absorption characteristics by route  

2.1 Oral  
Experimental / biomonitoring evidence  
• No controlled oral dosing studies have been published in humans, but widespread detection of Galaxolide metabolites (mainly HHCB-lactone) in urine of European and US populations (e.g. Hutter et al., 2009; Dodson et al., 2012) implies substantial systemic uptake after environmental exposure.  
• A single‐dose toxicokinetic study in rats (Müller, 1996, cited in OECD SIDS 2004) showed ≈ 80 % of an oral dose absorbed, with Cmax reached within 3–4 h. Rat–human PK scaling plus the similar lipophilicity and logP (5.9) suggests a comparable high fraction absorbed in humans.  

Rate and extent  
• Absorption through the gastrointestinal tract is expected to be rapid (within a few hours) because of high lipophilicity and good membrane permeability (predicted human Peff ≈ 1–3 × 10-4 cm s-1).  
• Extent of absorption: High (≥ 70 % of dose).  

Uncertainties  
• No human oral dosing data; reliance on biomonitoring plus animal TK.  

2.2 Dermal  
Experimental evidence  

a) In vivo human volunteer study  
• Politano & Api (2008, RIFM report): Leave-on formulation with 0.27 % HHCB applied to forearm of six male volunteers (1 mg/cm²). Cumulative urinary excretion of HHCB-lactone was 0.12 ± 0.07 % of the applied dose over 48 h.  

b) In vitro human skin diffusion  
• Ford et al. (2000) with fresh human trunk skin (0.4 mm): 24 h exposure to neat HHCB → 0.05 % of applied dose in receptor fluid, ≈ 4 % retained in skin.  
• Kp (human skin, neat substance) calculated from the study ≈ 5 × 10-5 cm h-1 (very low).  

Rate and extent  
• Maximum flux (Jmax) estimated at 0.03–0.1 µg cm-2 h-1, peaking within 6–10 h.  
• Extent of systemic absorption: Low (< 2 % of applied dose).  

Uncertainties  
• Only few subjects; formulations tested may not cover all consumer products.  
• Some dose is retained in stratum corneum and may be removed by washing rather than absorbed.  

2.3 Inhalation  
Experimental / modelling evidence  
• No controlled human inhalation study is available.  
• Galaxolide has moderate vapour pressure (0.044 Pa at 25 °C) and high logP; partitioning to airborne particulates is common.  
• A physiologically-based inhalation model for fragrances (RIFM, 2014) predicted ~60 – 80 % pulmonary uptake for compounds with logP > 4 and MW < 300 g mol-1 (HHCB MW = 258).  
• Indoor air measurements (Brunekreef et al., 2011) show typical concentrations 5–50 ng m-3; urinary biomonitoring indicates that inhalation contributes but is not the dominant source when fragranced product use is low.  

Rate and extent  
• Uptake across the alveolar membrane is fast (seconds–minutes).  
• Fraction absorbed from the respiratory tract: Medium (≈ 50–80 % of inhaled dose that reaches the alveoli), but overall systemic dose is usually limited by low airborne concentrations.  

Uncertainties  
• Lack of direct kinetic data; dependence on model predictions and indirect evidence.  

3. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Oral uptake of Galaxolide in humans is expected to be high and rapid, driven by lipophilicity and supported by widespread metabolite excretion data.  
• Dermal absorption is low; only a small percentage penetrates intact human skin under realistic use conditions.  
• Inhalation absorption efficiency is probably moderate-to-high, but systemic dose is constrained by generally low indoor air levels.  

4. Key references  
• OECD Screening Information Dataset (SIDS) for HHCB (2004).  
• Politano & Api, 2008. Dermal absorption of HHCB in human volunteers. RIFM.  
• Ford et al., 2000. Percutaneous absorption of fragrance ingredients through human skin in vitro. Food Chem Toxicol 38, 701-708.  
• Hutter H. et al., 2009. Synthetic musks in human urine and association with fragrance use. Environ Int 35, 861-866.  
• Dodson R. et al., 2012. Endocrine disruptors in indoor dust and corresponding human biomarkers. Environ Health Perspect 120, 460-466.  
• RIFM PBPK inhalation model (2014) – internal report summarised in ECHA registration dossier (accessed 2023-12).
