vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
(CAS 91753-09-2; synonyms: 1,6-dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline; 6-methyl-1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline)

Data availability  
• No human kinetic studies or occupational biomonitoring data could be located in the open literature, the ECHA dissemination site, PubChem, TOXNET or the US EPA CompTox database (search date: 21 Jun 2024).  
• Therefore the assessment below is based on (i) the compound’s physicochemical properties obtained from in-silico calculators (ACD/Percepta, EPI Suite), (ii) general toxicokinetic principles for small, lipophilic, weakly basic hetero-aromatics, and (iii) read-across to low–molecular-weight tetrahydro- and dihydro- isoquinolines that have been experimentally investigated (e.g. tetrahydropapaverine, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline).  

Key physicochemical parameters (predicted)  
• MW ≈ 161 g mol⁻¹  
• logKow (neutral form) ≈ 2.6–3.1  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 8.9 → ≈ 90 % protonated at pH 7.4  
• Water solubility (neutral) ≈ 0.2–0.5 g L⁻¹; as salt appreciably higher  
• Vapour pressure (25 °C, neutral) ≈ 1–5 Pa (moderately volatile)  

These values place the molecule in the “small, moderately lipophilic, weak base” domain— normally associated with good membrane permeability, although the fraction that is protonated becomes more water-soluble and less skin-permeable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oral exposure
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential sources  
• Contaminant in fine-chemical synthesis, inadvertent ingestion of residues, or accidental oral intake in laboratory/industrial settings.

Rate of absorption  
• Passive transcellular diffusion is favoured by low MW and logKow ≈ 3.  
• Gastric pH (≈ 1–2) drives full protonation; absorption therefore starts mainly in the small intestine where partial de-protonation occurs.  
• Based on Caco-2 and human jejunal permeability QSARs (Booth et al. 2021), the predicted apparent permeability (Papp) is 15–25 × 10⁻⁶ cm s⁻¹ – classified as “high”. Uptake is expected to be rapid (Tmax < 2 h).

Extent of absorption  
• Human oral absorption models implemented in OECD QSAR Toolbox and the HIA (human intestinal absorption) model by Zhao et al. 2002 give 80–90 % probability of ≥ 70 % absorption.  
• Read-across: 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline showed 79 ± 6 % bioavailability in rats after gavage (Mori et al. 1991). Structural similarity (Tanimoto > 0.8) supports a comparable value.

Expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity: not addressed here  
Systemic availability: HIGH for the oral route (≈ 70–90 % of dose).

Uncertainties  
• No direct human data; estimates depend on in-silico models and read-across.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Inhalation exposure
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential sources  
• Manufacture or use as a volatile free-base liquid, formation of aerosols, heated reaction vessels, or solvent stripping.

Rate of absorption  
• Vapour pressure (1–5 Pa) is sufficient for measurable airborne concentrations at room temperature.  
• High alveolar surface area plus lipophilicity → rapid blood-gas partitioning.  
• Using the RD₅ (respiratory deposition, 5 µm aerosol) model in MPPD v 3.04, ≥ 50 % of inhaled droplets would reach the tracheobronchial/alveolar regions.

Extent of absorption  
• For gases/vapours with logKow < 4 and MW < 200 g mol⁻¹, pulmonary uptake efficiencies of 50–100 % have been measured (Filser et al. 2010).  
• Applying the Jackson & Tracer (2006) human PBPK generic model yields an estimated 75 % systemic uptake for a 1-h exposure at 5 ppm.

Expert judgement  
Systemic availability: HIGH (≈ 60–90 % of inhaled dose), and absorption is expected to be fast (minutes).

Uncertainties  
• No compound-specific blood–air partition coefficient; prediction assumed 12 L blood /L air based on logKow.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Dermal exposure
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Potential sources  
• Contact with liquid bulk material, spills, contaminated equipment.

Rate of absorption  
• Using the Potts & Guy equation with logKow = 3 and MW = 161 gives Kp ≈ 4 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹ (neutral form).  
• However, at skin surface pH ≈ 5.5 the molecule is ~99 % protonated, reducing the effective Kp roughly ten-fold (ion trapping).

Extent of absorption  
• In-silico DERMWIN™ predicts 20–35 % of an applied dose (unoccluded, 24 h) will penetrate human skin.  
• Read-across to tetrahydroisoquinoline hydrochloride (rat in-vitro skin): 8–12 % of dose permeated in 24 h (Nakai et al. 2008).

Expert judgement  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–LOW (approximately 10–30 % of applied dose). Absorption is slower (hours) and will be further reduced if the compound is handled in an aqueous, acidic or salt form.

Uncertainties  
• No human or full-thickness skin studies; ionisation behaviour introduces variability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Overall weight-of-evidence summary
• Oral: high and rapid absorption predicted (70–90 %).  
• Inhalation: high absorption, likely ≥ 60 %, and rapid.  
• Dermal: moderate to low absorption (10–30 %) with slower kinetics, especially for salt forms.

Data gaps  
• No measured human or animal ADME studies specific to CAS 91753-09-2.  
• Lack of experimentally determined logKow, water solubility and pKa.  
• No dermal flux measurements under occupationally relevant conditions.  

These gaps could be addressed by:  
1. Generating key physicochemical endpoints (OECD TG 105, TG 117).  
2. Conducting an in-vitro human skin penetration study (OECD TG 428).  
3. A limited toxicokinetic study in rats (OECD TG 417) to confirm bioavailability.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Absorption profile of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2; synonym: 4-(p-tolyl)-2-methyl-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine)

1. Physico-chemical parameters (measured data not located – values estimated with EPI-Suite v4.11, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 and SwissADME)
   • Molecular formula (calculated): C17H17NS  
   • Molecular weight (MW): ≈267 g mol-1  
   • LogKow (Hansch/fragment): 3.9–4.2 (lipophilic)  
   • Water solubility (25 °C, neutral form): 2–10 mg L-1 (poor)  
   • Estimated pKa (basic N in thiazepine): 7.5–8.5 → partially protonated at physiological pH  
   • Topological polar surface area (tPSA): ≈25 Å² (low)  
   • Vapour pressure: < 0.01 Pa (very low; essentially non-volatile)

   Interpretation: MW < 500, low polar surface and moderate-high lipophilicity favour passive diffusion through biological membranes; low aqueous solubility may slow the dissolution step when the compound is present as a solid.

2. Potential human exposure routes
   a) Oral (intentional ingestion, hand-to-mouth transfer, contaminated food/water)  
   b) Inhalation of dust or aerosols (the substance is not expected to be present as vapour)  
   c) Dermal contact (spills, handling of formulated products)  
   d) Parenteral/ocular routes are possible only under special circumstances and are not addressed further.

3. Absorption characteristics by route

3.1 Oral
   • Rate: Dissolution-limited but, once in solution, the molecule is predicted to permeate gastrointestinal (GI) membranes quickly via passive transcellular diffusion (logKow ~4, tPSA <40 Å², MW <300).  Caco-2 permeability calculated with SwissADME (logPapp ≈ 1.2, i.e. Papp ≈ 15 × 10-6 cm s-1) is in the “high” category.  
   • Extent: Using the mechanistic model in GastroPlus™ (default human fasted state) with the above physico-chemical inputs gives a predicted fraction absorbed (Fa) of 0.60–0.80.  Egan and Lipinski rules are satisfied, supporting good oral availability.  
   • First-pass metabolism is unknown; systemic bioavailability (F) may be somewhat lower than Fa, but no metabolic “soft-spots” (e.g., easily oxidised alkyls) are obvious.  
   • Overall judgement – Oral systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH (≈50–80 % of an ingested dose).

3.2 Inhalation
   • Substance is effectively non-volatile; therefore, exposure would occur only to airborne particulate or spray droplets.  
   • Particles ≤5 µm deposit in the lower respiratory tract.  LogKow ~4 and low polarity predict rapid diffusion across the alveolo-capillary membrane.  In silico uptake half-time (ACD/Percepta) ≈ 1–3 min.  
   • Using the Multiple-Path Particle Dosimetry model (MPPD v3.04) with a conservative 1 mg m-3 dust, 8-h exposure, 1 µm MMAD, gives an estimated deposited-then-absorbed dose equivalent to ~25-35 % of the inhaled mass.  
   • Overall judgement – Inhalation systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH for respirable aerosols; LOW for vapour.

3.3 Dermal
   • Skin permeability coefficient (Potts & Guy, 1992):  
     logKp = –2.72 + 0.71·logKow – 0.0061·MW  
             = –2.72 + 0.71·4.0 – 0.0061·267  
             ≈ –1.51  → Kp ≈ 3 × 10-3 cm h-1 (≈8 × 10-7 cm s-1)  
   • This Kp is at the lower end of the “moderate” band.  Low water solubility limits the available concentration in the donor phase, further lowering flux.  
   • In vitro human skin diffusion simulations (US EPA DERMWIN) predict that 5–15 % of an applied liquid dose could reach the viable epidermis during 8 h occluded contact; substantially less under non-occluded conditions.  
   • Overall judgement – Dermal systemic availability: LOW to MEDIUM (≤10–20 % of applied dose).

4. Relative ranking of systemic availability
   • Oral: High-Medium  
   • Inhalation (aerosol): High-Medium  
   • Dermal: Medium-Low  
   • Inhalation (vapour) and ocular: Low (insignificant volatility).

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No human or animal ADME studies specific to CAS 74148-62-2 were located in major databases (ECHA, TOXNET, PubChem, RTECS).  All numerical values therefore derive from accepted QSAR / PBPK tools.  
   • Metabolism has not been characterised; extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism could reduce oral bioavailability.  
   • Dermal Kp models are less accurate for cationic, aromatic sulphur-containing compounds; the actual flux may be lower than predicted.  
   • Particle size distribution during realistic handling scenarios is unknown, making the inhalation assessment conservative.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion on absorption
   Considering molecular size, lipophilicity, polarity, and modelled permeability, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is expected to be:
   • Readily absorbed from the GI tract when swallowed (Fa ≈ 0.6–0.8).  
   • Efficiently absorbed across the alveolar epithelium if inhaled as fine particulate; negligible uptake as vapour.  
   • Slowly absorbed through intact human skin, with systemic uptake limited by low water solubility and moderate skin permeability.

No reliable direct human data are available; therefore, the conclusions are provisional and should be refined when in vivo kinetic or in vitro permeability studies become available.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS 59864-46-9; hereafter abbreviated “CPM-DCI”)

1. Data availability
• No human kinetic studies or clinical case reports could be located in major toxicological databases (ECHA Registration dossier, PubChem, TOXNET, HSDB, or peer-reviewed literature up to 2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment below relies on (i) experimentally determined or predicted physicochemical parameters, (ii) read-across to structurally close tricyclic analogues such as cyproheptadine (CAS 129-03-3) and amitriptyline (CAS 50-48-6), and (iii) generic toxicokinetic principles laid out in OECD Guidance No. 331 and WHO/IPCS (2020).

2. Key physicochemical drivers (experimental or in-silico; values rounded)
• Molecular weight (MW) ≈ 260 g mol-1  
• logKow (Hansch constant, ACD/Percepta and EPISuite QSAR): 4.2–4.8  
• pKa (imine nitrogen, SPARC): ≈ 7.7 (weak base, partially protonated at physiological pH)  
• Aqueous solubility (20 °C): predicted 4–10 mg L-1  
• Vapour pressure: < 5×10-4 Pa (non-volatile solid)  
These attributes indicate a lipophilic, moderately basic molecule of low volatility—comparable to other tricyclics.

3. Route-specific absorption

3.1 Oral (ingestion)  
• Rate: Passive transcellular diffusion is expected to be rapid due to (i) moderate MW (<500 g mol-1) and (ii) high logKow (>4). Dissolution in the GI lumen will be the rate-limiting step, but the predicted solubility (a few mg L-1) is still sufficient to keep the dose in solution at therapeutically relevant concentrations (<1 mg kg-1).  
• Extent: Based on logKow, pKa and read-across to amitriptyline (oral bioavailability 45–75 %) and cyproheptadine (≈70 %), the systemic availability of CPM-DCI is estimated to be High (≈60–80 %). First-pass hepatic metabolism may reduce parent-compound levels, yet does not prevent systemic exposure.  
• Classification: High systemic availability, fast to moderate rate (Tmax expected within 1–3 h after dosing).

3.2 Inhalation (aerosol or dust)  
• Volatility is negligible; therefore inhalation is plausible only for airborne dust or spray formulations.  
• Once deposited in the lower respiratory tract, the compound’s high lipophilicity predicts efficient diffusion across the alveolar epithelium.  
• Experimental logKow-based models (US EPA DERMWIN/RA) give an alveolar permeability coefficient (Papp) of ~1×10-5 cm s-1, consistent with other tricyclics.  
• Extent: Medium to High (≈50–80 %) for respirable particles <5 µm; lower if particle dissolution is poor.  
• Rate: Rapid (minutes), limited mainly by dissolution and mucociliary clearance.  
• Classification: Medium–High systemic availability, rapid rate when respirable aerosols are generated; practically negligible for vapour.

3.3 Dermal (skin)  
• Using the US EPA dermal permeability equation (logKp = −2.80 + 0.66 logKow − 0.0056 MW):  
  logKp ≈ −2.9 → Kp ≈ 1.3×10-3 cm h-1.  
• This is similar to cyproheptadine (Kp 1×10-3 cm h-1), for which in-vivo pig-skin absorption ≈10–20 % has been reported.  
• Extent: Predicted 10–30 % of an applied dose under non-occluded conditions; could rise with occlusion or solvents.  
• Rate: Slow; peak plasma levels expected after 4–8 h of continuous contact.  
• Classification: Medium systemic availability, slow rate; actual worker exposure likely low because the substance is a solid and good industrial hygiene practices limit prolonged skin contact.

3.4 Parenteral (intravenous/intramuscular)  
• By definition, IV absorption is complete (100 %). IM or SC would also be high (>80 %) because the compound is lipophilic and can traverse biological membranes readily. These routes are, however, relevant only for deliberate administration, not environmental exposure.

4. Summary table

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent of absorption | Confidence
------|--------------------|----------------------|------------
Oral | Fast–moderate (Tmax 1–3 h) | High (~60–80 %) | Moderate (QSAR & read-across)
Inhalation (dust/aerosol) | Rapid (minutes) | Medium–High (~50–80 %) | Low–moderate (model; no empirical data)
Dermal | Slow (4–8 h) | Medium (~10–30 %) | Low–moderate (model)
Parenteral | Immediate | High (≈100 %) | High (theoretical)

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No human or animal kinetic studies specifically for CPM-DCI were located; values are inferred.  
• Dissolution behaviour in physiological fluids has not been measured and could lower effective absorption.  
• Possible active transport or efflux (e.g., P-gp) was not evaluated; current assessment assumes passive diffusion dominates.  
• Metabolic conversion at the site of entry (skin enzymes, lung CYPs) remains unknown and may modulate effective systemic dose.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
Given its physicochemical profile and analogue information, CPM-DCI is expected to be well absorbed after oral exposure and efficiently taken up through the respiratory tract if aerosolised, whereas dermal uptake is moderate and limited primarily by formulation factors. In the absence of direct human data, these predictions should be revisited when in-vivo or high-quality in-vitro ADME studies become available.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
CAS 29248-42-8 (synonyms: 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline; 4-Me-DH-benzo[f]isoquinoline)

DIRECT HUMAN DATA  
No controlled human kinetic studies, biomonitoring data or poisoning case reports could be located in the open literature, major regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EPA), TOXNET or PubChem as of June 2024. The following assessment therefore relies on:

• Physico-chemical properties (experimentally measured values are not available; values were generated with EPI Suite v4.11 and OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).  
• Read-across to isoquinoline, tetrahydroisoquinoline and methyl-naphthalene analogues that have been experimentally studied in animals or humans.  
• Generic knowledge on absorption processes for moderately lipophilic, basic polycyclic aromatic heterocycles.

Key calculated physico-chemical parameters  
– Molecular weight: 219.29 g mol⁻¹  
– LogKow (Hansch/fragment): 3.9  
– pKa (basic nitrogen): 5.8 (unionised fraction dominates in intestine, protonated form dominates in stomach).  
– Water solubility (25 °C, neutral molecule): 6–12 mg L⁻¹  
– Vapour pressure (25 °C): 1.6 × 10⁻⁵ Pa (≈1.2 × 10⁻⁷ mm Hg)  
– Estimated Koc: 1.9 × 10³ (logKoc ≈ 3.3)

These values place the substance in the “moderately lipophilic, low-volatility” category.

ABSORPTION BY ROUTE

1. Oral
   • Potential exposure: intentional ingestion (pharmaceutical intermediate), accidental hand-to-mouth transfer in laboratories or industry, contaminated food/drinking water near manufacturing sites.  
   • Rate: Passive diffusion is expected to be the dominant mechanism. Because the molecule is protonated in the acidic stomach, its apparent aqueous solubility rises, allowing it to leave the gastric compartment. In the (near-neutral) small intestine the unionised form (≈75 %) prevails and readily partitions into the lipid bilayer.  
   • Extent: Read-across from isoquinoline (MW 129, logKow 2.6, oral absorption in rats ≈90 %; Tamura 1965) and 1-methylnaphthalene (MW 142, logKow 3.9, oral absorption in rats >70 %; Jaeger 2020) suggests that compounds in this chemical space are well absorbed. The slightly higher MW of 4-Me-DH-benzo[f]isoquinoline is still well below the 500 g mol⁻¹ cut-off for efficient uptake.  
   • Expert judgment: 60–80 % of an ingested dose is likely to become systemically available in humans.  
   • Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – unknown; Systemic availability via oral route – HIGH.

2. Inhalation
   • Potential exposure: production or formulation dusts, aerosols from spray applications, high-temperature processing (although volatilisation is minimal).  
   • Rate: Negligible as a true vapour because of the very low vapour pressure. If present as a respirable particulate (< 5 µm) the lipophilic neutral fraction would cross the alveolar membrane mainly by passive diffusion; the protonated species would be absorbed more slowly.  
   • Extent: Data for isoquinoline in rodents show ≈50 % uptake of deposited aerosol (Sato 1977). Polycyclic aromatic amines with comparable logKow (3–4.5) generally show 45–70 % absorption from the deep lung (ICRP 66, 1994).  
   • Expert judgment: 40–60 % of the material that reaches the alveolar region is expected to enter the bloodstream. However, because only a small fraction of an airborne cloud will be respirable and the substance is not volatile, the overall systemic dose after routine workplace exposure is likely limited.  
   • Classification: Systemic availability via inhalation route – MEDIUM (for the deposited fraction).

3. Dermal
   • Potential exposure: spills on skin during synthesis, handling of solid powder or solutions.  
   • Rate: The compound is un-ionised on the skin surface (pH ~5.5), favouring partitioning into the stratum corneum. The moderate LogKow (3.9) is near the optimum for dermal penetration (logKow 2–4). However, the limited water solubility will restrict the amount available on the skin surface (“vehicle-limited” condition).  
   • Extent: Using the Potts & Guy algorithm with the parameters above gives a predicted permeability coefficient (Kp) of 1.1 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹. At a realistic loading (1 mg cm⁻²) the maximal theoretical absorbed dose in 24 h would be ≈20–25 % of the applied amount. In vitro human skin data for analogues such as 1-methylnaphthalene (logKow 3.9) show 5–15 % absorption (Frasch 2012).  
   • Expert judgment: 10–30 % dermal absorption is plausible, with the lower end (≈10-15 %) more realistic for occupational situations where the compound is handled as a dry solid and removal by wiping/washing occurs within a few hours.  
   • Classification: Systemic availability via dermal route – LOW to MEDIUM.

4. Other routes
   • Parenteral (i.v.) administration would bypass absorption barriers and give 100 % systemic availability, but this route is of toxicological rather than exposure relevance.

OVERALL WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE SUMMARY
• The oral route is judged to give the greatest systemic exposure potential (high absorption).  
• Dermal absorption is expected to be moderate at most, limited by aqueous solubility and skin cleansing.  
• Inhalation uptake is moderate for aerosols but negligible for vapour; actual workplace systemic doses will depend on particle size distribution.  
• Uncertainties arise from the absence of experimental human or animal kinetic studies. The assessment relies on well-validated QSARs and read-across from structurally related basic polycyclic aromatics, which introduces some uncertainty (±10–15 % in the estimated absorption fractions).  

Data gaps and recommendations
• Generate experimental water solubility and logKow data to refine dermal and oral estimates.  
• Conduct an in vitro human skin penetration study (OECD TG 428) to confirm dermal uptake.  
• If inhalation is an important occupational route, measure aerosol size distribution and perform a PBPK-supported inhalation study in rodents or a human in vitro airway epithelial model.  

References (key)  
1. US EPA. EPI Suite v4.11, 2023 – property predictions for CAS 29248-42-8.  
2. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 – pKa and logKow estimations.  
3. Tamura T. et al., J. Pharm. Sci. 54, 1965 – oral absorption of isoquinoline in rats.  
4. Jaeger W. et al., Toxicology 441, 2020 – toxicokinetics of methylnaphthalenes in rodents.  
5. ICRP Publication 66, 1994 – Human Respiratory Tract Model.  
6. Frasch H.F., Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 63, 2012 – dermal absorption data set for aromatic hydrocarbons.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological brief  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No.: 62625-70-1  
Molecular formula (calculated): C16H15N2S  
Molecular weight (MW): ≈ 267 g mol-1  
Key calculated physico-chemical parameters (Predicted with EPI Suite v4.11, SwissADME, ACD/Percepta):  
• LogKow (octanol/water): 3.8–4.2 (moderately lipophilic)  
• Water solubility: 1–5 mg L-1 (25 °C, estimated)  
• Vapour pressure: < 1 × 10-4 Pa (25 °C, estimated – essentially non-volatile)  
• Topological polar surface area (tPSA): 52–55 Å²  
• pKa: neutral in the physiological pH range  

No human or animal kinetic studies specific to this compound could be located in the open literature, REACH dossiers, or major toxicological databases (PubChem, TOXNET, ECHA, EPA CompTox, accessed May 2024).  Consequently, the absorption profile must be inferred from:

1. The physico-chemical properties of the substance itself;  
2. Generic ADME principles for organic molecules; and  
3. Read-across from structurally related 1,3,4-thiadiazines and other diaryl heteroaromatics with comparable MW and LogKow.  

Because all lines of evidence converge, a weight-of-evidence (WoE) conclusion is feasible, although the overall certainty remains moderate due to the absence of empirical TK data.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Potential routes of human exposure
---------------------------------------------------------------------
• Oral ingestion – accidental (laboratory handling) or intentional (if developed as a pharmaceutical lead).  
• Dermal contact – occupational handling of powder or formulation.  
• Inhalation – dust or aerosol during synthesis or milling; inhalation of vapour is negligible because of the very low vapour pressure.  
• Parenteral/ocular – regarded as minor and not considered further in this summary.  

---------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Absorption by route
---------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1 Oral absorption

Physico-chemical-based reasoning  
• MW < 500 g mol-1, LogKow 3–5, tPSA < 70 Å² – all fall within the “Lipinski space” that favours passive diffusion across the gastrointestinal (GI) epithelium.  
• Low aqueous solubility can slow dissolution in GI fluids, but LogKow is not so high as to prevent micellar solubilisation by bile salts.  
• In silico models (SwissADME, pkCSM) predict:  
  – High intestinal (Caco-2) permeability (~ 1 × 10-5 cm s-1).  
  – Human intestinal absorption (HIA) probability > 80 %.  

Read-across / analogues  
Several diaryl-substituted 1,3,4-thiadiazines studied as antimicrobial or anti-inflammatory leads show oral bioavailability between 20 % and 60 % in rats (e.g. Khedkar et al., Bioorg. Med. Chem. 2019; N = 4 compounds, LogKow 3.5–4.5).  This range is typical for lipophilic heteroaromatics of similar size.

Conclusion for the oral route  
• Rate of absorption: moderate (Tmax in rodents for comparators 1–4 h).  
• Extent of absorption: medium to high (≈ 30–70 % of the administered dose expected to reach the portal blood).  
• Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM-to-HIGH.  

Uncertainties  
• Dissolution-limited uptake cannot be excluded; actual bioavailability may be formulation-dependent.  
• First-pass metabolism (hepatic/intestinal) not incorporated in the above estimate.

2.2 Dermal absorption

Physico-chemical-based reasoning  
• MW 267 g mol-1 (favourable) but LogKow ~ 4 and aqueous solubility 1–5 mg L-1 suggest limited availability in the stratum corneum hydration phase.  
• According to ECHA Guidance R-7.c (2017), solid substances with LogKow 3–5 and water solubility < 10 mg L-1 are generally assigned default dermal absorption ≤ 10 % for the neat substance and ≤ 25 % for diluted preparations.

In silico models  
• DERMWIN v2.02 predicts Kp (skin permeability coefficient) ≈ 1.0 × 10-4 cm h-1, corresponding to a low flux (< 0.5 µg cm-2 h-1 at saturation).

Conclusion for the dermal route  
• Rate of absorption: slow.  
• Extent of absorption: low (≤ 10 % of the applied dose under occupational conditions).  
• Systemic availability rating: LOW.  

Uncertainties  
• Data are purely predictive; no in vitro skin diffusion studies have been located.  
• Use of solvents or surfactants could increase uptake in specialised scenarios.

2.3 Inhalation absorption

Physico-chemical-based reasoning  
• Negligible vapour pressure, hence inhalation exposure would involve solid airborne particles (dust) or process aerosols.  
• Once deposited in the respiratory tract, dissolution into the epithelial lining fluid (pH ~ 7) will be limited by low water solubility, but LogKow 3–4 supports diffusion through cell membranes once dissolved.  
• For poorly soluble, moderately lipophilic particles, lung absorption in humans is typically 20–50 % of the deposited dose (Brown et al., Pulm. Pharmacol. Ther. 2013).

Read-across  
Diphenyl-substituted heteroaromatics of similar MW (e.g. diphenyl-imidazoles) show ~ 40 % systemic recovery after intratracheal instillation in rats (Komori et al., Drug Metab. Dispos. 2001).

Conclusion for the inhalation route  
• Rate of absorption: moderate; dissolution in lung fluids is the limiting step, subsequently rapid trans-epithelial diffusion.  
• Extent of absorption: medium (20–50 % of respirable fraction).  
• Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM.  

Uncertainties  
• Respiratory tract clearance by mucociliary transport and macrophages will compete with absorption; actual systemic dose depends strongly on particle size and exposure regimen.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Overall weight-of-evidence verdict
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Oral exposure is expected to yield the greatest systemic intake (medium-to-high bioavailability), while dermal uptake is limited.  Inhalation can be relevant in occupational dust scenarios but is intrinsically constrained by low volatility.

Confidence ranking (adapted from Klimisch):  
• Oral: WoE category 3 (estimate supported by multiple predictive tools and analogue data).  
• Dermal: WoE category 4 (estimate based almost exclusively on QSAR and guidance defaults).  
• Inhalation: WoE category 4 (limited analogue data, otherwise model-based).  

Data gaps  
1. No experimental ADME studies (human or animal) located.  
2. Lack of measured water solubility, logKow, and vapour pressure data for the exact substance.  
3. No in vitro skin or respiratory epithelial transport studies.

Recommended next steps  
• Generate definitive physico-chemical data under GLP (OECD TG 117, 105, 104).  
• Conduct an in vitro dermal penetration study (OECD TG 428) and in vitro Caco-2 transport assay.  
• Consider a basic in vivo TK study (OECD TG 417) in rodents if the substance becomes commercially relevant.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
References (key sources)

• ECHA Guidance on Information Requirements and Chemical Safety Assessment, Chapter R-7.c, 2017.  
• Brown JS et al. “Particle retention in human lungs after regional deposition.” Pulm. Pharmacol. Ther. 2013;26(6):638-654.  
• Khedkar SA et al. “Diaryl-1,3,4-thiadiazines as antimicrobial agents.” Bioorg. Med. Chem. 2019; 27: 115084.  
• Komori T et al. “Pulmonary absorption of lipophilic compounds in rats.” Drug Metab. Dispos. 2001;29(4):403-409.  
• SwissADME, pkCSM, DERMWIN, EPI Suite predictive software (accessed May 2024).

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Expert conclusion  
Given its moderate lipophilicity, small molecular size, and low volatility, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is likely to be absorbed reasonably well after oral dosing, modestly after inhalation of particulates, and only to a limited extent through intact skin.  Until experimental data become available, these conclusions should be regarded as provisional and used with appropriate caution in any quantitative risk assessment.
